Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:18543 9 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-03603-6 www.nature.com/scientificreports/ of integrating the Transformer model into single-cell RNA sequencing data analysis. Despite some variation in performance gains, scMSCF achieves significant overall progress in clustering accuracy and stability. Differential expression analysis Differential expression analysis is essential in single-cell RNA sequencing data clustering, as it reveals gene expression differences that help identify and distinguish cell types. This analysis offers critical insights into cellular heterogeneity and provides robust support for accurate cell classification. In this study, we evaluate the marker gene identification performance of scMSCF against Seurat, SHARP, and CellVAGE, all of which have demonstrated robust ARI scores in single-cell RNA sequencing data. Figure 6 illustrates the expression levels and distribution patterns of the top five significantly differentially expressed genes within each cell cluster. As shown, scMSCF more effectively identifies key marker genes linked to cell subtypes. To further substantiate scMSCF’s efficacy in marker gene identification, Fig. 7 presents the average overlap rates between identified and true marker genes for scMSCF, Seurat, SHARP, and CellVAGE across three distinct datasets. Results show that scMSCF consistently achieves a higher overlap rate across all cell groups, indicating superior accuracy and consistency in cell type recognition. This performance highlights scMSCF’s strengths in accurately capturing differentially expressed genes and demonstrates its high stability and precision when handling complex multicellular data, affirming scMSCF’s potential as a powerful tool for analyzing complex single-cell data. Additionally, Supplementary File SF10 provides cluster-specific overlap rates across three datasets for the scMSCF algorithm. Fig. 5. ARI performance comparison between initial clustering and scMSCF clustering enhanced by the basolateral model. Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:18543 10 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-03603-6 www.nature.com/scientificreports/ Fig. 7. Average overlap rate of identified marker genes with true marker genes for scMSCF, Seurat, SHARP, and CellVAGE across mouse skin cells, mouse aorta myeloid, and human bone vascular cells datasets. Fig. 6. Heatmap illustrating differences in marker gene identification among scMSCF, Seurat, SHARP, and CellVAGE on the mouse skin cells dataset. Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:18543 11 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-03603-6 www.nature.com/scientificreports/ To elucidate the dynamic evolution of cell states and further validate the scMSCF algorithm’s capabilities in analyzing complex biological processes, pseudotime trajectory analysis was conducted. This method captures dynamic transitions between cell states and their temporal progression, offering a comprehensive framework to assess the algorithm’s effectiveness in modeling cellular dynamics. Detailed methodologies and results are available in Supplementary Document SN6. To provide deeper biological insight into the marker genes identified by scMSCF, we performed additional functional annotation and pathway enrichment analyses. The results indicate that these marker genes significantly participate in immune-related biological processes and signaling pathways, emphasizing their crucial roles in immune response regulation, inflammatory reactions, and intercellular communication. Detailed methodologies and comprehensive enrichment results for these analyses are provided in Supplementary Document SN7. Ablation study This ablation study accesses the impact of the confidence threshold on model performance. In particular, the scMSCF method selects subsets of high-confidence cells—determined by their proportion in the dataset, known as the confidence ratio—and utilizes these subsets as the entire training set for deep learning via the Transformer model. Choosing the right proportion of high-confidence cells is crucial for effective model training. Setting a high threshold, such as 80%, may result in a training set overly concentrated with high- confidence cells, thereby reducing sample diversity and increasing the risk of overfitting. This can cause the model to miss valuable information from low-confidence samples, thereby diminishing its generalization ability. Conversely, a low confidence ratio, such as 20%, might incorporate too many low-confidence samples into the training set, introducing noise that undermines model stability and accuracy. Low-confidence samples are typically associated with higher uncertainty, making it harder for the model to learn meaningful features, which in turn affects clustering accuracy. Balancing the proportion of high-confidence cells is essential to optimize the training set’s quality and diversity, thus enhancing overall model performance. In this experiment, we evaluated datasets with varying sizes using confidence ratios ranging from 20 to 80%. Extreme values of 10% and 90% were excluded due to their impracticality for model training. A 10% threshold would yield a training set dominated by low-confidence samples, leading to unstable training, while a 90% threshold would excessively reduce training diversity, impairing model generalization. By evaluating thresholds ranging from 20 to 80%, our goal was to identify the optimal confidence ratio that ensures a balance between training set quality and diversity. Based on the results in Fig. 8, the choice of confidence ratio has a significant impact on scMSCF’s clustering performance. We suggest the following ranges: for datasets with fewer than 3000 cells, a confidence ratio of 80–60%; for datasets with 3000 to 4500 cells, a range of 50–40%; and for datasets with more than 4500 cells, a range of 30–20%. Fig. 8. Trends in ARI across different dataset sizes for scMSCF as the confidence ratio varies from 20–80%. The x-axis represents the confidence ratio percentage, and the y-axis shows the ARI (Adjusted Rand Index), which evaluates clustering accuracy. The datasets included are: FearMem (6361 cells), LymphTIM (615 cells), iPSCMid (767 cells), PBMCv3 (4352 cells), MouseKidney (1385 cells), Lymphoma (2913 cells), PBMC5k (5025 cells), and PBMCChrome (3363 cells). This figure highlights how different confidence ratios affect clustering performance across datasets of varying sizes, providing insights into the optimal confidence levels for different data scales. Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:18543 12 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-03603-6 www.nature.com/scientificreports/ In addition to the confidence threshold analysis presented above, we further investigated the sensitivity of other key hyperparameters, including the number of Transformer layers, feature dimension (d_model), and learning rate. These experiments, detailed in Supplementary Document SN8, provide additional insights into how different parameter settings affect clustering performance and offer practical guidance for robust hyperparameter tuning. Discussion In this study, we proposed scMSCF, an innovative multi-step clustering framework designed to address the challenges of high-dimensionality, sparsity, and noise commonly associated with single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data analysis. By integrating multi-dimensional reduction strategies, weighted ensemble clustering methods, and Transformer-based deep learning techniques, scMSCF demonstrated exceptional clustering performance, robustness, and scalability. Evaluations on multiple real and simulated datasets showed that scMSCF significantly outperformed mainstream methods in key metrics such as Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) and Normalized Mutual Information (NMI). Notably, scMSCF introduced a confidence-based training set selection mechanism, which identifies high-confidence cells through a voting strategy within the weighted ensemble clustering process. This innovation greatly improved the reliability and diversity of the training data, providing a robust foundation for the Transformer model’s deep feature learning. Consequently, scMSCF achieved superior performance in identifying rare cell types and resolving heterogeneous cell populations, particularly in complex biological scenarios. In addition to its clustering accuracy, scMSCF exhibited excellent computational efficiency and scalability. Across simulated datasets of varying sizes, scMSCF showed an approximately linear increase in runtime with data size, indicating its suitability for large-scale data analysis. Furthermore, scMSCF excelled in pseudotime trajectory analysis, further highlighting its ability to extract meaningful biological insights. For example, in Parkinson’s disease-related iPSC midbrain neuron datasets, scMSCF not only accurately revealed dynamic cell state transitions over time but also aligned pseudotime results closely with actual time points, offering a clear depiction of cell transitions from early developmental stages to maturity. These results underscore scMSCF’s potential as a powerful tool for analyzing complex single-cell datasets while supporting studies of cellular differentiation and disease progression. To further substantiate the computational practicality of scMSCF, we conducted runtime and GPU memory profiling under two distinct high-performance computing platforms. As reported in Supplementary Document SN9, scMSCF was tested on both small- and medium-scale datasets (iPSC Midbrain and PBMC5k, respectively), and results showed that training and inference completed in under 10 min on typical modern GPUs, with memory usage staying well below 10 GB. These findings not only confirm scMSCF’s runtime scalability but also its deployability across diverse computing environments, reinforcing its suitability for real-world large-scale applications. Despite its significant advantages, scMSCF has certain limitations. First, scMSCF is sensitive to hyperparameter settings, requiring careful tuning to achieve optimal performance, especially regarding confidence thresholds and Transformer model parameters. Second, its effectiveness relies on the consistency and quality of initial clustering results, as these directly influence the selection of training cells and subsequent model performance. These limitations suggest areas for future improvement, particularly in developing methods to enhance robustness to parameter variation and initial clustering quality, further broadening the applicability and ease-of- use of scMSCF in diverse practical scenarios. Data Availability The six scRNA-seq datasets analyzed in this study are available in the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) repository under the accession numbers GSE246147(FearMem), GSE154763(LymphTIM), GSE247600(iPSCMid).The PBMCv3, MouseKidney, Lymphoma, PBMC5k, and PBMCChrome datasets analyzed in this study can be accessed from the 10X Genomics repository at the following links: ​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​w​w​w​.​1​0​x​g​e​n​o​m​i​c​s​.​c​o​m​/​d​a​t​a​s​e​t​s​/​p​ e​r​i​p​h​e​r​a​l​-​b​l​o​o​d​-​m​o​n​o​n​u​c​l​e​a​r​-​c​e​l​l​s​-​p​b​m​-​c​s​-​f​r​o​m​-​a​-​h​e​a​l​t​h​y​-​d​o​n​o​r​-​m​a​n​u​a​l​-​c​h​a​n​n​e​l​-​1​–​3​-​1​-​s​t​a​n​d​a​r​d​-​3​-​1​-​0​(​P​B​ M​C​v​3​), ​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​w​w​w​.​1​0​x​g​e​n​o​m​i​c​s​.​c​o​m​/​d​a​t​a​s​e​t​s​/​1​k​-​m​o​u​s​e​-​k​i​d​n​e​y​-​n​u​c​l​e​i​-​i​s​o​l​a​t​e​d​-​w​i​t​h​-​c​h​r​o​m​i​u​m​-​n​u​c​l​e​i​-​i​s​o​l​ a​t​i​o​n​-​k​i​t​-​3​-​1​-​s​t​a​n​d​a​r​d​(​M​o​u​s​e​K​i​d​n​e​y​), ​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​w​w​w​.​1​0​x​g​e​n​o​m​i​c​s​.​c​o​m​/​d​a​t​a​s​e​t​s​/​h​o​d​g​k​i​n​s​-​l​y​m​p​h​o​m​a​-​d​i​s​s​o​c​i​a​t​ e​d​-​t​u​m​o​r​-​t​a​r​g​e​t​e​d​-​c​o​m​p​a​r​e​-​i​m​m​u​n​o​l​o​g​y​-​p​a​n​e​l​-​3​-​1​-​s​t​a​n​d​a​r​d​-​4​-​0​-​0​(​L​y​m​p​h​o​m​a​), ​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​w​w​w​.​1​0​x​g​e​n​o​m​i​c​s​.​c​ o​m​/​d​a​t​a​s​e​t​s​/​5​-​k​-​p​e​r​i​p​h​e​r​a​l​-​b​l​o​o​d​-​m​o​n​o​n​u​c​l​e​a​r​-​c​e​l​l​s​-​p​b​m​-​c​s​-​f​r​o​m​-​a​-​h​e​a​l​t​h​y​-​d​o​n​o​r​-​v​-​3​-​c​h​e​m​i​s​t​r​y​-​3​-​1​-​s​t​a​n​d​ a​r​d​-​3​-​0​-​2​(​P​B​M​C​5​k​), and ​h​t​t​p​s​:​/​/​w​w​w​.​1​0​x​g​e​n​o​m​i​c​s​.​c​o​m​/​d​a​t​a​s​e​t​s​/​p​e​r​i​p​h​e​r​a​l​-​b​l​o​o​d​-​m​o​n​o​n​u​c​l​e​a​r​-​c​e​l​l​s​-​p​b​m​-​c​ s​-​f​r​o​m​-​a​-​h​e​a​l​t​h​y​-​d​o​n​o​r​-​c​h​r​o​m​i​u​m​-​c​o​n​n​e​c​t​-​c​h​a​n​n​e​l​-​5​-​3​-​1​-​s​t​a​n​d​a​r​d​-​3​-​1​-​0​(​P​B​M​C​C​h​r​o​m​e​). 6. **Remote sensing scene classification with masked image modeling**
   - DOI: https://doi.org/10.1117/12.2680898
   - Verdict: yes
   - Classifier reason: The citing paper explicitly applies masked image modeling pretrained backbones to remote sensing scene classification and benchmarks their performance, which is direct use of the target paper’s method family.
   - Matched patterns: none
   - LLM evidence source: abstract_fallback
   - LLM confidence: high
   - LLM evidence quote: This research aims to explore the potential of MIM pretrained backbones on four well-known classification datasets
   - Abstract evidence: Remote sensing scene classification has been extensively studied for its critical roles in geological survey, oil exploration, traffic management, earthquake prediction, wildfire monitoring, and intelligence monitoring. In the past, the Machine Learning (ML) methods for performing the task mainly used the backbones pretrained in the manner of supervised learning (SL). As Masked Image Modeling (MIM), a self-supervised learning (SSL) technique, has been shown as a better way for learning visual feature representation, it presents a new opportunity for improving ML performance on the scene classification task. This research aims to explore the potential of MIM pretrained backbones on four well-known classification datasets: Merced, AID, NWPU-RESISC45, and Optimal-31. Compared to the published benchmarks, we show that the MIM pretrained Vision Transformer (ViTs) backbones outperform other alternatives (up to 18% on top 1 accuracy) and that the MIM technique can learn better feature representation than the supervised learning counterparts (up to 5% on top 1 accuracy). Moreover, we show that the general-purpose MIM-pretrained ViTs can achieve competitive performance as the specially designed yet complicated Transformer for Remote Sensing (TRS) framework. Our experiment results also provide a performance baseline for future studies.
   - Full-text evidence: not available in extracted text set

7. **Quaternion-based vision-transformer for polycrystalline EBSD scans pre-trained on large-scale synthetic data**
   - DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2025.114599
   - Verdict: yes
   - Classifier reason: The citing paper explicitly proposes a Quaternion-Based Vision Transformer Masked Autoencoder and says it is pre-trained on synthetic data using self-supervision, directly adapting the Masked Autoencoder (MAE) approach from the target paper rather than merely mentioning it as background.
   - Matched patterns: none
   - LLM evidence source: abstract_fallback
   - LLM confidence: high
   - LLM evidence quote: a Quaternion-Based Vision Transformer Masked Autoencoder (ViT-MAE) is proposed
   - Abstract evidence: Dimensionality reduction is crucial in materials science for extracting patterns from high-dimensional data. This is vital for optimizing material design spaces by cutting computational complexity and for integrating diverse data sources for advanced anomaly detection, identifying structural or functional material deviations. Traditional convolutional autoencoders focus on local features, failing to capture global contextual information essential for predicting material behavior under stress, where overall material properties, grain orientations, morphologies and sizes are key. To address this, a Quaternion-Based Vision Transformer Masked Autoencoder (ViT-MAE) is proposed for polycrystalline materials. This model processes quaternion-valued EBSD orientation maps with a 65% masking ratio, capturing both local and global microstructural features efficiently. Pre-trained on synthetic data using self-supervision, the model achieves robust generalization on real-world EBSD scans by minimizing a misorientation loss function on the quaternion manifold. This work lays the groundwork for advanced multimodal data analysis in materials science, offering an open dataset and pre-trained vision-transformer weights. The goal is to provide a reliable latent space for EBSD orientation maps, advancing future multimodal characterization through latent space merging.
   - Full-text evidence: not available in extracted text set

8. **Advancing animal behavior recognition with self-supervised pre-training on unlabeled data**
   - DOI: https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w
   - Verdict: yes
   - Classifier reason: The paper explicitly uses the target method as the backbone for pre-training and fine-tuning: it states that it performs self-supervised pre-training of a PatchTST-based encoder and that the pre-trained encoder is then used as a feature-extraction backbone in the downstream classification model. The paper also cites the target paper as the source of the PatchTST architecture in this context.
   - Matched patterns: none
   - LLM evidence source: fulltext
   - LLM confidence: high
   - LLM evidence quote: “we employ the TF-C framework to conduct large-scale pre-training of a patch time series Transformer (PatchTST)37 based encoder”
   - Abstract evidence: Deep learning-based animal activity recognition (AAR) achieves promising performance but remains constrained by its reliance on large labeled datasets. While pre-training offers a viable path toward reducing annotation dependency, existing approaches like transfer learning primarily utilize single-species labeled data, neglecting the substantial publicly available unlabeled data across species. To address this gap, this study introduces a contrastive learning based self-supervised learning framework that effectively leverages cross-species unlabeled data to mitigate annotation scarcity in AAR. The approach involves two stages: (1) Self-supervised pre-training of a patch time series Transformer (PatchTST) based encoder using cross-species unlabeled data with a time-frequency consistency objective to learn transferable motion representations; and (2) Fine-tuning on species-specific labeled data, where the pre-trained encoder serves as a feature extraction backbone in a novel classification model that integrates intra-axis local dynamics via PatchTST-based encoder and cross-axis global patterns via depth-wise separable convolution. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances recognition performance under limited labeled data, achieving improvements of 4.79% in accuracy and 4.57% in F1-score over a baseline trained from scratch, while also improving discrimination of semantically similar behaviors and maintaining robustness with reduced samples. This work establishes a new direction for scalable and label-efficient animal behavior monitoring.
   - Full-text evidence: Advancing animal behavior recognition with self-supervised pre-training on unlabeled data Axiu Mao1,2,3,4, Miaoyun Peng1, Guikun Liu1, Meilu Zhu5 & Kaiying Wang2,4 Deep learning-based animal activity recognition (AAR) achieves promising performance but remains constrained by its reliance on large labeled datasets. While pre-training offers a viable path toward reducing annotation dependency, existing approaches like transfer learning primarily utilize single- species labeled data, neglecting the substantial publicly available unlabeled data across species. To address this gap, this study introduces a contrastive learning based self-supervised learning framework that effectively leverages cross-species unlabeled data to mitigate annotation scarcity in AAR. The approach involves two stages: (1) Self-supervised pre-training of a patch time series Transformer (PatchTST) based encoder using cross-species unlabeled data with a time-frequency consistency objective to learn transferable motion representations; and (2) Fine-tuning on species-specific labeled data, where the pre-trained encoder serves as a feature extraction backbone in a novel classification model that integrates intra-axis local dynamics via PatchTST-based encoder and cross-axis global patterns via depth-wise separable convolution. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances recognition performance under limited labeled data, achieving improvements of 4.79% in accuracy and 4.57% in F1-score over a baseline trained from scratch, while also improving discrimination of semantically similar behaviors and maintaining robustness with reduced samples. This work establishes a new direction for scalable and label-efficient animal behavior monitoring. Keywords Animal behavior, Time series, Self-supervised learning, Time-frequency consistency, Transformer model List of symbols t/T Time-domain f/F Frequency-domain X Cross-species unlabeled dataset xt i i-th original time-series sample xT i Time domain-based augmented sample X T i Time-based augmentation dataset xf i Spectral representation derived from xt i xF i Spectral-based augmented sample X F i Spectral-based augmentation dataset ∼ X T Set of combination of xt i and X T i ∼ X F Set of combination of xf i and X F i ET Time-domain encoder EF Frequency-domain encoder LT i Time-domain contrastive loss τ Temperature hyperparameter LF i Frequency-domain contrastive loss RT /RF Projectors ht i/hT i Feature extracted from xt i and xT i using ET 1School of Communication Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, Zhejiang Province, China. 2Key Laboratory of Equipment and Informatization in Environment Controlled Agriculture, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, Beijing, P.R. China. 3Guangxi Yangxiang Group Co., Ltd., Guigang 537131, China. 4College of Biosystems Engineering and Food Science, Zhejiang University, 866 Yuhangtang Road, Hangzhou 310058, China. 5Department of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, The University of Hong Kong, Pokfulam 999077, Hong Kong, China. email: zjuwky@zju.edu.cn OPEN Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:43928 1 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w www.nature.com/scientificreports hf i /hF i Feature extracted from xf i and xF i using EF zt i/zT i Projected representation of ht i/hT i using RT zf i /zF i Projected representation of hf i /hF i using RF Stf i Distance between embeddings zt i and zf i LC i Consistency loss LT F −C i Joint loss function λ Weighting coefficient X′ Species-specific labeled dataset x′ i Sample from X′ L Temporal length of original time -series sample P Patch number S Stride A Axis number N Total number of patches per axis WP Shared linear projection matrix Wpos Positional embedding matrix ∼x ′ i Embedded patch token ∼ f ′ i Condensed feature of f ′ i ​ after averaging along patch dimension ˙f ′ i Enhanced feature of ∼ f ′ i after depth-wise separable convolution z′ i Flattened feature vector of ˙f ′ i for final classification Abbreviations AAR Animal activity recognition CNN Convolutional neural network GELU Gaussian error linear unit GNSS Global Navigation Satellite System LSTM Long short-term memory PatchTST Patch time series Transformer SSL Self-supervised learning TF-C Time-frequency consistency t-SNE t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding Animal behavior serves as a vital indicator for evaluating both physiological and psychological well-being in animals1. Compared to conventional manual observation methods, the development of intelligent and fine- grained animal behavior monitoring systems can significantly reduce manpower and time investments while enhancing cost efficiency2. Currently, wearable sensors, such as accelerometers, gyroscopes, and Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS), have been widely used in animal behavior monitoring, owing to their compact, energy-efficient, and interference-resistant design with high-fidelity sensing. They are typically attached to different animal body parts like ears, neck, or legs to accurately capture movement signals, which are then input to models for feature extraction and classification of different animal behaviors (e.g., grazing, drinking). Deep learning has emerged as a powerful computational approach in animal activity recognition (AAR) tasks, owing to its automated pattern recognition and feature extraction from complex datasets3,4. Literature has demonstrated the promising performance of deep learning models in distinguishing various animal activities2,5. For instance, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks can identify cow feeding and ruminating behaviors with up to 87% accuracy by analyzing triaxial acceleration data from nose-worn wearable devices6. Eerdekens et al. applied convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to behavior recognition of horses and dogs based on accelerometer data. Their research results consistently show that CNNs perform excellently, with an accuracy rate exceeding 97%1,7–9. Yin et al. proposed the CNN_LSTM_Attention model, which achieves 89.3% accuracy in classifying pig health status10. Although deep learning-based AAR methods have made significant progress, their high performance often relies on large amounts of labeled data. However, due to the labor-intensive and time-consuming nature of labeling work, such data is often scarce, which directly impairs the model’s ability to learn features and generalization. To address the scarcity of labeled data, prevailing approaches in literature commonly employ three principal strategies, i.e., expanding labelled data via augmentation techniques11,12, leveraging unlabeled data via semi-supervised learning13 or active learning14, and pre-training models on extensive datasets via transfer learning15,16. Amongst, pre-training serves as a promising solution, obtaining high-quality model initialization by learning general representations from large datasets and thus significantly reducing reliance on labeled data17. For instance, Kleanthous et al. achieved precise identification of three sheep activities by developing a hybrid model that integrates CNNs with handcrafted time-frequency domain features and leveraging deep transfer learning16. This approach effectively tackles sensor heterogeneity and positional variance, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance with 98.55% accuracy on source data and 96.59% on target data. Nevertheless, existing approaches in AAR primarily depend on restricted labeled datasets from single species for pre-training, neglecting the substantial quantities of publicly available unlabeled data from different species. These unlabeled resources, while more readily accessible and rich in behavioral information, remain largely untapped in current research paradigms. Therefore, effectively leveraging such multi-species unlabeled data to mitigate annotation scarcity presents a critical yet under-explored challenge. Self-supervised learning (SSL) offers an efficient paradigm that harnesses massive unlabeled data for model initialization, effectively mitigating the scarcity of labeled data in downstream tasks. Masked Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:43928 2 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ reconstruction18,19 and contrastive learning20,21 are the dominant SSL techniques. Following these paradigms, some time-series SSL methods have recently emerged22–28. Masked reconstruction trains models to recover randomly masked input segments, promoting learning of robust data representations29,30. However, such methods face inherent challenges with time-series data, where the core semantics of sensor signals like periodicity, trends, and peak-valley structures are intrinsically linked to temporal dynamics. Masking disrupts this temporal integrity, substantially complicating reconstruction and hindering the model’s ability to capture key data semantics31. Conversely, contrastive learning has broader practical adoption. It trains models to identify similarities between positive sample pairs (e.g., augmented views of the same instance) while differentiating them from negative pairs (views from different instances). Standard methods like SimCLR32 and SimMTM31 adopt this framework. However, applying traditional augmentations inherited from the field of natural language processing and computer vision, including flipping, permutation, or cropping, to motion time-series remains challenging. These operations can invert trends, disrupt event causality, or damage peak-valley structures due to the critical dependence of signal semantics on temporal order and dynamics33, thereby compromising semantic consistency between original and augmented samples and undermining representation learning. To address this limitation, methods like Time-Frequency Consistency (TF-C)34, Mixing-up35, and FEI36 have shifted towards frequency- domain augmentation, including frequency masking, mixing, and amplitude perturbation, with empirical evidence confirming their efficacy in preserving semantic consistency for motion time-series data. Based on these considerations, contrastive learning with frequency-domain augmentation presents a more suitable approach for time-series data in AAR, effectively addressing semantic consistency challenges while preserving crucial behavioral patterns. Such a technique will be consequently adopted and validated in this work. In this study, we introduce a contrastive learning-based SSL method designed to learn general feature representations from publicly available unlabeled datasets, under the assumption that representations learned from large-scale cross-species data can provide a superior initialization for species-specific downstream classification tasks, thereby effectively alleviating the performance degradation caused by scarce labeled data. The method follows a two-stage pipeline: (1) First, we employ the TF-C framework to conduct large- scale pre-training of a patch time series Transformer (PatchTST)37 based encoder on cross-species unlabeled data, optimizing time-frequency representations through consistency loss minimization. Herein, we assume the existence of generalizable motion patterns transferable across species during similar behaviors, based on established literature38–40 documenting behavioral similarities between species and our recent validation of cross-species model training feasibility41, thereby supporting model pre-training on cross-species unlabeled datasets. In addition, further assume that the temporal and frequency-domain representations of the same behavioral sample share consistent core semantics, which enables the use of the TF-C objective to minimize their representational distance in the feature space, thereby facilitating the learning of more robust and invariant features. (2) Then, the pre-trained encoder acts as a feature-extraction backbone within a newly designed classification model, which is fine-tuned on species-specific labeled data. This model incorporates both intra-axis feature extraction via PatchTST and cross-axis feature fusion via depth-wise separable convolution. We evaluate the generalization capability of the fine-tuned model using leave-one-subject-out validation and compare its performance against a baseline trained from scratch. The main contributions of this work are summarized as follows. • Unlike existing pre-training methods that typically depend on limited labeled datasets from a single species, this study pioneers a novel paradigm for AAR by constructing the first large-scale cross-species unlabeled da­ taset and developing a corresponding self-supervised learning methodology. It effectively addresses the dual challenges of annotation scarcity and species-specific dependency, establishing a new research direction that leverages cross-species unlabeled data through self-supervised learning techniques. • We develop a novel classification architecture that integrates patch-level segmentation for capturing fine- grained temporal dynamics within individual axes with depth-wise separable convolution for efficient cross-axis feature fusion. The proposed design enables simultaneous modeling of local patterns and global behavioral dependencies while maintaining parameter efficiency, effectively addressing both feature rep­ resentation learning and computational complexity challenges in wearable sensor-aided animal behavior recognition. • Experimental results validate that the proposed method enhances animal behavior classification under limit­ ed labeled data, achieving improvements of 4.79% in accuracy and 4.57% in F1-score over the baseline while demonstrating consistent advantages in discriminating semantically similar behaviors, improving classifica­ tion balance, and maintaining robustness with reduced training samples. Materials and methods Overview To address the challenge of limited labeled data in animal activity recognition, this study proposes an SSL framework based on contrastive learning that leverages publicly available cross-species unlabeled data. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the framework consists of two consecutive phases: (1) In the pre-training stage, a PatchTST- based encoder is trained using the TF-C framework on cross-species unlabeled data, where the model learns robust representations through three complementary supervision signals: time-domain contrastive learning, frequency-domain contrastive learning, and cross-domain temporal-spectral alignment, integrated via a joint loss function; (2) In the fine-tuning stage, the pre-trained encoder serves as a feature extraction backbone for the downstream classification model, which is subsequently optimized on target species-specific labeled data using a carefully designed architecture that incorporates depth-wise separable convolution for effective cross- axis feature fusion. Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:43928 3 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ Model pre-training on cross-species unlabeled data Existing contrastive learning research often employs temporal augmentations to generate multi-view data, but struggles to maintain semantic consistency between original and augmented samples34,42. Time-Frequency Consistency (TF-C)34 addresses this issue by performing augmentation in the frequency domain, thus effectively preserving semantic consistency by maintaining temporal-spectral correlations. Inspired by this, we introduce an SSL method based on the TF-C principle to pretrain a PatchTST-based encoder using cross-species unlabeled data. As shown in Fig. 1, the method incorporates three dedicated components to supervise feature learning from distinct viewpoints. The respective objectives of these components are: (a) to learn time-invariant features from the time-domain signal; (b) to reveal frequency-domain invariance by introducing perturbations to the spectral characteristics; and (c) to achieve cross-domain alignment between the temporal and spectral representations. A dedicated loss function is derived from each objective, and these losses are subsequently integrated into a composite loss function to jointly optimize the contrastive learning. The details are also described below. Within the time domain, a time-domain contrastive loss LT i is constructed to learn time-invariant features. As illustrated in Fig. 1, given an unlabeled sensor dataset X, for each time-series sample xt i, we apply several time-based augmentation techniques to generate an augmentation set X T i . Here, the augmentation techniques include jittering, scaling, time shifting, and cropping. From this set, we randomly select one augmented sample xT i ( xT i ∈X T i ), which along with the original sample xt i are mapped into embedding vectors ht i and hT i via a shared time-domain encoder ET . The encoder ET is designed based on the PatchTST architecture37, as detailed in Sect. “Model fine-tuning on species-specific labeled data”. Since xT i is derived from xt i, we expect the embedding of xT i to be close to that of xt i, but away from embeddings of other samples xt j or their augmented variants xT j derived from the dataset ∼ X T , where xt j ∈X, i ̸= j, ∼ X T = {xt j, X T j }. This forms a positive pair (xt i, xT i ) and negative pairs (xt i, xt j) and (xt i, xT j ). We employ a temperature-scaled cross-entropy based contrastive loss to maximize similarity within positive pairs and minimize similarity across negative pairs, corresponding to the conventional contrastive objective for time-domain augmentation consistency: LT i = −log exp ( sim(ht i, hT i ) τ ) ∑ xt j∈ ∼ X T ⋖i̸= jexp ( sim( ht i, ET ( xt j )) τ ), (1) Fig. 1. Overall flowchart of the proposed method. The method operates in two main stages: (1) Pre-training stage: a PatchTST-based time-domain encoder ET is trained via the Time-Frequency Consistency (TF-C) framework on cross-species unlabeled data, where the model learns invariant representations by minimizing a joint loss combining time-domain contrastive loss ( LT i ), frequency-domain contrastive loss ( LF i ), and cross-domain consistency loss ( LC i ). (2) Fine-tuning stage: the pre-trained encoder ET serves as a feature extraction backbone in a classification model enhanced with depth-wise separable convolution, followed by fine-tuning on species-specific labeled data. Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:43928 4 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ where sim (u, v) = uTv ∥u∥·∥v∥ denotes cosine similarity, ⋖i̸= j is an indicator function to avoid self-contrastive pairs, and τ is a temperature hyperparameter ( τ = 0.2, as in the original study that proposed TF-C34). Here, xt j ∈ ∼ X T denotes either original time-series samples or their augmented variants. Within the frequency domain, a frequency-domain contrastive loss LF i is built to learn frequency-invariant features. For each time-series sample xt i, we first convert it into a spectral representation via Fourier transform, resulting in xf i ​. We then apply frequency-domain augmentation strategies, including spectral perturbation and masking, to this representation to generate a set of augmented spectral samples X F i . From this set, we randomly select one augmented sample xF i ( xF i ∈X F i ). Both the original spectral sample xf i ​ and its augmented version xF i are then mapped into embedding vectors hf i and hF i , respectively, using a frequency-domain encoder EF . It is worth noting that EF shares the same PatchTST-based architecture as the time-domain encoder ET , but has independent parameters. Similar with the loss within time-domain, the contrastive loss can be defined as follows: LF i = −log exp ( sim( hf i , hF i ) τ ) ∑ xf j ∈ ∼ X F ⋖i̸= jexp ( sim( hf i , EF ( xf j )) τ ), (2) where, xf j ∈ ∼ X F denotes either original spectral representations or their augmented variants. For cross-domain alignment, a novel consistency loss LC i is designed to align time-domain and frequency- domain representations within a shared latent space. Specifically, two separate cross-domain projectors RT and RF are employed to map the time-domain embeddings ht i, hT i and frequency-domain embeddings hf i , hF i into a unified time-frequency space, yielding projected representations zt i = RT ( ht i ) , zT i = RT ( hT i ) , zf i = RF ( hf i ) , and zF i = RF ( hF i ) . To enforce TF-C, we design a loss that encourages the embeddings derived from the original time-series sample and its spectral representation to be closer to each other than to those originating from augmented versions. Formally, we define the distance between the original time and frequency embeddings as Stf i =d( zt i, zf i ), and introduce three pairs: StF i =d( zt i, zF i ), ST f i =d( zT i , zf i ), and ST F i =d( zT i , zF i ), each involving at least one augmented view. The time-frequency consistency loss is then formulated as a margin-based objective: LC i = ∑ Spair ( Stf i −Spair i + δ ) , Spair ∈{ ST f i , StF i , ST F i } , (3) where δ > 0 is a margin hyperparameter. This loss ensures that the distance between the original time and frequency embeddings is smaller than the distances involving augmented samples, thereby promoting alignment between the two domains while preserving semantic consistency across augmentations. In the end, the model is optimized using a joint loss function LT F −C i that integrates the three obtained loss terms, LT i , LF i , and LC i , as follows: LT F −C i = λ ( LT i + LF i ) + (1 −λ ) LC i , (4) where λ ∈[0,1] is a weighting coefficient that balances the contributions of the time-domain and frequency- domain contrastive losses against the cross-domain consistency loss. During pre-training, the total loss is computed by averaging LT F −C i over all samples in the pre-training dataset. Model fine-tuning on species-specific labeled data Upon completion of model pre-training, the pretrained PatchTST-based encoder functions as a feature extractor within a newly constructed classification model. This model is subsequently fine-tuned using species-specific labeled data. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the PatchTST-based encoder first extracts features from the signal along each individual axis. The temporal patching mechanism employed in PatchTST is particularly effective at capturing local dynamic variations within the signal segments. These axis-specific features are integrated via a depth- wise separable convolution layer, which adaptively fuses cross-axis information while maintaining parameter efficiency. Given a sensor input sample x′ i from the species-specific labeled dataset X′ , with total temporal length L, we process it through a PatchTST-based backbone for extracting features, denoted as f ′ i . The overall procedure, illustrated in Fig. 2, consists of three main stages: patch partitioning, token embedding, and temporal encoding. First, the signal along each axis j is partitioned into fixed-length patches using a sliding window with size P and stride S, where j ∈[1,2, . . . , A] and A denotes the total number of sensor axes. This process is depicted in Fig. 3. To handle boundary effects, symmetric padding is applied by replicating the last value k times such that the padded sequence accommodates an integer number of patches. The total number of patches per axis is given by: N = ⌊L −P S ⌋+ 2, (5) Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:43928 5 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ where ⌊·⌋ denotes the floor operation. Each patch corresponds to a local temporal segment of the motion signal, for instance, acceleration fluctuations during standing behavior. This patching strategy reduces the number of input tokens from L to approximately L S , significantly lowering the computational burden of subsequent attention mechanisms. All patches {x′ i (j, p)}A,N j,p=1 from every axis j are then independently mapped into a latent space of dimension D via a shared linear projection WP ∈RD× P , combined with positional embedding Wpos ∈RD× P to preserve temporal order: ∼x ′ i (j, p) = WP · x′ i (j, p) + Wpos, (6) where ∼x ′ i (j, p) ∈RD represents the embedded patch token. These tokens are subsequently fed into a standard Transformer encoder for temporal modeling. The encoder employs multi-head self-attention to capture global dependencies across time steps. Each attention head operates in parallel, enabling efficient multidimensional feature interaction. The architecture further includes feed-forward networks with Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) activation43, residual connections around each sub-layer, and layer normalization for stable training and improved gradient flow. Notably, the same projection and Transformer weights are shared across all patches and all axes. Recognizing that the features obtained above are extracted axis-independently and lack explicit modeling of inter-axis relationships, we introduce a depth-wise separable convolution module to facilitate cross-axis interaction and feature fusion. As illustrated in Fig. 1, let f ′ i ∈RA× N× D denote the extracted feature for each input sample x′ i . We first average the features along the patch dimension for each axis, resulting in a condensed representation ∼ f ′ i ∈RA× D. This feature is then processed via a depth-wise separable convolution block. Specifically, a channel-wise grouped 1D convolution with a kernel size of 3 (denoted as Conv1× 3) is applied to each axis independently, capturing local cross-channel patterns. This is followed by a pointwise convolution ( Conv1× 1) to fuse information across all channels, producing an enhanced feature ˙f ′ i ∈RA× D′ . The operation is formulated as: Fig. 3. Example of patch operation. Fig. 2. Patch time series Transformer (PatchTST) based feature extractor37. Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:43928 6 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ ˙f ′ i = GELU (Conv1× 1( Conv1× 3 ( ∼ f ′ i ) )) . (7) Finally, the feature ˙f ′ i is flattened into a vector z′ i of dimension of A · D′ , which is then passed through a fully connected layer for final classification. Datasets As shown in Table 1, the datasets used in this study comprise two distinct parts: a cross-species unlabeled dataset employed for model pre-training, and a species-specific labeled dataset used for fine-tuning. All data are sourced from publicly available open-source repositories. A detailed description of each dataset is provided below. Cross-species unlabeled dataset This dataset aggregates publicly accessible accelerometer data from cattle, sheep, and horses, totaling 576,897 unlabeled samples12,16,44,45. Consistent with common practice in animal activity recognition2, only triaxial accelerometer data are utilized, as this modality has been widely validated as both prevalent and effective for the task. Initially, the dataset was fully labeled across 13 categories of common animal behaviors. To simulate an unlabeled environment for model pre-training, we subsequently removed all labels. Species-specific labeled dataset This dataset comprises 42,943 annotated samples collected from five goats46, encompassing five behavioral categories: standing (43.15%), grazing (35.35%), walking (20.19%), running (0.87%), and trotting (0.44%). A severe class imbalance is present, with an imbalance ratio of 98.05; the minority class, trotting, contains only 189 samples. This dataset is used for fine-tuning the pretrained model. Implementation details Data preprocessing For the cross-species pre-training dataset, we applied a systematic preprocessing pipeline to ensure consistency across heterogeneous data sources. First, multiple publicly available datasets were cleaned and harmonized by standardizing their sampling rates. High-frequency recordings (e.g., 100 Hz) were down-sampled, while low- frequency data (e.g., 12.5 Hz) were up-sampled to 25 Hz via nonlinear interpolation. This rate was chosen based on previous studies indicating that 25 Hz provides a suitable balance between performance retention and computational efficiency2. To maintain uniformity across datasets, only triaxial acceleration data (common to all sources) were retained. The signals were segmented using a 2-second sliding window and normalized through z-score standardization, resulting in a final tensor of dimensions 576,897 × 50 × 3. The same preprocessing steps, including 2-second window segmentation and z-score normalization, were applied to the species-specific labeled dataset, yielding a tensor of size 42,943 × 50 × 3. Experimental setting This study evaluates the effectiveness of the proposed method by comparing a classification model initialized with pretrained parameters (our method) against one with random initialization (baseline). The objective is to assess whether cross-species pre-training improves behavior recognition performance for the target species. We employ accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, macro-average, and weighted-average as evaluation metrics to comprehensively assess model performance47,48. The macro-average metric computes the arithmetic mean of per-class scores, giving equal weight to each class, whereas the weighted-average accounts for class imbalance by weighting each class’s score by its sample size. Similar to previous studies49–51, we employed the leave-one-subject-out validation method with three random repetitions to assess the generalization of the fine-tuned classification model. In each repetition, we randomly assigned one goat for testing, one for validation, and the remaining four for training, while ensuring that the testing and validation sets were derived from different goats across all three runs. The model was trained using a class-balanced cross-entropy loss function52, augmented with an L2 regularization term (weight decay = 0.01) to mitigate overfitting. Optimization was performed with the Adam optimizer at a learning rate of 1 × 10⁻⁴. Each training run consisted of 100 epochs with a batch size of 32. The model achieving the highest validation accuracy was saved and evaluated on the test set. An early stopping strategy with a patience of 10 epochs was applied Species Animal count Sensor Placement Sampling rate Behavior category Cattle12 6 Accelerometer Neck 25 Hz Feeding, drinking, grazing, rumination, licking, resting (lying/standing), moving, urinating, attacking, fleeing Cattle44 8 Accelerometer &GNSS Neck 50 Hz Eating, drinking, resting, walking Sheep16 9 Accelerometer Neck 12.5 Hz Feeding, dynamic behaviors (walking, scratching), static behaviors (standing) Horse45 18 IMU Neck 100 Hz Standing, walking, trotting, cantering Goat46 5 Accelerometer &Gyroscope Neck 100 Hz Stationary behaviors, walking, trotting, running, grazing, climbing, competing for food Table 1. Public datasets for AAR based on wearable sensors. Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:43928 7 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ during training. All experiments were implemented in PyTorch and executed on a single NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090 GPU. Results and discussion Performance comparison with the baseline method The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed pretrained framework significantly enhances animal activity recognition performance compared to the baseline model with random initialization. As summarized in Table 2, the classification model initialized with pretrained parameters achieves an accuracy of 92.55%, an F1-score of 82.32%, a precision of 81.68%, and a recall of 89.55%. These results represent an improvement over the baseline across all metrics, with increments of 4.79% in accuracy, 4.57% in F1-score, 3.18% in precision, and 4.64% in recall. The consistent gains in every evaluation metric confirm that cross-species pre-training can effectively improve classification performance and generalization capability, reflecting the information- capturing ability of TF-C based SSL method from unlabeled data. Figure 4 presents the recall confusion matrices for both the baseline method and our method, where the values on the principal diagonal of each matrix represent the percentage of correctly classified samples for each behavior category51. The baseline results indicate that trotting is frequently misclassified as running, and walking is often confused with grazing. This observation aligns with earlier studies46,53 on the same dataset, which attributed these confusions to the inherent difficulty in distinguishing trotting from running during manual labeling, as well as the common co-occurrence of walking and grazing in goats. Notably, our method with self-supervised pre-training improves the recall for trotting and walking by 10.2% and 15.2%, respectively, demonstrating its ability to capture discriminative features that help separate easily confusable behaviors. These findings suggest that self-supervised learning on unlabeled data can effectively extract valuable information to enhance fine-grained classification under limited labeled data scenarios, offering a promising direction for mitigating confusion between behavior categories with similar appearance. Figure 5 illustrates the training loss and accuracy curves across three experiments for both our method and the baseline, indicating that the pre-trained model converges more rapidly and reaches a higher training accuracy. Furthermore, the pre-trained model exhibits reduced variance across experiments, demonstrating greater training robustness. These results confirm that initializing with pre-trained weights enables the classification model to adapt more efficiently during fine-tuning, leading to faster convergence and improved performance. This effectively validates the utility of pre-training on unlabeled data as an effective strategy for enhancing model training. Visualization of the pre-training process To qualitatively verify the effectiveness of TF-C in mining information from unlabeled data, we visualized the feature vectors extracted by the time encoder (i.e., the feature extractor) before and after pre-training using t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE)54, as shown in Fig. 6. For the purpose of this analysis, the original labels of the samples were restored to evaluate the discriminative power of the features learned by the pre-trained time encoder. It can be observed that the feature extractor before pre-training produced Fig. 4. Recall confusion matrices of the baseline method (a) and our method (b). Model Accuracy# F1-Score Precision Recall Baseline method 87.76% 77.75% 78.50% 84.91% Our method 92.55% 82.32% 81.68% 89.55% Table 2. Comparison performance of our method with the baseline. # The best result for each metric is highlighted in bold. Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:43928 8 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ weakly discriminative representations, with limited separation between different categories in the feature space. In contrast, after pre-training, the extracted features exhibited clearer boundaries between classes and reduced misclassification tendencies. Although some samples within the same class appear relatively scattered, this is expected due to the inherent cross-species nature of the dataset, i.e., not all animal species exhibit identical behavioral patterns for the same category of behavior. Furthermore, the loss curve during the pre-training process is presented in Fig. 7, which further demonstrates the robustness and stability of the proposed TF-C method during the pre-training process. Fig. 6. t-SNE visualization of the feature vectors extracted by the time encoder before (a) and after (b) pre- training. Fig. 5. Training loss and accuracy curves across three runs for the baseline (a) and proposed methods (b). Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:43928 9 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ Analysis of our proposed classification model In addition to the PatchTST-based feature extractor, our classification model incorporates the depth-wise separable convolution, which combines grouped 1D convolution with pointwise convolution. To evaluate their individual contributions to the final classification performance, we compared four configurations: (1) the model without depth-wise separable convolution, (2) with only grouped 1D convolution, (3) with only pointwise convolution, and (4) with both operations. Results in Table 3 show that each operation independently improves performance, confirming that both are critical for goat behavior classification. It is worth noting that the macro- average accuracy places greater emphasis on classification balance across different behavioral categories47. We observe that the inclusion of grouped 1D convolution significantly enhances the macro-average accuracy. This improvement can be attributed to the ability of 1D convolution applied along each sensor axis to capture local temporal dependencies within axis-specific signals. By extracting more fine-grained motion patterns, the model learns discriminative features that are particularly beneficial for recognizing behaviors with limited samples, thereby improving classification performance even for under-represented categories. The integration of pointwise convolution following the grouped 1D convolution further enhances overall model performance, yielding improvements of 0.05% in accuracy, 3.61% in F1-score, 7.88% in macro-average accuracy, and 0.49% in weighted-average accuracy compared to the model without these components. This gain can be attributed to the role of pointwise convolution in effectively combining cross-axis features extracted by the preceding grouped 1D convolutions, thereby enabling a more comprehensive local-to-global feature representation. Robustness against variations in dataset size This work employs self-supervised learning to leverage large-scale unlabeled cross-species data, aiming to enhance robustness under limited labeled samples. To systematically assess classification performance with varying labeled data availability, we compared our method against the baseline using progressively reduced training subsets (75%, 50%, and 25% of the original labeled data) during the fine-tuning process. As illustrated in Fig. 8, the proposed approach consistently surpasses the baseline in both accuracy and F1-score across all data volumes. These findings indicate that the features acquired through self-supervised pre-training possess strong generalization ability and remain less sensitive to variations in labeled data volume. The results further verify that our approach can effectively leverage cross-species data diversity to construct more reliable models in label-scarce scenarios. Limitations and implications This work presents the first attempt to utilize a cross-species unlabeled dataset for self-supervised pre-training in AAR, demonstrating significant potential to mitigate annotation scarcity. However, the unlabeled data used in this work were derived from originally annotated datasets by removing labels, thus representing a simulated Configurations Accuracy# F1-score Macro-average Accuracy Weighted-average Accuracy Grouped 1D convolution Pointwise convolution 87.71% 74.14% 75.23% 90.02% ✔ 87.38% 75.50% 83.57% 90.33% ✔ 88.32% 75.87% 76.88% 89.56% ✔ ✔ 87.76% 77.75% 83.11% 90.51% Table 3. Performance evaluation of grouped 1D Convolution and Pointwise Convolution operations. # The best and second-best values for each metric are highlighted in bold and underlined, respectively. Fig. 7. Time-frequency consistency loss curve during pre-training. Scientific Reports | (2025) 15:43928 10 | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-27736-w www.nature.com/scientificreports/ corpus rather than real-world unlabeled data. Theoretically, the presence or absence of labels in the pre-training data has a minimal impact on the TF-C based pre-training, as the method focuses solely on time-frequency consistency within a sample itself and does not use category labels. Nevertheless, empirical validation with genuinely unlabeled data remains essential to further confirm the practical efficacy of this self-supervised approach. Although the current pre-training data encompass multiple species and scenarios to simulate real- world variability, the dataset remains limited in scale and diversity since it was constructed from existing annotated sources. Therefore, future work will prioritize collecting larger-scale, truly unlabeled data from a wider range of animal species and authentic environments. Such expansion is essential to enable the training of a foundational model with strong generalization capabilities for animal behavior recognition. Moreover, this learning paradigm could be extended to other sensory modalities such as audio and vision, further alleviating the labeling bottleneck and promoting scalable intelligent monitoring in the field of precision livestock farming. Furthermore, while the objective of our pre-training was to learn universal behavioral features across species, the model did not explicitly account for the fact that similar behaviors can manifest through distinct motion patterns in different animals. This oversight may limit the generalizability and discriminative power of the learned representations. To address this, future work should incorporate species-specific prior knowledge, such as taxonomic or morphological information, into the self-supervised learning framework. By guiding the model to disentangle species-invariant behavioral patterns from species-specific motion characteristics, we can enhance both the quality of pre-trained features and their transferability to downstream recognition tasks. Conclusion This study presents a self-supervised learning framework that effectively leverages cross-species unlabeled data to enhance AAR under limited annotation scenarios. The primary strength of this work lies in its two-stage design: self-supervised pre-training on cross-species unlabeled data using the Time-Frequency Consistency objective to learn transferable motion representations, followed by fine-tuning on species-specific labeled data, where depth-wise separable convolution enables effective local-to-global feature extraction by capturing both fine- grained temporal patterns and cross-axis dependencies. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach substantially enhances recognition performance under limited labeled data, achieving a 4.79% improvement in accuracy and a 4.57% increase in F1-score compared to the baseline trained from scratch. This performance gain validates the practical value of our method, showing consistent advantages across multiple aspects, including improved discrimination of semantically similar behaviors, enhanced classification balance across different behavioral categories, and maintained robustness with reduced training samples. However, the study has limitations, including the use of simulated rather than genuine unlabeled data and the lack of explicit modeling for species-specific motion characteristics, which may affect the generalizability of the learned features. For future applications, this paradigm offers a viable pathway toward foundational animal behavior models that can adapt to new species or environments with minimal supervision. We recommend subsequent research to focus on constructing large-scale, truly unlabeled datasets collected in real-world settings and to explore the integration of multi-modal data or biological priors for improved representation learning. Such efforts would further advance scalable and accurate monitoring in precision livestock farming.​.
