13. **fNIRS Foundation Model for Few-Shot Based fNIRS Classification**
   - DOI: https://doi.org/10.1109/bci65088.2025.10931275
   - Verdict: yes
   - Classifier reason: The citing paper explicitly uses a self-supervised masked autoencoder framework for its proposed foundation model, which indicates direct application/adaptation of the target paper’s MAE method rather than just mentioning it as background.
   - Matched patterns: none
   - LLM evidence source: abstract_fallback
   - LLM confidence: high
   - LLM evidence quote: we propose a novel foundation model for fNIRS data based on a self-supervised masked autoencoder framework
   - Abstract evidence: Functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) is a non-invasive technique with significant potential for applications in brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) including mental health diagnostics and cognitive state monitoring. However, the reliance on large labeled datasets for high-performing classification methods poses a critical challenge, given the time-consuming and resource-intensive nature of fNIRS data collection. To address this, we propose a novel foundation model for fNIRS data based on a self-supervised masked autoencoder framework. The proposed method enables efficient pre-training on unlabeled data, reducing the dependence on labeled datasets while maintaining robust performance for downstream tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves performance comparable to supervised learning approaches while requiring only one-third of the labeled training data. It consistently outperforms state-of-the-art self-supervised models in both linear probing and fine-tuning settings. Moreover, ablation studies show that a larger masking size aligns with the low-frequency nature of fNIRS signals, enabling the model to capture broader patterns and further enhance classification accuracy. These findings validate the proposed method as an effective and scalable solution for fNIRS-based classification tasks.
   - Full-text evidence: not available in extracted text set

14. **View-label driven cross-space structure alignment for incomplete multi-view partial multi-label classification**
   - DOI: https://doi.org/10.1007/s44443-025-00130-2
   - Verdict: yes
   - Classifier reason: The citing paper directly uses the target paper’s masked autoencoder idea as a methodological basis for its own view completion module. In the related-work/method description it explicitly cites the target and says it is inspired by that approach to build its autoencoder-based reconstruction pipeline.
   - Matched patterns: none
   - LLM evidence source: fulltext
   - LLM confidence: high
   - LLM evidence quote: Inspired by He et al. (2022); Liu et al. (2023c), we propose a View-Label Hybrid-Driven Autoencoder (VLAE) for missing view completion.
   - Abstract evidence: Despite significant advancements in multi-view multi-label learning driven by its broad applicability, real-world scenarios frequently suffer from dual incompleteness in both view and label spaces due to data acquisition uncertainties. The incompleteness of multi-view features degrades the comprehensiveness of sample representations, leading to failure in capturing semantically discriminative patterns essential for classification. To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose a novel learning framework termed V iew- L abel Driven C ross-Space S tructure A lignment Network (VLCSA). Departing from existing low-quality view completion approaches, we devise a view-label hybrid-driven autoencoder (VLAE) that extracts discriminative view-specific features through joint optimization of cross-view semantic consistency and label-guided instance-level embeddings, which allows for accurate reconstruction of missing views. Furthermore, we propose cross-space structure alignment (CSA), which imposes view-label hybrid-driven losses on both original and complete feature spaces to enforce structural consistency between partial and holistic semantic topologies. Recognizing the suboptimal data reconstruction quality during initial training phases, we propose phase-aware excitation (PAE) to mitigate error accumulation in early-stage learning. Additionally, we introduce star-shaped interactive sharing module (SIS) that facilitates efficient cross-view information exchange while leveraging view complementarity to ensure consistent and robust feature aggregation, circumventing conflicts with view-consistency alignment objectives. d on five widely-adopted benchmark datasets indicate that the proposed VLCSA framework outperforms numerous established baselines in terms of comprehensive evaluation metrics.
   - Full-text evidence: Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37:114 https://doi.org/10.1007/s44443-025-00130-2 ORIGINAL PAPER View-label driven cross-space structure alignment for incomplete multi-view partial multi-label classiﬁcation Shenrun Ding1 · Jiarui Chen1 · Tongwei Gao1 · Yinghao Ye1 · Xiaohuan Lu1 Received: 16 April 2025 / Accepted: 20 June 2025 / Published online: 17 July 2025 © The Author(s) 2025 Abstract Despite signiﬁcant advancements in multi-view multi-label learning driven by its broad applicability, real-world scenarios frequently suffer from dual incompleteness in both view and label spaces due to data acquisition uncertainties. The incom- pleteness of multi-view features degrades the comprehensiveness of sample representations, leading to failure in capturing semantically discriminative patterns essential for classiﬁcation. To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose a novel learning framework termed View-Label Driven Cross-Space Structure Alignment Network (VLCSA). Departing from existing low-quality view completion approaches, we devise a view-label hybrid-driven autoencoder (VLAE) that extracts discriminative view-speciﬁc features through joint optimization of cross-view semantic consistency and label-guided instance- level embeddings, which allows for accurate reconstruction of missing views. Furthermore, we propose cross-space structure alignment (CSA), which imposes view-label hybrid-driven losses on both original and complete feature spaces to enforce structural consistency between partial and holistic semantic topologies. Recognizing the suboptimal data reconstruction qual- ity during initial training phases, we propose phase-aware excitation (PAE) to mitigate error accumulation in early-stage learning. Additionally, we introduce star-shaped interactive sharing module (SIS) that facilitates efﬁcient cross-view infor- mation exchange while leveraging view complementarity to ensure consistent and robust feature aggregation, circumventing conﬂicts with view-consistency alignment objectives. d on ﬁve widely-adopted benchmark datasets indicate that the proposed VLCSA framework outperforms numerous established baselines in terms of comprehensive evaluation metrics. Keywords Multi-view multi-label classiﬁcation · Incomplete multi-view learning · Partial multi-label classiﬁcation 1 Introduction The revolutionary breakthroughs in data acquisition tech- nologies have triggered an exponential growth of multi- source heterogeneous data. Traditional single-view analysis methods, constrained by their reliance on unitary data rep- resentation, increasingly fail to meet the demand for com- prehensive understanding of observed targets (Yuan et al. 2021; Fang et al. 2021). From multi-modal medical diagnos- tics to multi-sensor perception in autonomous driving, from cross-platform social media content to multi-dimensional industrial inspection features, complementary information captured through diverse views now provides unprecedented opportunities for comprehensively understanding complex B Xiaohuan Lu xhlu3@gzu.edu.cn 1 College of Big Data and Information Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang, China objects (Li et al. 2021; Wang et al. 2022). For instance, in medical diagnosis, cross-modal data comprising X-ray, CT, and MRI scans provides multi-scale features of lesions. In autonomous driving systems, heterogeneous sensor data from cameras, LiDAR, and radar constructs environmental awareness through 2D vision, 3D point clouds, and motion ranging respectively (Thakur and Mishra 2024). Such multi- view data forms complementary representation sets of the same object through diverse acquisition sources or feature extraction methods, whose effective integration has become crucial for breaking through the limitations of single-source information(Liuetal.2022;Huetal.2021;Wenetal.2020a). Concurrently, the semantic complexity of real-world sce- narios reveals the growing limitations of single-label anno- tation systems. In urban scene understanding, an image may simultaneously contain co-occurring labels like “pedestri- ans”, “trafﬁc lights”, and “congested roads”. For multimedia content analysis, video clips require joint annotation of textual, auditory, and visual multimodal semantics (Chen 123 114 Page 2 of 20 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 et al. 2022; Yu et al. 2024). Multi-label systems not only expand the dimensionality of categorical feature spaces but also uncover deep semantic correlations through label co- occurrence relationship modeling (Wang et al. 2025; Wen et al. 2022). However, conventional multi-label classiﬁca- tion methods predominantly rely on single-view features, struggling to address the dual challenges of inter-view repre- sentation discrepancies and complex label correlations. This has motivated researchers to integrate multi-view learning with multi-label classiﬁcation, giving rise to the interdis- ciplinary ﬁeld of Multi-View Multi-Label Classiﬁcation (MVMLC). Its core objective lies in constructing a uniﬁed representation learning framework that achieves synergistic optimization of cross-view feature alignment and multi- label relationship modeling. Recent years have witnessed signiﬁcant advancements in Multi-View Multi-Label Classi- ﬁcation(MVMLC)research.Latentsubspace-basedmethods demonstrate distinctive merits, exempliﬁed by the LCBM modelthatconstructssharedlatentsubspacestojointlymodel multi-view features and label dependencies, effectively cap- turing cross-view semantic correlations (Sun and Zong 2020). Deep learning approaches break through traditional framework limitations, such as the SIMM network that inno- vatively integrates adversarial loss with label supervision to extract view-shared features while preserving view-speciﬁc details through parameter regularization (Wu et al. 2019). Ensemble learning strategies also achieve breakthroughs, including Zhang et al.’s LSA-MML that aligns multi-view latent semantics via kernel space matrix factorization (Zhang et al. 2018). Nevertheless, these methods typically assume complete availability of all views and labels, overlooking critical real-world challenges. Due to data collection complexities and annotation costs, real-world datasets often exhibit dual incompleteness: missing multi-view features and partially annotated labels (Liu et al. 2024a). For instance, autonomous driving systems may capture only camera data while lacking LiDAR point clouds (incomplete views), with street-view images annotated for “vehicles” but missing “pedestrian” labels (incomplete labels). In medical diagnostics, patients might have CT scans available but lack MRI imaging (view absence), while pathology reports provide only partial anno- tations for comorbid conditions (label absence). This preva- lent dual incompleteness poses severe challenges to existing MVMLC models, highlighting the urgent need for devel- oping novel frameworks targeting Incomplete Multi-View Partial Multi-Label Classiﬁcation (IMVPMLC). Tan et al.’s feature decomposition model maps heterogeneous features into a latent shared space linked with label matrices (Tan et al. 2018), while Zhao et al.’s CDMM achieves robust modeling in incomplete scenarios via consistency-diversity neural net- works (Zhao et al. 2021). Deep learning approaches further transcend traditional frameworks: The framework introduced by Liu et al. (DICNet) addresses the dual-incomplete classiﬁ- cation problem by leveraging contrastive learning to enhance cross-view feature consistency (Liu et al. 2023a). Xie et al.’s UPDGD-Net incorporates uncertainty-aware pseudo- labels with dual-graph constraints to preserve multi-view structures (Xie et al. 2024), and the approach proposed by Liu et al. (Masked Two-channel Decoupling framework, MTD) decouples features into shared and view-proprietary features (Liu et al. 2024b). Nevertheless, existing methods exhibit notable limitations. Most models rely on prede- ﬁned missing-view indicator matrices, limiting inference to observed data while neglecting deep exploration of latent cross-view semantic relationships. To address these challenges, we propose a novel learning framework called view label-driven cross-spatial structure alignment network (VLCSA), which is speciﬁcally designed to improve the efﬁciency of multi-view multi-label classiﬁ- cation in the presence of incomplete data. Traditional view completion methods may not be able to effectively capture semantic information, resulting in semantic inconsistency or weak discriminability of the completed data. A mechanism is needed to ensure that the extracted features have strong semantic discriminability and maintain cross-view seman- tic consistency while completing the data. Therefore, our framework introduces a view-label hybrid-driven autoen- coder (VLAE). Different from traditional low-quality view completion methods, VLAE promotes the extraction of view semantic features through a joint optimization process to effectively reconstruct the missing view data. Blindly align- ing the semantic features across different views may lead to high-quality views learning from low-quality ones. To address this issue, the view-label hybrid-drive incorporates label-guided information embedding during the alignment of semantic features, ensuring the preservation of seman- tic consistency across views under the guidance of category information. This effectively mitigates semantic drift in the feature extraction process, which refers to the progressive divergenceoflearnedembeddingsfromtheirinherentseman- tic prototypes. It is worth noting that it is still unknown whether the complete feature space reconstructed by VLAE can maintain the intrinsic semantic structure of the orig- inal data. In order to ensure that the completion process itself does not destroy the semantic coherence, a mechanism is needed to ensure the consistency of the local (original space) and global (complete space) semantic topological structures. According to the structural consistency hypoth- esis, the semantic neighbor relationship of a sample in the original (partial view) space should be roughly consistent with its neighbor relationship in the reconstructed complete space. This reﬂects that the manifold structure inherent in the data should be continuous and undistorted before and after completion. Therefore, VLCSA also adopts a cross- space structural alignment (CSA) mechanism to apply the 123 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 Page 3 of 20 114 view-label hybrid-drive to the original feature space and the reconstructed complete feature space. This method ensures the structural consistency between partial and overall seman- tic topologies and semantic coherence during the completion process, effectively bridging the semantic gap caused by missing data. Recognizing that the initial stage of train- ing is often plagued by suboptimal data quality, we further introduce phase-aware cosine excitation (PAE) as a strategy to alleviate the accumulation of errors in the early learning stage. This mechanism acts as a corrective measure to adjust the learning process to produce better results as training pro- gresses. Common view fusion methods dynamically adjust the weight of each view in the ﬁnal fusion according to its quality, which implicitly acknowledges that the view seman- tic information is unequal in importance, which conﬂicts with the view semantic consistency assumption. This makes it difﬁcult for the model to learn a truly consistent cross- view representation, and requires a sophisticated design of the fusion weight acquisition method. Now there is an urgent need for a more direct and simpler fusion mechanism that can effectively utilize the complementary information between views, naturally maintain the view semantic consistency assumption, and avoid complex weight calculations. There- fore, we also design a star-shaped interactive sharing module (SIS). This module ﬁrst splices the features of each view into a shared star topological space as a hub for cross-view interaction. Then, a lightweight depth-wise separable convo- lution is used to stably enhance the local feature associations between views. In order to deeply explore complementarity and avoid semantic deviations caused by a single path, SIS innovatively introduces a dual-branch feature modulation mechanism, which generates two feature streams through high-dimensionalprojectionandchanneldecomposition,and then achieves ﬂexible fusion through star operation. Finally, SIS naturally aggregates a robust representation that fully integrates the complementary information of multiple views and strictly maintains semantic consistency by performing a simple cross-view summation of the modulated view fea- tures. In summary, the key contributions of our proposed VLCSA model are as follows: • We design a view-label hybrid-driven autoencoder (VLAE)thatsynergisticallyintegratescross-viewseman- tic consistency preservation and label-guided instance embedding. This architecture extracts discriminative fea- tures from raw data while effectively reconstructing missing view data through collaborative optimization. • We propose cross-space structure alignment (CSA), which applies a view-label hybrid-driven loss in both original and complete feature spaces to achieve guided alignment of local and global semantic structures. Addi- tionally, we develop phase-aware excitation (PAE) to reduce the excessive focus on low-quality reconstruction data at the beginning of training. • We design a star-shaped interactive sharing module (SIS) that enables efﬁcient cross-view information interac- tion while leveraging the complementary relationship between views to ensure consistent and robust feature aggregation. Extensive experiments have conﬁrmed the advancement and robustness of VLCSA. 2 Related works Considering the strong relationship between our target task and the ﬁelds of multi-label classiﬁcation and multi-view learning, this section will systematically present pertinent research, focusing on multi-view multi-label classiﬁcation (MvMlC) and incomplete multi-view partial multi-label clas- siﬁcation (IMvPMlC). 2.1 Multi-view multi-label classification Multi-view learning aims to utilize multiple information sources or diverse representations (views) of data to enhance learning performance. This approach can be applied along- side various downstream tasks. Xu et al. presented the deep multi-view clustering method called MFLVC, utiliz- ing classic contrastive learning to aggregate features from various views of the same sample while distancing different samples at the instance level (Xu et al. 2022). Multi-label classiﬁcation has become a vibrant area of research within machine learning, garnering considerable attention and inter- est. Ma and Chen proposed DM2L for multi-label learning with incomplete labels, applying low-rank constraints on same-label instances and high-rank constraints on different- label instances, utilizing kernel tricks for non-linearity, and employing a concave-convex objective (Ma and Chen 2021). Multi-view multi-label classiﬁcation (MvMlC) integrates multi-view learning and multi-label classiﬁcation, requiring the simultaneous handling of multi-view multi-label data, which consequently adds to the complexity of the problem. LVSL is an intricate method catering to non-aligned MvMLC tasks that employs Laplacian graph regularization derived from the feature space during the learning of view-speciﬁc labels, thus preserving the original data’s geometric struc- ture. Additionally, it imposes a low-rank constraint on the learnable label correlation matrix to consider the local label low-rank structure (Zhao et al. 2022). 2.2 Incomplete multi-view multi-label classification Recent multi-view multi-label classiﬁcation (MvMLC) stud- ies typically presume complete data, yet real-world scenarios 123 114 Page 4 of 20 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 often exhibit dual incompleteness with missing views and labels.Thismismatchbetweentheoreticalpremisesandprac- tical requirements creates unresolved challenges. To handle view absence, Liu et al. developed Recformer (Liu et al. 2023b), an autoencoder for view reconstruction through cross-view information fusion. For label incompleteness, GLOCAL (Zhu et al. 2017) eliminates prior label correla- tions by directly learning Laplacian matrices, employing a masking mechanism to suppress missing-label noise. Despite progress in addressing individual incompleteness aspects, current methods predominantly focus on isolated scenarios. Current frameworks lack effective solutions for the simulta- neous absence of views and labels, a widespread but under- studied issue in practical MvMLC scenarios. Notably, Li and Chen proposed NAIM3L, which addresses the challenges of missing labels and incomplete views by exploiting the global high-rank and local low-rank structure of multi-label data and achieves efﬁcient classiﬁcation (Li and Chen 2021). Additionally, Long et al.’s Multi-Scale Locality Preserving Projection (MSLPP) enhances feature discriminability from a manifold learning perspective (Long et al. 2024). Liu et al. proposed the Reliable representAtion learNing networK (RANK), which introduces a quality-aware sub-network to dynamically evaluate the quality of each view for each sam- ple and adaptively assign weights for multi-view fusion, thereby improving the robustness of incomplete multi-view classiﬁcation (Liu et al. 2025). Although existing methods partially mitigate data incom- pleteness through predeﬁned missing-view indicator matri- ces, they remain fundamentally constrained by passive strategies. Over-reliance on observable data while neglect- ing latent cross-view semantic correlations leads to semantic consistency breakdowns during feature reconstruction. To address these limitations, we propose the View-Label driven Cross-space Structure Alignment network (VLCSA), which transcends traditional passive paradigms through a view- label collaborative driving mechanism for active semantic reconstruction of missing data. 3 The proposed method ThissectionestablishesaformaldeﬁnitionfortheIncomplete Multi-View Partial Multi-Label Classiﬁcation (IMvPMlC) problem. Building upon this foundation, we present a four-component methodological framework comprising: (1) View-Label Hybrid-Driven Autoencoder, (2) Cross-Space Structure Alignment, (3) Star-Shaped Interactive Sharing, and (4) Incomplete Multi-Label Classiﬁcation. The frame- work of VLCSA is shown in Fig. 1. 3.1 Problem formulation and main notations We consider a multi-view dataset with n samples observed from m views. The data is represented as matrices {X(v) ∈ Rn×dv}m v=1, where X(v) is the feature matrix for the v-th view and dv is the feature dimension of the v-th view. Each sample i has feature vectors {X(1) i , X(2) i , . . . , X(m) i }. The label matrix Y ∈{0, 1}n×l indicates the category membership for each sample, where l is the number of categories. Speciﬁcally, Yi, j = 1 means the i-th sample belongs to the j-th category, and Yi, j = 0 means it does not. To handle missing data, we introduce two binary matrices: the missing-view indicator F ∈{0, 1}n×m and the missing-label indicator C ∈{0, 1}n×l. Fi, j = 1 means the j-th view of the i-th sample is available, while Ci, j = 1 means the j-th label for the i-th sample is available. In preprocessing, missing views in {X(v)}m v=1 and missing labels in Y are set to 0 for consistency. The goal is to train a model for incomplete multi-view partial multi-label classiﬁcation (IMvPMlC) to predict the complete label set for each sample, even when some views and labels are missing. The following sections detail the approach and model. For clarity, the key symbols used in this paper are summarized in Table 1. 3.2 View-label hybrid-driven autoencoder The intrinsic challenge of incomplete multi-view data lies in the fact that the defective observation space induces models to learn biased joint distributions, thereby compromising the generalization capability for downstream tasks (Liu et al. 2023b). A fundamental solution to this problem suggests that proper completion of missing views could potentially enhance model performance. However, conventional view completion approaches relying solely on inter-view correla- tions face critical limitations. These single-driven paradigms that depend exclusively on view-wise semantic consistency tend to fall into semantic drift dilemmas, where reconstructed features deviate from the core discriminative patterns crucial for classiﬁcation tasks, due to the absence of semantic super- vision from label systems (Wen et al. 2023a). Inspired by He et al. (2022); Liu et al. (2023c), we propose a View-Label Hybrid-Driven Autoencoder (VLAE) for missing view com- pletion. Our framework achieves dual objectives through two innovative components: 1) A multi-view aggregation loss that explicitly coordinates inter-view interactions to main- tain cross-view semantic consistency, and 2) A label-guided instance-level embedding alignment mechanism that ensures discriminative feature learning. This dual-driven architec- ture effectively bridges the gap between data reconstruction and task-oriented discrimination by synergistically integrat- 123 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 Page 5 of 20 114 FC Mask Cross-Space Structure Alignment View-Label Hybrid-Driven Autoencoder L FC Star-Shaped Interacve Sharing Y C CLS- LOSS Incomplete Space Mask Complete data Complete Encoder Shape: Sample Color : View LDL-LOSS MVA-LOSS Complete Space Incomplete Encoder PAE-LOSS Incomplete fusion Reconstructed data interpolaon R D Addive Fusion Incomplete Decoder Classiﬁer DropPath C:Missing-Label Indicator R:Predicon Label Matrix Y:Real Lable D DwConv1D X 1 X 2 X m D m D 2 D 1 X 1 X 2 X m    RE-LOSS Reconstructed data Mul-view data Star operaon Star operaon L LayerNorm FC Linear Funcon FC Linear Funcon Missing shield Missing-View Indicator Fig. 1 The framework of VLCSA begins with the input of multi-view data into a view-label hybrid-driven autoencoder to reconstruct the missing view data. Cross-space structural alignment is then carried out in both the incomplete and complete spaces. Next, star-shaped interactive sharing is performed for view fusion, and ultimately, the classiﬁcation result is produced through a classiﬁer Table 1 Main notations n the number of all samples m the number of all views l the number of all labels dv the dimension of the v-th view de embedding dimension X(v) original data of view v (Rn×dv) G(v) masking matrix (Rn×dv) M(v) masked data of view v (Rn×dv) E(v) embedding feature of view v (Rn×de) H label similarity matrix ({0, 1}n×n) Q(v) feature similarity matrix of view v ({0, 1}n×n) Z fusion feature of incomplete data (Rn×de) D(v) reconstructed data of view v (Rn×de) ¯X(v) complete data of view v (Rn×dv) ¯M(v) masked data of complete view v (Rn×dv) ¯E(v) embedding feature of complete view v (Rn×de) ¯Q(v) feature similarity matrix of complete view v ({0, 1}n×n) S stacking feature (Rn×m×de) P parameterized projection matrix ¯Z fusion feature of complete data (Rn×de) R prediction label matrix ({0, 1}n×l) Y real label matrix ({0, 1}n×l) F missing-view indicator ({0, 1}n×m) C missing-label indicator ({0, 1}n×l) ./ element-wise division ∥·∥2 vector’s l2 norm 123 114 Page 6 of 20 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 ing view-speciﬁc characteristics with supervised semantic constraints. Figure 2 shows the view-label hybrid-driven approach in detail. Random mask strategy To mitigate the adverse effects of excessive information redundancy in raw multi-view repre- sentations during feature learning, we propose a stochastic masking approach inspired by He et al. (2022). For each view v, we deﬁne a binary mask G(v) ∈{0, 1}n×dv initialized with all ones. The masking operation proceeds as: (1) For instance i in view v, randomly select u = dv · β feature indices from [1, dv]; (2) Set corresponding positions in G(v) to 0. The ﬁnal masked data is obtained via:  M(v)m v=1 =  X(v) ⊙G(v)m v=1 (1) where ⊙denotes element-wise multiplication. This stochas- tic feature suppression mechanism provides dual beneﬁts: it eliminates redundant feature dimensions while maintaining holistic information integrity through partial feature preser- vation. The hyperparameter β controls the masking intensity, with higher values inducing stronger regularization against feature redundancy. View-speciﬁcinformationembedding Toaddressthechallenges of heterogeneous multi-view data processing, we develop dedicated deep autoencoder architectures for individual views. Each network follows an encoder-decoder paradigm where the encoder component generates view-speciﬁc latent embeddings while the decoder reconstructs the original view from latent representations. Despite employing conven- tional multi-layer perceptron (MLP) structures, our design Fig. 2 This is the framework diagram of the view-label hybrid-driven. The missing label matrix is obtained by the missing label indicator and the real label, and the sample similarity matrix is obtained by transposing and multiplying itself. It serves as a semantic anchor to guide the incomplete space and the complete space to achieve cross-space structural alignment. The left side of the ﬁgure is a single calculation example, and the right side is the entire label similarity matrix as a semantic anchor to guide cross-view consistency learning 123 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 Page 7 of 20 114 fulﬁlls two critical objectives: (i) preserving distinctive view- speciﬁc semantic patterns in the embedding space, and (ii) establishing dimensional uniformity across heterogeneous views to enable coherent cross-modal analysis. The feature embedding process is formally deﬁned as:  (v) : M(v) ∈Rn×dv →E(v) ∈Rn×de m v=1 (2) where E(v) denotes the transformed embeddings with uniﬁed dimensionality de, generated through parametric projection in MLP layers. This architecture achieves dual objectives: maintainingtheﬁdelityofview-inherentinformationthrough non-linear encoding while ensuring cross-view analyzability via dimensionally harmonized representations. View-label hybrid-driven To enhance the discriminative power and effectiveness of multi-view embeddings for sub- sequent recovery and classiﬁcation tasks, we impose con- straints on the feature extraction process through two princi- pal mechanisms: view-wise deep semantic consistency and label-guided instance embedding. The fundamental assump- tion of multi-view semantic consistency posits that while different views may originate from distinct modalities or rep- resentation formats, they should maintain coherent semantic information when describing the same object or concept. This consistency motivates our approach to minimize the distance between any two view-speciﬁc embeddings of the same instance in the latent space (Xu et al. 2022; Trosten et al. 2021). We formulate the multi-view aggregation objective as follows: Lmva = 1 de m  u=1 m  v=1 v̸=u  n  i=1 I[Fi,uFi,v=1] · (E(u) i ) −(E(v) i )  2 2 n k=1 I[Fk,uFk,v=1]  (3) E(u) i ∈ Rn×de represents the embedding of the i- th instance from the u-th view. (·) denotes the ℓ2- normalization function and I[·] is the indicator function that outputs 1 when both views exist simultaneously (i.e., Fi,uFi,v = 1), and 0 otherwise. The indicator function I[·] serves as a masking operator that selectively activates the loss computation only for available view pairs, thereby nat- urally handling missing views in the input data. While cross-view alignment of deep semantic features is theoretically justiﬁed and essential for effective multi-view learning, conventional aggregation approaches often neglect genuine semantic supervision. This oversight can lead to semantic drift - a detrimental phenomenon where feature embeddings progressively deviate from their original seman- tic cores during the fusion process. This deviation manifests as the gradual erosion of class-discriminative information, propagation of view-speciﬁc biases across modalities, and degraded generalization capability in downstream tasks. To address this critical challenge, we introduce semantic anchor as fusion centers that explicitly guide cross-view alignment toward the authentic semantic structure inherent in label information. Speciﬁcally, we construct a label-driven seman- tic structure-preserving prior graph to enforce consistency between the sample geometry in the embedding space and the ground-truth semantic structure in the label space. For- mally,thelabelsimilaritymatrixH ∈[0, 1]n×n,whichserves as our semantic structure-preserving prior: H = (Y ⊙C)(Y ⊙C)T./(CCT) (4) where ./ denotes element-wise division and the missing- label indicator C serves dual purposes: mitigating the impact of incomplete labels while normalizing similarity values. Speciﬁcally, CCTcomputes the co-available label counts between sample pairs (i, j), establishing an adaptive nor- malization basis. The label manifold hypothesis (Wu et al. 2014) suggests that sample similarity implies label simi- larity. Conversely, we exploit this relationship inversely by constructing sample-level prior graphs from label similari- ties. These graphs serve as semantic anchors to guide the alignment between the geometric structures in each view’s embedding space and the true semantic structure. For each view v, we compute pairwise feature similarities using cosine similarity measure (·), generating a global similarity graph Q(v) ∈[0, 1]n×n: Q(v) i j = (E(v) i , E(v) j ) + 1 /2 =  E(v) i · E(v) j E(v) i  2 E(v) j  2 + 1  /2 (5) where ⟨·⟩denote the vector dot product operation. We derive global similarity graphs {Q(v)}m v=1 for m views. The label- driven semantic structure-preserving prior graph H serves as semantic anchors to align geometric structures across views through cross-entropy minimization: Lldl = −1 2m m  v=1  n  i=1 n  j=1 j̸=i I[Fi,vF j,v=1] · Hi j log Q(v) i j + (1 −Hi j) log(1 −Q(v) i j )  n q=1 n k=1 k̸=q I[Fq,vFk,v=1]  (6) 123 114 Page 8 of 20 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 The indicator function I[·] outputs 1 when two samples, i and j, simultaneously exist in the view v (i.e., Fi,vF j,v = 1), and outputs 0 otherwise. The function I[·] is also employed to compute the number of available sample pairs within each view. Blindly aligning views may lead to the propa- gation of high-quality views learning from low-quality ones, as there is no guarantee that the aggregation center corre- sponds to the true semantic center. Instead, we introduce a label-driven semantic structure graph as semantic anchors, incorporating real semantic information during the aggre- gation of multi-view semantic consistency. This allows for the semantic alignment across views. Hence, our view-label hybrid-driven loss is formulated as: Lvld = Lmva + Lldl (7) Missing view reconstruction In order to fully integrate the complementary information from multiple views in the pres- ence of missing data, and to maximize the potential of multi-view data, a simple yet efﬁcient approach is adopted (Wen et al. 2020b, 2023b; Yan et al. 2023). This approach involves using a missing-view matrix F for weighted multi- view fusion to obtain a representative and comprehensive fused representation. Speciﬁcally, for the fused representa- tion of the i-th sample, Zi, we have: Zi = m  v=1 E(v) i Fi,v m v′=1 Fi,v′ (8) Through this weighted fusion process, we are able to inte- grate the complementary information from all views into a uniﬁed space, thereby obtaining a comprehensive represen- tation of the sample. To address the challenges posed by missing views, and further enhance the utility of Zi, we introduce a symmet- ric cross-view decoder module. This decoder learns shared knowledge across views and aims to reconstruct information for all views. Speciﬁcally, we reconstruct the features of the v-th view using the following mapping process:  (v) : Z ∈Rn×de →D(v) ∈Rn×dv m v=1 (9) where D(v) ∈Rn×dv is the reconstructed feature for the v-th view. Although fully recovering the missing information is a challenging task, the model can still generate reconstruc- tions that closely approximate the real data by leveraging the cross-view similarities and complementarities contained in the fused representation Z. Since we cannot directly obtain a supervisory signal for the reconstruction accuracy, we intro- duce a weighted reconstruction loss function as a constraint (Wen et al. 2021). This indirectly guides the model to focus on the reconstruction process for the missing views. Speciﬁ- cally, the weighted reconstruction loss function is deﬁned as: Lre = 1 m m  v=1  1 dvn n  i=1 E(v) i −D(v) i  2 2 Fi,v  (10) where D(v) i is the i-th sample’s reconstructed instance for the v-th view. By minimizing this loss, we can effectively constrain the model to preserve the integrity and complemen- tarity of key information and recover the missing information in the presence of missing views, thus facilitating effective feature learning (Liu et al. 2023b). Finally, by utilizing both the original missing data  X(v)m v=1 and the decoded reconstructed data  D(v)m v=1, we can obtain the complete view data  ¯X(v)m v=1:  ¯X(v)m v=1 =  X(v) + D(v) ⊙  1 −F(v) m v=1 (11) Thus, we can better exploit the cross-view consensus semantics and complementary information from the com- plete view data for accurate classiﬁcation. 3.3 Cross-space structure alignment Due to the inherent requirement for geometric consistency between missing local structures and complete semantic topologies, direct feature space mapping often results in semantic discontinuity between partial and holistic repre- sentations (Yang et al. 2016). To address this, we propose Cross-Space Structural Alignment (CSA) that enforces topo- logical isomorphism between incomplete features and com- plete feature spaces through a view-label hybrid-driven loss mechanism. This approach effectively maintains the conti- nuity of semantic manifolds under geometrically incomplete conditions by establishing cross-space structural constraints. Mask and feature embedding Despite having access to the complete view data, there are still two concerns before for- mal processing. First, the original multi-view data contains a signiﬁcant amount of redundant information. Second, the inherent feature redundancy in the raw data may be further ampliﬁed due to missing view recovery. Therefore, we con- sider leveraging random masks for feature suppression:  ¯M(v)m v=1 =  ¯X(v) ⊙¯G(v)m v=1 (12) where ¯M(v) is the complete view data after masking. The initial mask recovery process forces the model to infer the global structure of the views from the known parts (the shared semantic features across views). The second mask, applied at different locations, introduces local perturbations to the 123 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 Page 9 of 20 114 same view, compelling the model to further learn the local invariance of the view, thus preventing overﬁtting to speciﬁc patterns. Next, an encoder component for the complete view data is employed to extract the view-speciﬁc deep semantic information and construct heterogeneous view embedding dimension consistency:  ¯ (v) : ¯M(v) ∈Rn×dv →¯E(v) ∈Rn×de m v=1 (13) Cross-space alignment Next, we detail the Cross-Space Structure Alignment (CSA) mechanism to maintain the con- sistency of both local and global semantic topology. Similar to incomplete space, we drive alignment through a view-label hybrid-driven strategy in the complete space. By introducing semantic anchor points as aggregation centers, we aim to pre- serve deep semantic consistency across different views. First, we minimize the distance between any two view embed- dings of the same sample in the complete space by employing multi-view embedding aggregation: L′mva = 1 de m  u=1 m  v=1 v̸=u  1 n n  i=1 ( ¯E(u) i ) −( ¯E(v) i )  2 2  (14) There is no need to consider the masking of missing views, as all view pairs participate in the calculation in the complete space. Moreover, the label-driven semantic structure preserving prior graph remains crucial in the complete space, as it serves as a semantic anchor to guide consistency in the geometric structure across different views. Speciﬁcally, we continue to use the label similarity matrix H as a prior graph to align the geometry of each view’s embedding space with the true semantic structure in the label space. In the complete space, we recalculate pairwise feature similarities and generate the global similarity graph ¯Q(v) i j ∈[0, 1]n×n: ¯Q(v) i j = ( ¯E(v) i , ¯E(v) j ) + 1 /2 =  ¯E(v) i · ¯E(v) j  ¯E(v) i  2  ¯E(v) j  2 + 1  /2 (15) Through cross-entropy loss, the label-driven loss in the complete space is deﬁned as follows: L′ldl = −1 2m m  v=1  1 n(n −1) n  i=1 n  j=1 j̸=i Hi j log ¯Q(v) i j +(1 −Hi j) log(1 −¯Q(v) i j )  (16) Unlike the missing view handling in incomplete space, all views are available in the complete space, which allows us to fully leverage the feature information from each view for more comprehensive modeling. The view-label hybrid- driven loss is then deﬁned as: L′vld = L′mva + L′ldl (17) Phase-aware excitation Traditional homogeneity optimiza- tion strategies tend to amplify initial biases, as the network, due to its limited reconstruction capacity in the early stages of training, often generates low-quality features (Ben Yed- der et al. 2021). If excessive constraints are applied to these features prematurely, it can lead to the accumulation of erroneous gradients and misguide subsequent optimization directions.Tomitigatethisissue,weproposethephase-aware excitation (PAE), which dynamically adjusts the loss weight using a phase-aware cosine excitation function. This func- tion initially reduces the focus on unreliable reconstructed features and progressively increases the supervision strength as the training advances: Lpae =  1 −cos  π · t T  /2  · L′vld (18) The cosine term  1 −cos  π · t T  /2  approaches 0 in early training stages (t ≪T ), which signiﬁcantly weakens the strong constraints on low-quality reconstructed features topreventerroneousgradientsfromdominatingtheoptimiza- tion direction. As training progresses (t →T ), the weight coefﬁcientsmoothlyincreasesto1,graduallyintensifyingthe supervision strength for semantic structural alignment. This adjustment strategy enables the model to prioritize learning stable foundational feature representations before progres- sively reﬁning the semantic topological alignment. 3.4 Star-shaped interactive sharing The core challenge of multi-view feature fusion lies in how to balance the complementarity between views and maintain semantic consistency. Approaches that dynamically adjust the fusion weights based on view quality may, to some extent, violate the semantic consistency assumption between views. Moreover, the process of determining fusion weights requires intricate and complex design. Inspired by Ma et al. (2024), this paper proposes the star-shaped interactive shar- ing (SIS) module, which achieves the following objectives through structured feature interaction and lightweight oper- 123 114 Page 10 of 20 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 ations: 1) Establish explicit information exchange channels across views; 2) Enhance feature robustness by leveraging view complementarity; 3) Avoid conﬂicts with semantic con- sistency objectives. First, we concatenate the feature tensors of m different views, denoted as { ¯E(v)}m v=1 ∈Rn×de, along the view dimension to form a shared feature space in a star-topology. The concatenation operation is denoted by ϒ, which combines the individual feature tensors. This results in the following shared feature tensor S: S ∈Rn×m×de = ϒ ¯E(1), ¯E(2), . . . , ¯E(m) (19) Here, each tensor ¯E(v) corresponds to the feature represen- tation of the v-th view, and the resulting tensor S aggregates features from all m views in a single shared space. Next, we apply depthwise separable convolutions to enhance the local feature relationships across different views (Chollet 2017). This step uses a series of depthwise convolutions to extract meaningful local patterns in the shared feature space. Before applying the convolution, we normalize the feature tensor S using Layer Normalization. The LayerNorm operation ensures stability in the cross-view interactions by normalizing each feature across the feature dimensions. The resulting enhanced tensor is: ¯S = DwConv1Dk=3 p=1  LayerNorm(S)  (20) To avoid semantic bias due to single-path interactions, a dual-branch feature modulation strategy is proposed. A parameterized projection matrix P1 is used to expand the feature space into higher dimensions, followed by decompo- sition operation along the channel dimension:  P1(¯S) ∈Rn×m×2de  →  A1, A2 ∈Rn×m×de  (21) where A1 and A2 represent the two branches of features obtained by decomposing the projected feature map. Each branch captures different aspects of the feature space, facil- itating better interaction between them. Next, we perform a dual-branch feature modulation via a gating mechanism. Speciﬁcally, the features from A1 are passed through a non- linear activation function, (GELU activation), and then element-wise multiplied ⊙(star operation) with the features from A2. The result is further processed by a learnable pro- jection matrix P2: A = P2 [ (A1) ⊙A2] (22) This operation modulates the interaction between the fea- tures, dynamically adjusting the contribution of each branch based on the learned gating mechanism. To stabilize training, we introduce a learnable layer scaling factor γ and incorpo- rate DropPath regularization. The DropPath technique helps Algorithm 1 Training process of VLCSA. Input: Incomplete multi-view data {X(v)}m v=1, missing-view indica- tor F ∈{0, 1}n×m, partial multi-label data Y ∈Rn×l, missing-label indicator C ∈{0, 1}n×l. Initialization: Randomly initialize model parameters and set hyper- parameters ( α, β, learning rate, batch size, and training epochs T ). for t = 1 to T do 1. Compute masked data {M(v)}m v=1 using (1) and compute feature embedding {E(v)}m v=1 using (2). 2. Compute the view-label hybrid-driven loss Lvld using (3) and (6). 3. Compute the fused features of incomplete data Z using (8), and reconstruct the features {D(v)}m v=1 using (9). 4. Compute reconstruction loss Lre using (10) and obtain complete view data { ¯X(v)}m v=1 using (11). 5. Mask the complete data and perform feature embedding using (12) and (13). 6. Compute the view-label hybrid-driven loss in the complete fea- ture space using (14) and (16), and mitigate early learning error accumulation by computing Lpae using (18). 7. Perform view information interaction and sharing in the star topology space using (20) and (22). 8. Compute the fused feature ¯Z of the complete data using (24). 9. Obtain the ﬁnal prediction R using the classiﬁer ψ and compute the classiﬁcation loss Lcls using (25). 10. Compute the total loss Lall using (26) and update the network parameters. end for Output: Trained VLCSA model. regularize the model during training by randomly dropping paths through the network. The ﬁnal output ¯A is computed as: ¯A = S + DropPath(γ ⊙A) (23) In this equation, S represents the original input, and the DropPath operation allows for a smoother and more sta- ble training process, particularly during the early stages of training. This approach ensures that the network gradually increases the strength of feature interaction, improving over- all optimization stability. Finally, robust and comprehensive feature representations are generated by summing the outputs across all views: ¯Zi = m  v=1 ¯A(v) i (24) This summation process consolidates information from multiple views, ensuring that the ﬁnal output ¯Zi beneﬁts from multi-granularity information complementarity, while maintaining semantic consistency across the different views. By utilizing this star-interaction topology, the SIS module enables effective multi-view feature fusion, while maintain- ing semantic consistency across the various branches and stages of the network. 123 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 Page 11 of 20 114 3.5 Incomplete multi-label classification After obtaining a uniﬁed representation during the feature fusion phase, we design a streamlined classiﬁcation mod- ule to map the feature space to the label space. Speciﬁcally, the classiﬁer’s architecture consists of a two-layer fully con- nected network combined with activation functions such as Sigmoid. The fully connected layers are responsible for computing the discrimination scores of each sample across different categories, while the Sigmoid function normalizes these scores into probability values within the [0, 1] range. This process can be formally represented as: R = ψ  ¯Z  , where R ∈[0, 1]n×l is the predicted probability matrix, ψ represents the classiﬁer. For the multi-label classiﬁcation task, the baseline model uses binary cross-entropy (BCE) as the loss function (Zheng et al. 2023). This function provides a supervisory signal by computing the difference between the predicted probabilities and the ground truth labels. How- ever, the common issue of label missingness in real-world scenarios makes the traditional BCE unsuitable for direct application. To address this, we introduce a missing label indicator matrix C ∈{0, 1}n×l to construct an improved weighted loss function: Lcls = − 1 n i=1 l j=1 Ci j n  i=1 l j=1 Yi j log  Ri j  +  1 −Yi j  log  1 −Ri j  Ci j (25) where Y is the ground truth label matrix, Ri j is the pre- dicted probability of the i-th sample for the j-th class, and C introduces a dynamic ﬁltering mechanism for the supervi- sory signal. When Ci j = 1, the corresponding label actively participates in the loss computation, while when Ci j = 0, the missing information is ignored. This mechanism maintains the stability of model training and effectively mitigates the negative impact of missing labels. The model’s multi-task loss function incorporates both classiﬁcation supervision and cross-space consistency con- straints. Speciﬁcally, it includes the following components: classiﬁcation loss, missing view reconstruction loss, missing space mixing-driven loss, and complete space mixing-driven loss. These elements work together to collaboratively opti- mize the overall training process. The overall objective function can be formally represented as follows, where α represents the penalty coefﬁcient for the missing view recon- struction loss: Lall = Lcls + αLre + Lvld + Lpae (26) 4 Experiments This section presents a detailed overview of the experimen- tal setup, including dataset speciﬁcations, baseline methods, and comprehensive analysis of the obtained results. The validity of our approach is rigorously examined through extensive benchmarking, ablation experiments, and supple- mentary investigations. 4.1 Experimental settings 4.1.1 Datasets Following established practices in previous works (Tan et al. 2018; Li and Chen 2021; Liu et al. 2023a, 2025), we evaluate our method on ﬁve standard multi-view multi-label bench- mark datasets: Corel5k, Pascal07, ESPGame, IAPRTC12, andMirﬂickr.Forconsistencywithpriorresearch,weemploy six different visual features as distinct views: GIST, HSV, Hue, SIFT, RGB, and LAB. Table 2 summarizes the fun- damental statistics of these datasets, with further details provided below: 1) Corel5k (Duygulu et al. 2002): This widely-used bench- mark collection contains 4,999 images with multi-label annotations, where each image carries 1-5 descriptive tags selected from a vocabulary of 260 possible labels. 2) Pascal07 (Everingham et al. 2010): Comprising 9,963 images across 20 object categories, Pascal07 serves as a standard evaluation resource for multi-label classiﬁca- tion approaches, featuring complex scenes with multiple coexisting objects. 3) ESPGame (Von Ahn and Dabbish 2004): Containing 20,770 images crowdsourced through a collaborative online annotation game, this dataset exhibits an average of 4.69 labels per image (range: 1-15 labels) with 268 unique semantic concepts. Table 2 This study provides a detailed statistical characterization of ﬁve benchmark datasets speciﬁcally constructed for multi-view multi- label learning scenarios Dataset #Sample #Label #View #Label/#Sample Corel5k 4999 260 6 3.40 Pascal07 9963 20 6 1.47 ESPGame 20770 268 6 4.69 IAPRTC12 19627 291 6 5.72 MIRFLICKR 25000 38 6 4.72 123 114 Page 12 of 20 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 4) IAPRTC12 (Escalante et al. 2010): Featuring 19,627 annotated images organized into 291 distinct classes, this collection provides rich textual metadata with up to 23 descriptive tags per image, obtained from accompanying titles or descriptive narratives. 5) Mirﬂickr (Huiskes and Lew 2008): The MIRFLICKR- 25k repository contains 25,000 user-generated images from Flickr, annotated with 38 semantic labels, primarily used for evaluating content-based retrieval and automatic annotation systems. 4.1.2 Incomplete dataset preparation To assess the effectiveness of our method in handling incom- plete data, we manually created a double-missing dataset following similar protocols used in prior works (Tan et al. 2018; Li and Chen 2021; Liu et al. 2023a, 2025). Speciﬁ- cally, we randomly removed 50% of the instances from each view, ensuring that each sample still retains at least one com- plete view to preserve the overall sample size. Additionally, we randomly assigned unknown labels to 50% of both the positive and negative class labels within each category. It is worth noting that missing views in {X(v)}m v=1 and missing labels in Y are set to 0. The purpose of this is to ensure that the multi-view data is dimensionally aligned, which is very important in batch-based training of deep neural net- works. The dataset was subsequently split into three parts: 70% for training, 15% for validation, and 15% for testing. Importantly, all subsets contain incomplete features, includ- ing both validation and test sets. Nevertheless, we preserved the original labels in the validation and test sets to enable accurate performance evaluation. 4.1.3 Comparative methods To evaluate our model’s performance, we conduct compre- hensive comparisons with 10 state-of-the-art approaches: C2AE (Yeh et al. 2017), DM2L (Ma and Chen 2021), LVSL (Zhao et al. 2022), IMVWL (Tan et al. 2018), NAIM3L (Li and Chen 2021), DICNet (Liu et al. 2023a), UPDGD (Xie et al. 2024), MSLPP (Long et al. 2024), MTD (Liu et al. 2024b), and RANK (Liu et al. 2025). Our VLCSA method andthesebaselinesaretestedonﬁvedatasetsdesignedtosim- ulate scenarios with both missing views and missing labels. For single-view multi-label classiﬁcation methods like C2AE and DM2L, we execute the algorithms separately on each view and record the optimal performance. Following established practices (Tan et al. 2018; Li and Chen 2021; Liu et al. 2023a, 2025), we modify approaches not origi- nally equipped for doubly incomplete data. Speciﬁcally, for methods incapable of handling missing views (e.g., C2AE, DM2L, and LVSL), we impute missing views using mean features computed from available views. For techniques that cannot process missing labels (e.g., LVSL), we ﬁll the absent labels with zeros. Table 3 provides a comparative summary of the key features across all evaluated methods. 4.1.4 Evaluation metrics and implementation details Consistent with established practices in IMvPMlC research (Tan et al. 2018; Li and Chen 2021; Liu et al. 2023a, 2024b), our evaluation employs four standard multi-label classiﬁca- tion metrics: ranking loss (RL), hamming loss (HL), average precision (AP), and area under the adaptive curve (AUC). Following conventional reporting standards in the ﬁeld, we present these metrics as “1-RL” and “1-HL” to account for their typically small numerical values. Our assessment frameworkalsoincludestwoadditionalcommonlyusedmea- sures: single error (OE) and coverage (Cov) (Zhang and Zhou 2013; Chen et al. 2019), which we similarly report as “1-OE” and “1-Cov” for consistency. We developed the VLCSA model using PyTorch in Python. The learning rate was maintained at 1 for most datasets, with exceptions for Pascal07 and Mirﬂickr where we applied a reduced rate of 0.1. Across all experiments, we maintained consistent training parameters: a batch size of 128, momentum of 0.9, and stochastic gradient descent (SGD)optimization.AlltrialswereperformedonaWindows- based hardware conﬁguration featuring an RTX 4060 GPU and Ryzen 7 7735H processor. To ensure statistical reliability and mitigate potential biases from random data splitting, we conducted multiple randomized trials and report both mean performance values and their corresponding standard devia- tions. 4.2 Experimental results The experimental results of VLCSA on ﬁve datasets under three scenarios—50% view missing, 50% label missing, and 70% training sample —are summarized in Table 4, where the bottom-right value in each cell indicates the standard deviation. To analyze the impact of varying missing rates, we systematically adjust one missing rate (views or labels) within [0%, 30%, 50%, 70%] while ﬁxing the other at 50%, with results illustrated in Fig. 3. Comparative experiments on complete datasets (without missing data) are also conducted, where all missing indicators are set to 1, as shown in the radar charts of Fig. 4. The principal conclusions from these experiments are: • Table 4 demonstrates VLCSA’s robustness to signiﬁcant data missingness, achieving superior performance across all metrics and datasets. Speciﬁcally, VLCSA surpasses RANK by nearly 2 percentage points in Average Preci- 123 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 Page 13 of 20 114 Table 3 Comparison classiﬁcation methods Method Sources Multi-view Missing-view Missing-label Core Strategy C2AE AAAI’17    Learn a deep latent space to associate feature and label domain data by combining deep canonical correlation analysis (DCCA) and autoencoders. DM2L PR’21    Address the problem of missing labels in multi-label learning by simultaneously modeling global and local low-rank label structures and label discriminative information. LVSL TMM’22    Address the problem of inconsistent labels in non-aligned views by learning view-speciﬁc labels and combining label structure information. iMVWL IJCAI’18    Learn a shared subspace from incomplete views to capture label correlation and weak label information, and learn a robust weak label classiﬁer in this subspace. NAIM3L TPAMI’22    Address the challenges of double missing data and non-aligned views by leveraging multi-view consensus and global and local structures of multi-labels. DICNet AAAI’23    Effectively extract high-level semantic features of samples through deep instance-level contrastive learning to enhance the consistency of multi-view data. UPDGD ACM MM’24    Through Monte-Carlo Dropout quantiﬁes the uncertainty of predicted labels, ﬁlls in missing labels, and imposes dual graph constraints at the view and label levels. MSLPP NN’24    Captures the Euclidean distance structure and cosine similarity structure of data simultaneously through multi-scale local preservation projection technology. MTD NeurIPS’24    Decouples single-channel representation into shared and proprietary representations, and designs cross-channel contrast loss to enhance semantic features. RANK TPAMI’25    Introduces a quality-aware subnetwork that can dynamically assign quality scores to each view of each sample, improving the reliability of cross-view feature fusion. Multi-view methods are speciﬁcally designed to handle data from multiple perspectives, while ’Missing-view’ and ’Missing-label’ methods are equipped to address situations where some views are missing as follows: (ab) varying view- missing ratios with 50% of the labels missing; (cd) 50% of the views missing under different label-missing rates sion (AP) on Corel5k and IAPRTC12 datasets. For AUC metrics, it exceeds UPDGD—the second-best model— by approximately 1 percentage point on both Corel5k and ESPGame datasets. • Figure 3 illustrates that increasing missing rates for either views or labels degrades model performance, with view absence causing more severe deterioration than label absence at equivalent rates. This discrepancy stems from views’ critical role in encoding essential sample infor- mation, compared to labels’ limited guidance capacity due to their long-tail distributions dominated by negative samples. • As visualized in Fig. 4 (with axes normalized per metric for clarity), VLCSA attains the highest scores across all six evaluation metrics on complete datasets. This con- ﬁrms that our model not only addresses dual missingness challenges but also excels in standard scenarios without missing data. These ﬁndings collectively validate VLCSA’s robustness and superior capability in handling both incomplete and com- plete data scenarios. 4.3 Ablation experiments To investigate the inﬂuence of various components in the VLCSA framework, we performed ablation experiments on the Corel5k and Pascal07 datasets under speciﬁc condi- tions: 50% view missing rate, 50% label missing rate, and 70% training sample utilization. We systematically evaluated key components by ﬁrst removing three critical loss func- tions (Lre, Lvld, and Lpae) from the complete framework, using the residual structure as a baseline. Subsequently, each component was incrementally reintroduced to quantify its individual contribution to model performance. This phased approach enabled precise measurement of each element’s impact. Further analysis examined the speciﬁc roles of three architectural elements: the Phase-Aware Excitation (PAE), Star-Shaped Interactive Sharing (SIS), and masking mecha- nism (mask). These components were selectively removed from the full VLCSA framework to assess their relative importance through comparative performance evaluation. Table 5 reveals that all components substantially con- tribute to ﬁnal classiﬁcation accuracy. The reconstruction loss Lre serves as an indirect supervisory signal that criti- Table 5 The ablation study for the VLCSA model was performed on the Corel5k and Pascal07 datasets, each with 50% missing views and 50% missing labels Method Corel5k Pascal07 Lcls Lre Lvld Lpae AP↑ 1-HL↑ 1-RL↑ AUC↑ AP↑ 1-HL↑ 1-RL↑ AUC↑  0.397 0.987 0.896 0.899 0.556 0.931 0.834 0.854   0.421 0.988 0.906 0.909 0.565 0.932 0.840 0.859   0.423 0.988 0.907 0.910 0.563 0.931 0.840 0.860   0.425 0.988 0.909 0.912 0.558 0.931 0.836 0.857    0.435 0.988 0.912 0.915 0.568 0.932 0.843 0.862    0.434 0.988 0.914 0.916 0.563 0.932 0.840 0.860    0.440 0.988 0.917 0.919 0.564 0.932 0.842 0.862     0.447 0.988 0.919 0.921 0.571 0.933 0.845 0.864 VLCSA w/o P AE 0.425 0.988 0.916 0.919 0.569 0.933 0.844 0.863 VLCSA w/o SI S 0.438 0.988 0.917 0.919 0.552 0.932 0.835 0.857 VLCSA w/o mask 0.430 0.988 0.909 0.912 0.565 0.932 0.839 0.860 Backbone refers to the VLCSA model that employs only the classiﬁcation loss Lcls 123 114 Page 16 of 20 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 Fig. 4 The performance of various methods across three complete datasets (with no missing views or labels) is presented. In the radar plot, the center represents the lowest performance, while the outer vertices indicate the highest results across six evaluation metrics cally improves view reconstruction quality. Both Lvld and Lpae operate in complementary spaces - the former con- straining embeddings in incomplete-view space, while the latter regulates complete-view space. This dual mecha- nism effectively extracts consensus semantics and maintains structural consistency between partial and global semantic topologies through cross-space alignment. The PAE compo- nent demonstrated particular signiﬁcance when ablated. Its removal necessitated replacement of Lpae with L′vld, result- ing in substantial performance degradation across metrics like Average Precision (AP). This conﬁrms PAE’s crucial role in preventing error propagation during initial learning phases. For SIS evaluation, we substituted its robust fusion mechanism with a learnable weighted summation approach: ¯Zi = 1 m m v=1 ξ(v) ¯E(v) i ,where{ξ(v)}m v=1 representssoftmax- normalized learnable weights. The performance gap between these implementations highlights SIS’s superior capability in generating consistent and stable feature aggregations. Finally, elimination of the masking strategy (mask) caused measurable performance declines across all metrics, veri- fying its effectiveness in eliminating redundant information while preserving critical features. This comprehensive analy- sis conﬁrms that VLCSA’s components work synergistically to achieve optimal multi-view learning. Quantitatively, the synergistic integration of PAE and SIS emerges as the primary catalyst for VLCSA’s superiority. Ablating PAE (VLCSA w/o PAE) causes the most severe performance drop in Corel5k (AP: -0.022 vs. full model), while removing SIS (VLCSA w/o SIS) most signiﬁcantly degrades Pascal07 metrics (AP: -0.019). This highlights PAE’s critical role in stabilizing early-stage representation learning and SIS’s capacity for robust cross-view fusion. Among loss functions, Lpae contributes most substantially to feature alignment in complete-view space (adding Lpae alone boosts Corel5k AP by +0.028 vs. backbone), while Lvld optimally constrains incomplete-view embeddings. The masking mechanism further enhances generalization by ﬁl- tering redundant features (Corel5k AUC drops by -0.009 when removed). Crucially, the full model’s performance exceeds all partial combinations, conﬁrming that VLCSA’s advantagestemsfromco-adaptiveoptimizationofthesecom- plementary components. In order to more intuitively show the independent contri- bution of each module to the ﬁnal performance, we created a detailed ablation visualization (Fig. 6). This ﬁgure system- atically compares the changes in the model’s core indicators (such as average accuracy AP) after removing different com- ponents (including single loss functions Lre, Lvld, Lpae and Fig. 5 The inﬂuence of hyperparameters α and β is analyzed on (hj) Corel5k and (ik) Pascal07 datasets. Each dataset is conﬁgured with 50% known labels, 50% missing views, and 70% of the data used for training 123 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 Page 17 of 20 114 Fig. 6 Ablation visualization of different modules. The scale on the left side of the vertical axis corresponds to the values of the three indicators “1-HL”, “1-RL”, and “AUC”, while the scale on the right side corresponds to the value of the “AP” indicator key architectural modules PAE, SIS, mask) through a clear combination of bar and line graphs. The visualization results vividly depict the performance gain brought by each module and its relative importance ranking, providing intuitive evi- dence for understanding the synergy of the components in the VLCSA framework, which corroborates the aforementioned quantitative analysis. 4.4 Parameter study The primary objective of parameter analysis is to investi- gate how model performance responds to parameter varia- tions, thereby identifying critical hyperparameters requiring meticulous tuning. Such analysis enhances both prediction accuracy and generalization capability while revealing the model’s adaptability across different hyperparameter conﬁg- urations, ultimately facilitating efﬁcient parameter optimiza- tion. We conducted systematic sensitivity evaluations for two pivotal hyperparameters: the loss function’s penalty coefﬁ- cient α and the mask ratio β. All other parameters remained ﬁxed during these experiments. Speciﬁcally, we tested α val- ues across the logarithmic scale [1, 1 × 10−1, 1 × 10−2, 1 × 10−3, 1 × 10−4]. Figure 5 illustrates the resulting vari- ations in key metrics including Average Precision (AP) and Area Under Curve (AUC) on both Pascal07 and Corel5k datasets. Experimental ﬁndings demonstrate that VLCSA achieves optimal performance with α values in the range [1, 1 × 10−2] for both datasets. Notably, the model maintains stable performance across this broad parameter spectrum, highlighting its inherent robustness to penalty coefﬁcient variations. For the mask ratio β, we evaluated values [0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9]. The dual random masking mechanism in VLCSA necessitates careful consideration - excessively high β values critically reduce available features, leading to mea- surable AP degradation. Our analysis conﬁrms 0.25 as the optimal β setting across all evaluated datasets. Collectively, these experiments verify VLCSA’s parameter stability, with performance remaining robust against substantial hyperpa- rameter variations. 5 Discussion and limitations While VLCSA demonstrates robust performance in handling dual incompleteness across diverse datasets, several limita- tions warrant discussion to guide future research: • Sensitivity to extreme incompleteness VLCSA assumes that at least one view remains available per sample (as enforced during dataset preprocessing). Performance may degrade signiﬁcantly in pathological cases where entire views are missing for all samples (e.g., complete sensor failure in autonomous driving sce- narios). Similarly, extreme label scarcity (e.g., >70% missing labels) exacerbates semantic drift in the label- guided alignment (VLAE), as the semantic prior matrix H (4) becomes sparse and noisy. Future work could integrate external knowledge bases or cross-domain cor- relations to alleviate this issue. • Computational overhead The cross-space alignment (CSA) and star-shaped inter- action (SIS) modules introduce additional computa- tional costs compared to single-space methods. The dual autoencoding process (incomplete + complete spaces) and pairwise similarity calculations (Q(v), ¯Q(v)) scale quadratically with sample size n. For ultra-large-scale datasets, this may limit real-time applicability. Opti- mizations such as stochastic sampling of instance pairs or hashing-based similarity approximation could be explored. • Assumption of view-label semantic coherence VLCSA relies on the premise that label correlations reﬂect underlying view semantics. This may fail in datasets with weak view-label correlations (e.g., medical images where a single view captures multiple unrelated 123 114 Page 18 of 20 Journal of King Saud University Computer and Information Sciences (2025) 37 :114 pathologies).ThesemanticanchorH mightmisguidefea- ture alignment if label noise is high or if multi-view fea- tures encode orthogonal information. Robustness could be improved by incorporating uncertainty estimation into H. • Generalization to streaming data VLCSA operates in batch mode and assumes static miss- ing patterns. In dynamic environments with continuously evolving missingness (e.g., incremental sensor deploy- ments), the model cannot adapt without retraining. Future extensions may explore online variants with incremental view/label recovery. 6 Conclusion In this study, we introduce a novel framework named View- Label Driven Cross-Space Structure Alignment Network (VLCSA) to address the limitations of IMvPMlC. The frame- work consists of several key components, each designed to tackle speciﬁc challenges in multi-view learning. First, we propose a view-label hybrid-driven autoencoder (VLAE). This component incorporates semantic anchors as fusion centers, enabling joint optimization of cross-view semantic consistency. By guiding cross-view alignment towards the true semantic structure, VLAE achieves precise reconstruc- tion of missing views. Next, we develop cross-space structure alignment (CSA), which employs a view-label hybrid-driven loss function. This loss function is applied to both the original and complete feature spaces, ensuring that partial and overall semantic topologies are effectively aligned across different spaces. To mitigate the issue of obtaining poor reconstruction data during the initial training phase, we introduce phase- aware excitation (PAE). This mechanism helps alleviate error accumulation caused by inadequate reconstruction quality in early learning stages. Finally, we incorporate the star- shaped interactive sharing module (SIS) to facilitate efﬁcient cross-view information exchange. Through a simple addition strategy, SIS ensures consistent and robust feature aggrega- tion. Extensive experiments on ﬁve benchmark multi-view multi-label datasets demonstrate that VLCSA achieves supe- rior performance in handling missing views and labels, as well as complete data. Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of each component in the proposed framework. 15. **AV-MaskEnhancer: Enhancing Video Representations through Audio-Visual Masked Autoencoder**
   - DOI: https://doi.org/10.1109/ictai59109.2023.00058
   - Verdict: yes
   - Classifier reason: The paper explicitly uses masked autoencoder ideas and cites MAE as the basis for its proposed method, extending the approach to audio-visual video representation learning. This is more than a background mention because the abstract says the work is 'Based on this' and proposes an 'Audio-Visual Masked Autoencoder.'
   - Matched patterns: none
   - LLM evidence source: abstract_fallback
   - LLM confidence: medium
   - LLM evidence quote: Previous work based on mask autoencoders such as ImageMAE [10] and VideoMAE [23] has proven the effectiveness... Based on this, we propose AV-MaskEnhancer
   - Abstract evidence: Learning high-quality video representation has shown significant applications in computer vision and remains challenging. Previous work based on mask autoencoders such as ImageMAE [10] and VideoMAE [23] has proven the effectiveness of learning representations in images and videos through reconstruction strategy in the visual modality. However, these models exhibit inherent limitations, particularly in scenarios where extracting features solely from the visual modality proves challenging, such as when dealing with low-resolution and blurry original videos. Based on this, we propose AV-MaskEnhancer for learning high-quality video representation by combining visual and audio information. Our approach addresses the challenge by demonstrating the complementary nature of audio and video features in cross-modality content. Moreover, our result of the video classification task on the UCF101 [21] dataset outperforms the existing work and reaches the state-of-the-art, with a top-1 accuracy of 98.8% and a top-5 accuracy of 99.9%.
   - Full-text evidence: not available in extracted text set

16. **Dual stage MRI image restoration based on blind spot denoising and hybrid attention**
   - DOI: https://doi.org/10.1186/s12880-024-01437-8
   - Verdict: yes
   - Classifier reason: The paper explicitly uses and builds on the blind-spot denoising framework from the target paper, naming Blind2Unblind and a global mask mapper as core components of its denoising stage.
   - Matched patterns: none
   - LLM evidence source: fulltext
   - LLM confidence: high
   - LLM evidence quote: Building upon the research in [15], we propose NRAE
   - Abstract evidence: BACKGROUND: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is extensively utilized in clinical diagnostics and medical research, yet the imaging process is often compromised by noise interference. This noise arises from various sources, leading to a reduction in image quality and subsequently hindering the accurate interpretation of image details by clinicians. Traditional denoising methods typically assume that noise follows a Gaussian distribution, thereby neglecting the more complex noise types present in MRI images, such as Rician noise. As a result, denoising remains a challenging and practical task. METHOD: The main research work of this paper focuses on modifying mask information based on a global mask mapper. The mask mapper samples all blind spot pixels on the denoised image and maps them to the same channel. By incorporating perceptual loss, it utilizes all available information to improve performance while avoiding identity mapping. During the denoising process, the model may mistakenly remove some useful information as noise, resulting in a loss of detail in the denoised image. To address this issue, we train a generative adversarial network (GAN) with adaptive hybrid attention to restore the detailed information in the denoised MRI images. RESULT: The two-stage model NRAE shows an improvement of nearly 1.4 dB in PSNR and approximately 0.1 in SSIM on clinical datasets compared to other classic models. Specifically, compared to the baseline model, PSNR is increased by about 0.6 dB, and SSIM is only 0.015 lower. From a visual perspective, NRAE more effectively restores the details in the images, resulting in richer and clearer representation of image details. CONCLUSION: We have developed a deep learning-based two-stage model to address noise issues in medical MRI images. This method not only successfully reduces noise signals but also effectively restores anatomical details. The current results indicate that this is a promising approach. In future work, we plan to replace the current denoising network with more advanced models to further enhance performance.
   - Full-text evidence: Liu et al. BMC Medical Imaging (2024) 24:259 https://doi.org/10.1186/s12880-024-01437-8 RESEARCH Open Access © The Author(s) 2024.
