AI-assisted cervical cytology precancerous screening for high-risk population in resource limited regions using compact microscope Jiaxin Bai1,#, Ning Li1,#, Hua Ye2,#, Xu Li1, Li Chen3, Junbo Hu4, Baochuan Pang5, Xiaodong Chen6, , Gong Rao1, Qinglei Hu7, Shijie Liu1, Si Sun8, Cheng Li5, Xiaohua Lv1, Shaoqun Zeng1, Jing Cai8,*, Shenghua Cheng2,*, Xiuli Liu1,* 1Britton Chance Center and MoE Key Laboratory for Biomedical Photonics, Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics-Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, China 2School of Biomedical Engineering and Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Medical Image Processing, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, China 3Department of Clinical Laboratory, Tongji Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, Hubei, China 4Department of Pathology, Maternal and Child Hospital of Hubei Province, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, Hubei, China 5Wuhan Landing Institute for Artificial Intelligence Cancer Diagnosis Industry Development, Wuhan, Hubei, China 6Duodao People's Hospital, Jingmen, Hubei, China 7Convergence Technology Co., Ltd, Wuhan, Hubei, China 8Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Union Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, China #These authors contributed equally *Corresponding authors: jingcai@hust.edu.cn (J. Cai), chengsh2023@smu.edu.cn (S. Cheng), xlliu@mail.hust.edu.cn (X. Liu) Abstract: The insufficient coverage of cervical cytology screening in underdeveloped countries or remote areas is currently the bottleneck hurdle to its widespread implementation. Conventional centralized medical screening methods are heavily dependent on sizable, costly investments as well as sufficient qualified pathologists. In this paper, we have developed a cervical precancerous assisted-screening system for identifying high-risk squamous intraepithelial lesion (SIL) cases in regions with limited resources. This system utilizes a low-cost miniature microscope and a low-pathologist-reliance artificial intelligence algorithm. We design a low-cost compact microscope with pixel resolution about 0.87 m/pixel for imaging cytology slides. To tackle the challenge of sparely-distributed lesion cells in cytology whole slide images (WSIs), we have developed a dual-stage slide classification model. In first stage, we train an instance-level classifier by self-supervised pretraining on large-number unlabeled cervical images and transfer learning on small-number labeled images, aiming to reduce negative cells within a slide. In the second stage, we employ our proposed Att-Transformer, which aggregates deep features extracted from the top 200 lesion probabilities instances, for slide-level classification. We train and validate our model on 3,510 low- resolution WSIs collected from four different centers, and evaluate our model on 364 slides from two external centers in remote areas, achieving AUC (area under receiver operating characteristic curve) of 0.87 and 0.89 respectively for screening high risk cases. We also evaluate it on new independent cohorts of 391 slides from the original four centers and achieve AUC of 0.89. Overall, all the results indicate that integration of our innovative algorithm together with the compact microscope represents a promising approach to cervical cytology precancerous screening for high- risk population in medical resource limited regions. This affordable and accessible screening is significant as it contributes towards the goal of eliminating cervical cancer worldwide. Index terms: Cervical cytology precancerous screening; resource limited regions; compact microscope; computational cytology Introduction: Cervical cancer is the fourth most common cancer among women worldwide and also the fourth leading cause of death from cancer in women1,2. In cases of cervical cancer, between 84% and 90% cervical cancer cases occurred in low- and middle-income countries such as South Africa, India, China and Brazil. This also means that these countries bear a heavy burden to budget3,4. Beyond human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccinations, cytology screening remains crucial for reducing the incidence of cervical cancer. Historically, Pap smear programs have played a significant role in reducing the incidence by up to 80% in developed countries5. Nevertheless, the widespread implementation of screens still faces challenges due to the lack of trained cytologists and the prohibitive cost of necessary equipment particularly in the regions with limited resources. In recent years, there has been a notable improvement in the cost-efficiency of portable optical instruments. The advancements have positioned them as viable alternatives to commercial systems, aiming to meet the diverse demands of bioscience, medical diagnosis, and other fields6–8. For example, portable microscopes have been used to diagnose Malaria, Sickle Cell Anemia and Tuberculosis9–11. These portable microscopes incorporate complementary metal oxide semiconductor (CMOS) sensors, and a concise optical system that may also harness the capabilities of smartphone cameras. Unlike bulky and costly professional microscopes typically found in medical facilities, these portable microscopes significantly slash expenses while ensuring an acceptable image quality. In this context, advanced medical image analysis algorithms have been developed to automate processing the acquired images, such as automatically quantifying cells in blood samples8, fastly identifying abnormal targets12 and so on, thereby enabling simply trained individuals to conduct the procedures effectively. For cervical cytology, automatic analysis algorithms can facilitate the detection of abnormal cells or high-risk cases. The synergy of compact microscopes and artificial intelligence (AI) represents a promising approach for large-scale periodic cervical screening in areas where medical resources are scarce. Over the last few decades, there have been many algorithm researches about classification and detection for cervical cells. Initially, these algorithms are usually based on handcrafted feature extraction, cell or nuclei segmentation13,14 and traditional machine learning classifier. Thereafter, convolutional neural network15 (CNN) and recent vision Transformer16 are utilized to identify cervical lesion cells through a data-driven manner, improving recognition accuracy17–24. Despite the advancements in cell-level and slide-level analysis, there remains a discernible gap between the two. To handle giga-pixel WSIs of cervical cytology containing tens of thousands of cells or instances (local cell images), researchers have adopted multi-stage or hybrid slide-level analysis methods based on large-number lesion cell annotations25–28. For instance, Cheng et al.25 introduced a method that combines high-resolution (HR) and low-resolution (LR) images to recommend lesion cells from a WSI using CNNs, and classified a WSI by aggregating deep features of the recommended top 10 lesion instances through a recurrent neural network (using 79, 911 annotated lesion cells). Lin et al.26 designed a dual-path network employing a synergistic grouping loss function for lesion retrieval, and classified slides by aggregating the first stage predictions by rule-based risk stratification (using 202, 557 abnormal cell images). Cao et al.27 proposed a patch-to-sample inference approach which trains patch-level classifier by a hard patch mining strategy and derives slide level diagnosis utilizing score embedding and token pooling techniques (using 25, 134 annotated lesion cells). Basically, these methods utilize high-resolution images to first train an accurate classifier or detector of abnormal cells and then aggregate learned deep features or predicted positive probabilities of the found abnormal cells in each WSI to represent and classify slides. In these methods, the efficacy of slide classification is highly contingent upon the precise identification of cells at the initial phase, which necessitates the utilization of high-resolution images replete with sufficient texture details and large-scale annotation datasets. Conversely, low-resolution WSIs (LRWSIs), captured by compact microscope, suffer from limited resolution and a dearth of intricate textures, render them incapable of facilitating accurate cell-level discernment. Therefore, these conventional approaches are unsuitable for the processing of LRWSIs. In the paper, we have established an auxiliary screening system for cervical cancer tailored to resource-limited regions, with the aim of detecting high-risk SIL (squamous intraepithelial lesion, including high-grade and low-grade). This system is based on a low-cost miniature microscope and an artificial intelligence algorithm that relies less on pathologists. We have designed a low-cost miniature microscope with a sampling rate about 0.87 m/pixel，offering acceptable image quality and easy of connection and control via a Universal Serial Bus (USB) interface with laptop or pad. To address the issue of sparely-distributed lesion cells within cytology WSIs, we have developed a two-stage slide classification model. In the first stage, an instance-level classifier is trained through self-supervised pretraining on large-number unlabeled cervical images and transfer learning on small amount labeled images, aiming to reduce negative cells within a slide. In the second stage, deep features extracted from redundant instances with the top 200 lesion probabilities are aggregated to represent and classify slides, which is achieved by the proposed attention-based Transformer (Att- Transformer). The Att-Transformer integrates a vision Transformer backbone with a Gated- Attention module29, designed to adapt and weight the spare-distributed lesion cells in cervical cytology slides. The Att-Transformer is also employed as the representation network for the instance-level classifier in the first stage. We trained and validated our model for classifying slides into positives and negatives on 3,510 LRWSIs, containing 13 groups of data collected from 4 different medical centers over different time periods. We achieved AUC of 0.85 for 194 positive and 195 negative cases in the internal test set. Subsequently, we evaluated our model on new independent cohorts of 391 slides from the four centers, achieving an AUC of 0.89 for high-risk SIL and negative cases. We further assessed the performance on two external centers (primary care regions), attaining AUC of 0.87 and 0.89 for high-risk SIL and negative cases in External-A (n=253) and External-B (n=111) respectively. We compared our optimal pathology WSI analysis algorithms CALM and TransMIL on our LRWSI datasets. An AUC below 0.6 in external test sets indicates that these weakly supervised multiple instance learning (MIL) methods are not suitable for cervical cytology WSIs with sparely- distributed lesion cells. Additionally, we conducted ablation studies demonstrating that 1) supervised features are more effective and robust for slide classifier; 2) the proposed Att- Transformer aggregate instance-level features better to represent slides and has better interpretability. Overall, all the results indicate that our algorithm, in conjunction with the miniature microscope, presents a promising cervical cytology precancerous screening solution for high-risk population in areas lacking medical resources. It is recommended that individuals identified as high- risk through this screening process should be referred to central regional hospitals for further diagnosis and treatment. We firmly assert that this cost-effective and readily available screening method is pivotal to realizing the goal of World Health Organization (WHO) to eliminate cervical cancer worldwide. Results: Compact microscope and cytology LRWSI dataset In this paper, we designed a low-cost and small-size microscope for resource-limited area as shown in Figure 1a. The core component of our hardware is an integrated optical module which can be controlled through a system on chip (SoC) and supports autofocusing. The size of the integrated optical module is 38.5 × 38.5 × 29.9 mm. The cost, only about 300 $, is orders of magnitude lower than that of traditional screening system used in medical centers. The optical module and other components, mainly the sample carrier and transmission-type light source, can be integrated through a mechanical frame which is produced by 3D printing. The field of view of our compact microscope is 3840 × 2160 pixels and the sampling rate is 0.87 m/pixel. We visualized the image quality of common pathology microscope and our compact microscope in Figure 1b. The left sub-figure shows both kinds of images well captured the morphological information of cells, such as outline and size of cells and nuclei, which basically support the detection of typical abnormal cells. The right sub-figure shows the pixel intensity of the same nucleus from both images, and our compact microscopy has lower contrast and obvious background noises. These characteristics of compact microscope may lead to uncertainty in cytologists’ diagnosis especially when detailed texture information is needed, thus accurately annotating massive lesion cells on LRWSIs is not feasible. To further demonstrate the differences in imaging quality between the two imaging devices, we have provided 100 pairs of images for the same region in Supplementary 2. In computational pathology field, there have been many high-performance weakly-supervised WSI classification methods30–33 which are built without the need for cell-level annotations. However, there are few such kind of methods for cytology WSIs. On the contrary, most of them need huge number of cell-level annotations as mentioned in the introduction. We show the different distribution characteristic of lesions between cytology and histopathology WSIs as shown in Figure 1d. The lesion cells in cytology slides are much more disperse and sparser while those in histopathology slides typically form large, cohesive areas and establish complete spatial organization. We test the proportion of lesion cell regions in Camelyon16 (110 slides) and a private fully annotated cytology dataset (106 slides). The results are shown in Figure 1c. The max proportion is 40% and 1.5% for histopathology and cytology respectively. This sparse lesion cell distribution makes it hard to find local lesions though slide-level supervision learning for cytology WSIs. To get our LRWSI datasets, we used our compact microscope to redundantly scan a slide and get about 90 fields of view for each slide. Each field of view has the size of 3840 ×2160 pixels (0.87 m/pixel). Then, we cropped out the poorly exposed edge areas and redundantly divided a view to instances (patch images of 256 × 256 pixels). Finally, we got about 5,760 instances for each slide. We collected a multi-center WSI dataset, including 13 groups of data collected from 4 medical centers over different time periods, total 3,899 LRWSIs. These slides were divided into training, validation and internal testing sets at slide-wise ration of 8:1:1. To evaluate the generalization of our method, we used two independent external datasets and one internal dataset of new cohorts: External-A, External-B and NewCohorts. External-A and External-B come from two new centers from grassroots areas, and NewCohorts is most recent cohorts from the original four centers. More details can be found in Description of datasets in Methods. Figure 1 | a) A sketch of our compact microscope and the form of a LRWSI. The optical module contains two lens sets: L1 and L2. L2 is fixed and L1 is controlled by a voice coil motor (VCM) to support autofocusing. We use CMOS sensor to capture the image and transmitted light-emitting diode (LED) light source to illuminate the sample between the light source and objective lens. A SoC is used to control the whole process and storage acquired LRWSIs. A LRWSI is usually composed of about 90 views of images. b) Visualizing the image quality of standard microscope and our compact microscope. The upper right part of the left image is a HR image acquired by standard microscope and the lower left part is a LR image acquired by our compact microscope. The right figure shows the average pixel intensity (R, G and B channel) of a line which is annotated by red line in left image. c) Proportion of positive foreground in Camelyon16 (110 slides) and a fully annotated cytopathological dataset (106 slides). The results show great difference between two kinds of WSIs. d) Difference modality between histology and cytology WSI. The abnormal area is annotated by red curve. Inspiration and algorithm design To address the challenges posed by the sparsely-distributed lesion cells in cytology WSIs and the low imaging quality of compact microscope, we designed a two-stage LRWSI analysis method as shown in Figure 2. The first stage aims to reduce negative cells within a slide while the second stage aggregates deep features of screened redundant positive instances to represent and classify slides. First, we divided each LRWSI into about 5700 instances redundantly, which have a size of 256 × 256 pixels and are resized to 224 × 224 pixel as the input of instance-level classifier. Then, we trained it by masked auto-encoder40 (MAE) based self-supervised pretraining on 239k unlabeled cervical images randomly cropped from slides in the training set and transfer learning on 7,599 labeled lesion images and 65,700 negative images randomly cropped from negative slides in the training set. In order to address the issue of accurately annotating lesion cells in LR images, we obtained LR lesion cell annotations by image registration between LR images and annotated HR images from the same slides (see Description of dataset in Methods). The self-supervised representation learning helps reduce the number of annotations required for training downstream instance-level classifier. Finally, we highlighted the redundant instances of top 200 lesion probabilities within a slide, and aggregated their deep features (200 × 768 dimensions) to represent and classify slides by Att-Transformer. In the weakly supervised MIL methods for histology, the manner of aggregating all instances is challenging for cervical cytology WSIs containing sparse lesion cells, as it is difficult to distinguish positive instance representations from massive representations within a slide, leading to potential overfitting to the aggregated long-sequence features. Previous cytology slide classification methods heavily rely on a large number of high-resolution annotated images to support a high- performance abnormal instance classifier and identify several lesion instances in a slide, which is not suitable for low-resolution images. Therefore, in the first stage of our model, we reduced the negative instances within a slide and selected top-200 redundant instance features. This ensures the discriminative information required for slide representation while avoiding the drawbacks of excessively long sequence of instance features. Under the transfer learning approach, we pretrained the classifier based on masked auto- encoder34 with a large-number of unlabeled cervical images (239k) which were randomly sampled from the training slides. Both the first stage and second made use of the proposed Att-Transformer (see Att-transformer in Methods). Att-Transformer is a combination of Vision Transformer (ViT) backbone and Gated- Attention module, which is utilized to adapt and weight the sparsely-distributed lesion cells in cytology slides. The ViT backbone aims to encode the input information and the Gated-Attention module provides an interpretable aggregation of all tokens by learning attention scores. We used ViT-B version as the backbone. By visualizing the tokens within the Gated-Attention module and their corresponding attention scores, we found that the attention mechanism aids in locating abnormal morphology cells within an instance image during the first stage, and assists in selecting abnormal instance-level representations during the second stage. We designed a two-stage self-supervised learning (SSL) strategy to optimize the Transformer backbone and the Gated-Attention module (see Pretraining and finetuning details in Methods). We used hard instances mining and data augmentation strategy to boost the accuracy of the first stage model (see Pretraining and finetuning details in Methods). Figure 2 | The proposed LRWSIs analysis system. Our system is a concise two stage system where the first and second stage make use of Att-Transformer as backbone. The Att-Transformer is composed by a ViT backbone and Gated-Attention module. The input of the first stage model is instance of 224 × 224 pixels (resize from 256 × 256 pixels). The output of the first stage contains merged features of instances, attention scores for patches in each instance and positive probability of instances. We choose merged features of instances which get top 200 positive scores as input of next stage model. The next stage model is similar as the first stage model, which use the features of top instances as input. The final output is attention score of each input instance and positive probability of slide. Feature representation based on self-supervised learning (SSL) and transfer learning To validate the effectiveness of the feature representations of the instance classifier, we visualized the instance features in the instance test set by t-SNE35. As shown in Figure 3, although there are some hard instances on the classification boundary which extend to opposite class, most positive and negative instances are well distinguished. The morphological characteristics of cells are also clustered by our model. For example, we can see the far to the right area shows the negative instances with few cells and the left area shows the characteristics of perinuclear halos and nuclear enlargement. Since the LR images lack adequate textures, some lesion cells cannot be identified by the model and manifest as hard samples. These results indicate that the instance classifier based on SSL and transfer learning is effective and meets the design goal of the first stage, which is to exclude the vast majority of negative instances within a slide. The strategy keeps a balance between limited image quality and strict classification boundary. Figure 3 | Visualization of the feature space of our model. 767 positive instances and 767 negative instances are randomly selected from the LR instance test set, and are used to get feature embedding by our first stage model. The embeddings are reduced to two dimensions by t-SNE and the corresponding instances and relative position are plotted to visualize the feature space. We only show one image in one place in map to avoid overlapping. Cervical precancerous screening for HSIL and LSIL cases To evaluate the effectiveness and generalization of our entire method, we compared it with state-of- the-art WSI classifier methods CLAM32 and TransMIL33. The two methods achieved optimal results on pathology datasets through aggregating pretrained features (ResNet50 pretrained on ImageNet) of all instances within a slide to represent and classify slides. We first train and validate the models for classifying slides into positives and negatives. Positive slides contain following subclasses: high grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HSIL), low grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL) and atypical squamous cells (ASC). In the internal test set, our method, CLAM and TransMIL achieved AUC of 0.85, 0.65 and 0.67 for positive and negative cases respectively. We further evaluated all methods for detecting high-risk SIL (HSIL and LSIL) cases on two external test sets External A (n=253), External B (n=111) and one internal test set of new cohorts named NewCohorts (n=391). Figure 4 shows the ROC curves of comparison methods and the sensitivity and specificity (threshold=0.5). On the three independent test sets, CLAM and TransMIL achieved poor binary classification results of AUC <= 0.57 for high-risk SIL and negative cases. The same situation also appears in the following figures. Our method achieved AUC of 0.87 and 0.89 on External-A and External-B, and obtained AUC of 0.89 on NewCohorts. Similarly, both sensitivity and specificity of our method far exceed CLAM and TransMIL on the three independent test sets. We also evaluated the screening performance for ASC, LSIL, HSIL and negative cases on the three test sets. Our method achieved average AUC of 0.77 on the three test sets while AUCs of the comparison methods are lower than 0.53 (Supplementary 1 Fig. 1). Due to the limited resolution of our compact microscope, it is hard to accurately identify ASC slides, which have low lesion degree and fewer lesion cells. Therefore, for ASC slides, the classification performance is relatively low. The experimental results mentioned above indicate that both CLAM and TransMIL, which rely on the aggregation of self-supervised representations of all instances within a slide to characterize and classify slides, are not suitable for cervical cytology slides with sparsely-distribution lesion cells. In contrast, our method obtains supervised representations through SSL and transfer learning, and aggregates of representations from the top-200 redundant instances to classify slides, effectively handling this sparsity in cytology slides. These experimental results also indicate that the combination of our compact microscope and AI algorithm can achieve effective screening for high-risk potential cervical cancer patients (HISL and LISL) in resource-limited areas. The overall hardware cost of the screening system based on our compact microscope is orders of magnitude lower than that of traditional screening system used in medical centers, making it affordable and potentially widely applicable. Figure 4 | Top figures illustrate the ROC curves of CLAM, TransMIL and our methods on internal test (InTest), NewCohorts, External-A and External-B set. AUC and sample size are added in the legend and above the figure respectively. Bottom figures show the sensitivity and specificity (threshold=0.5) of three methods for each test set. Supervised feature is more effective and robust for slide classifier We compared three different types of instance features to further demonstrate the effectiveness of our feature extraction: Supervised, Pretrained, and Pretrained on ImageNet. Supervised denotes features of top-200 lesion probability instances extracted by our first-stage model. Pretrained and Pretrained on ImageNet denotes features of the same top-200 instances extracted by the model pretrained only on unlabeled cervical images and the model pretrained on ImageNet respectively. We uniformly used the second-stage Att-Transformer to aggregate these three types of features and classify the slides. Notably, we did not use any instance-level augmentation in these experiments for reducing computation cost, thus the results are different from that in the last section (see Data augmentation in Pretraining and finetuning details in Methods). Figure 5 shows the ROC curves of slide classification results. For internal test set, Supervised, Pretrained and Pretrained on ImageNet achieved high AUC of 0.92, 0.91 and 0.87 for positive and negative cases respectively. In External-A, External-B and NewCohorts test sets, Pretrained and Pretrained on ImageNet achieved average AUC of 0.55 and 0.46 for high-risk SIL and negative cases respectively, while our method achieved average AUC of 0.86. We can see that Pretrained and Pretrained on ImageNet features have high AUC values in the internal test set, but they decrease significantly on the independent test sets, while supervised features maintains a highly consistent level. This indicates that features of Pretrained and Pretrained on ImageNet are over-fitted during slide classification, whereas supervised features are capable of identifying true lesion information among the top 200 instances and thus support a robust slide classifier. These experimental results indicate that supervised representation learning is more effective and robust compared to self- supervised representation and ImageNet pre-trained representation. Figure 5 | The ROC curves of Supervised, Pretrained and Pretrained on ImageNet (ImageNet for short in legend) features on InTest, NewCohorts, External-A and External-B set. AUC and sample size are added in the legend and above the figure respectively. Interpretable slide classification based on Att-Transformer We designed Att-Transformer as a new model, which not only takes advantage of transformer’s capability of modeling long and flexible feature sequence, but also utilizes the gated attention for weighting and aggregating instance features to represent slides. Attention mechanism has achieved great success for pathology slide analysis as it supports learnable feature aggregation. Instance features containing discriminative information are emphasized and the attention scores provide interpretability. Figure 6 shows the two-stage attention mechanism. We can see that instances with top attention scores in a view are more likely to contain true lesion cells, and the patches with the top attention score in an instance are more likely to overlap with typical positive cells, such as hyperchromatic cells with a large nucleus. Figure 6 | Visualization of attention mechanism and interpretability. Cell-level ROI and instance-level ROI are acquired by the attention score of first stage model and second stage model. We frame out instances whose positive probability is in top 200 as instance-level ROI in a view of a slide. We also plot the attention scores of patches by heat map and frame the cell-level ROI where patches get highest attention scores. The standard reference images are the same region of cell-level ROI in standard WSI. Further, we compared classification performance of different instance aggregation methods on the same top-200 instance features extracted by our method. Figure 7 shows AUC, sensitivity and specificity for high-risk SIL and negative cases by these methods in External-A, External-B and NewCohorts test sets. We compared 8 different aggregation methods: MaxPool, MeanPool, ABMIL36, GatedAtt36, CLAM32, TransMIL33 and our Att-Transformer. We can see that Att- Transformer achieves optimal overall performance. All these results reflect the interpretability and superiority of Att-Transformer as instance feature aggregator. Figure 7 | AUC, sensitivity and specificity of different methods: CALM, ABMIL, GatedAtt, CLAM, MeanPool, MaxPool, TransMIL and Att-Transformer. Sample size is added above the figure. Discussion New imaging techniques and artificial intelligence may hold the key to enabling low-cost cervical precancerous screening and we demonstrate a promising solution. Our work shows the application of a miniature microscope for primary cervical cancer screening in resource limited areas, which is portable, low-cost, and has acceptable image quality for high-risk SIL cases. To address the problem of sparely-distribution lesion cells within cervical cytology WSIs, different from macro-size lesions in histopathology WSIs, we proposed an instance-level feature extraction method based on SSL and supervised transfer learning, and demonstrated the necessity of supervised training for cervical cytology analysis. Our feature extraction process greatly enhances the performance and generalization of the system. Finally, we achieved acceptable classification AUC for screening high- risk SIL cases by the interpretable Att-Transformer to weight and aggregate instance-level representation of potential lesion cells. We also released a powerful cervical feature extraction and classification model to enrich the infrastructures of community. WHO has set the goal of eliminating cervical cancer worldwide, but the challenge lies in achieving widespread screening coverage in underdeveloped countries and grassroots areas. These countries and regions face difficulties in adopting existing screening techniques, which rely on professional equipment and experienced pathologists, due to limited financial budgets and a shortage of skilled pathologists. The approach proposed in this paper, based on the compact microscopes and AI algorithms for precancerous screening, offers advantages such as low cost, portability and high efficiency, making it highly suitable for screening demand in underdeveloped countries and grassroots areas. The method presented in this paper demonstrates overall Sensitivity = 0.86 and Specificity = 0.74 (AUC = 0.90) for the high-risk SIL cases in three independent test sets, effectively screening these high-risk individuals and prompting them to seek further examination, diagnosis, and treatment at centralized medical centers. In the field of computational pathology, weakly supervised slide analysis methods relying solely on slide labels have gradually become mainstream, achieving comparable slide classification accuracy compared to early methods that relied on local cell annotations. Lesions within tissue pathology slides are typically present in large area form, far outnumbering cytology slides. Therefore, even without supervised representations of local cells, good classification of slide-level representation through aggregating long-sequence instance features can still be achieved, but this is not suitable for cytology slides. The diseased cells in cervical cytology slides are extremely sparse and dispersed, accounting for approximately 1/100 or even lower. This distribution characteristic results in that the effectiveness of slide-level representation highly depends on the accuracy of local cell image recognition or discriminative representation. If the accuracy of lesion cell recognition or representation is inaccurate, subsequent aggregated slide-level features are prone to overfitting during classification. The experimental results of this paper demonstrate the necessity of supervised representations of local cells. The common manner of using self-supervised or ImageNet pre-trained cell representations in pathology MIL methods cannot achieve effective classification for cytology slides. The stability of the compact microscope designed in this study is still insufficient compared to commercial pathology scanners, such as light attenuation. Subsequently, we will further optimize and replace components to enhance its stability during long-term use and transportation. Our study only screened for cervical squamous cell carcinoma and did not consider patients with adenocarcinoma. Although squamous cell carcinoma accounts for the vast majority of cervical cancer cases, adenocarcinoma is showing an increasing trend and has a worse prognosis. Therefore, future exploration will focus on adenocarcinoma screening using the compact microscope. Patients with atypical squamous cells (ASC) generally have lower overall lesion severity, with lower number and grade of lesion cells within a slide than those in LSIL and HSIL patients. Consequently, using a low-resolution compact microscope for ASC screening results in a lower AUC. Improving screening accuracy for low-risk populations is also a direction for future work. Additionally, the lack of standardization in slide preparation and staining processes in underdeveloped countries or grassroots areas affects the generalization of AI algorithms. Standardizing and implementing data quality control are critical for the application of our screening system. Conclusion For the high-risk population of cervical cancer in medical resource limited areas, we have developed a low-cost precancerous screening method based on the compact microscope. Our method utilizes self-supervised pretraining on large-number unlabeled cervical images and transfer learning on small-number labeled images to obtain an instance-level classifier, aiming to reduce negative cells within a slide. Then, deep features extracted from redundant instances of the top-200 lesion probabilities are aggregated to represent and classify slides by the proposed Att-Transformer. Our method achieves overall AUC of 0.90 for high-risk SIL and negative cases on three independent test sets (n=755). The experimental results indicate: 1) the necessity of supervised instance representation for the generalization of cytology slide classification with sparsely-distributed lesion cells; 2) Att-Transformer outperforms other aggregators and possesses interpretability; 3) our method can effectively screen the high-risk SIL population, outperforming the state-of-the-art MIL methods in histopathology. The screening method formed by the compact microscope and WSI analysis algorithm overall has high screening accuracy, good generalization, and low cost. It is expected to achieve primary screening for high-risk populations in medical resource limited areas, contributing to increasing screening coverage in these areas and promoting WHO's efforts to eliminate cervical cancer worldwide. Methods Description of compact microscope In order to design a low-cost and portable imaging device, we used a microscope module from Convergence Technology Co., Ltd (Wuhan, Hubei, China), which is only 48 × 38 × 20 mm. Sony IMX 258(1/3.06 '', pixel size 1.12 m) image sensor and two aspherical lens sets are integrated inside the module. The two lens sets are with focal lengths of 3.6 mm and 4.7 mm, serving as objective and tube lenses respectively. This configuration achieves an approximate magnification of 1.3 for the entire microscopy system, providing a pixel resolution of 0.87 m/pixel. The module also integrates a voice coil motor (VCM) module with a travel range of 200μm and a step precision of less than 8 m, which is supported by integrated autofocus algorithm. A diffuse LED light panel serves as a transmitted illumination source. Effective microscope illumination can be acquired by adjusting the distance between the panel and the sample. The structural components are constructed using 3D-printed materials to form the framework of the entire system. In order to meet the demand for edge computing, we have selected the Hi3519 System on Chip (SoC) for image acquisition and storage. This SoC incorporates a highly efficient neural network inference engine, capable of delivering up to 2.5 trillion operations per second (TOPS) in neural network computations. The SoC is also equipped with abundant peripheral interfaces, including high-definition multimedia interface (HDMI) and USB, which streamline the connectivity process with devices such as microscope module and display units. The entire system is designed with energy efficiency, ensuring that the power consumption remains below 5 watts. When powered by a 6000mAh battery, the device is projected to offer over 4 hours of continuous operation. Parameters are shown in the Table 1, and a picture of our small microscope and more details can be seen in Supplementary 1 Fig. 2. Table 1. parameters of our microscope Performance Parameter Field of view 1.39 mm × 1.39 mm Pixel resolution 0.87 μm/pixel Optical resolution < 2 μm Illumination LED Backlight Module Image sensor IMX258 Control unit HI3519AV100 Storage 32 G TF card Power 4.8 W Cost < 300 $ Size 14.0 cm × 11.0 cm × 13.5 cm Weight < 2 kg Description of datasets LRWSI datasets Table 2 shows the LRWSI datasets used in our paper for training, validating and testing. A LRWSI is composed by about 90 views and each view is acquired by a single shoot. The pixel size of a raw view is 3840 × 3160 pixels with sampling rate of 0.87 m/pixel. Before processing, we removed the poorly exposed edges by cropping the center 1600 × 1600 pixels region. For internal dataset, we collect 3,899 LRWSIs from four different centers at different periods: A1-A5, B1-B2, C1-C2 and D1-D4. We made our own slides of the cell samples from Tongji Hospital of Huazhong University of Science and Technology which is referred as A. Slides made by Wuhan Union Hospital of Huazhong University of Science and Technology is referred as B. C and D are slides made by Maternal and Child Hospital of Hubei Province and Tongji Hospital of Huazhong University of Science and Technology respectively. The internal set has only binary labels: POS and NEG. A POS slide may be one of the following subclasses: HSIL, LSIL and ASC. We randomly separated the whole dataset to train, validate and test set by 8:1:1. For external dataset, we collect 972 LRWSIs which have four subclasses: ASC, HSIL, LSIL, NEG. E1 and F1 are named External-A and External-B in paper respectively which are from two external grassroots centers. E is slides acquired from grassroots community by Wuhan Landing Institute for Artificial Intelligence Cancer Diagnosis Industry Development. F is slides from Duodao People's Hospital, Jingmen, Hubei. NewCohorts is composed by C3, D5 and D6 which is new cohorts from the same centers of internal datasets. Table 2. Details of LRWSI dataset IN SOURCES TRAIN (NEG/POS) VALID (NEG/POS) TEST (NEG/POS) TOTAL A1 267/18 33/2 34/2 356 A2 22/56 2/6 2/6 94 A3 239/54 29/6 29/6 363 A4 18/57 1/6 2/6 90 A5 14/0 1/0 2/0 17 B1 373/392 49/45 45/49 953 B2 0/380 0/46 0/48 474 C1 95/52 12/6 12/6 183 C2 78/80 10/10 10/10 198 D1 0/17 0/3 0/4 24 D2 147/142 18/16 20/19 362 D3 161/158 18/17 19/19 392 D4 159/156 19/20 20/19 393 total 1573/1562 192/183 195/194 3899 OUT SOURCES ASC LSIL HSIL NEG E1 55 96 58 99 F1 40 5 7 99 C3 30 15 15 100 D5 45 17 3 100 D6 47 38 3 100 LR and HR instances datasets To get the positive probability of top instances, we finetuned the classifier on the LR datasets mixed with the HR images which is based on previous work25 for training stability. The LR datasets are acquired by image registration as shown in Table 3. We align HR and LR instances of the same region and acquired a small-scale finetuning datasets as cytopathologist annotators cannot directly label resolution limited images. We get 7,599 positive instances contain at least one positive cell and get 65,700 negative instances as training set, since negative samples are easy to be acquired by cropping from negative WSIs. For HR instances, we just resize the HR instances to the same size of LR instances and put it into model at certain proportion when training. We randomly crop instances form train set LRWSIs to make our SSL datasets. We made used of about 239, 000 samples for our pretraining process. We have also obtained a set of a LR instance datasets specifically designed for testing purposes, which contains 767 positive instances and 767 negative instances. The LR test set serves not only as a critical resource for validating the first-stage instance-level classifier, but also as an essential foundation for creating t-SNE maps in our research presentations. Table 3. Detail of LR instances datasets name Num of positive instances Num of negative instances total LR pretrain set \ \ 239000 LR train set 7599 65700 73299 LR test set 767 767 1534 Fully annotated WSI To statistic different proportion of positive foreground in histology and cytology WSIs, we made use of Camelyon16 and a cytology WSI dataset. Camelyon16 is a fully annotated open- source dataset which contains 110 standard histology WSIs. Our cytology WSI dataset is a standard cytology WSI dataset which contains 106 fully annotated standard WSIs. We first calculate the foreground area by threshold segmentation using Hue, Saturation, Value in Hexcone Model, which removes all background region without information. Then we calculate the area of the positive foreground using annotation. The final proportion of positive foreground is the ratio of the latter to the former. Pretraining and finetuning details For all training steps, we resized the original instance of 256 × 256 pixels to 224 × 224 pixels as the input of model. Patch size is 16 × 16 pixels. We use ViT-B as the backbone for all experiments. The optimizer is Adam37 with 5% warm up and cosine like learning rate decay. The ViT backbone mentioned in this paper is without class token. Att-Transformer Att-Transformer is a more concise version of ViT which connects embedding layer, ViT backbone and a Gated-Attention module. The Gated-attention module is defined as: 𝑍= ∑𝑎𝑘 𝐾 𝑘=1 ℎ𝑘 (1) 𝑎𝑘= exp (𝑊𝑇(tanh(𝑉ℎ𝑘 𝑇) ⊙𝑠𝑖𝑔𝑚(𝑈ℎ𝑘 𝑇))) ∑ exp (𝑊𝑇(tanh(Vℎ𝑗 𝑇) ⊙𝑠𝑖𝑔𝑚(𝑈ℎ𝑗 𝑇))) 𝐾 𝑗=1 (2) The ℎ𝑘∈𝑅1 × 768 is a token of the output of ViT backbone and 𝑍∈𝑅768 is the merged feature after Gated-Attention module. ⊙ is element-wise multiplication. 𝑠𝑖𝑔𝑚 and 𝑡𝑎𝑛ℎ are the sigmoid non-linearity and hyperbolic tangent function. 𝑈∈𝑅𝑑 × 768, 𝑉∈𝑅𝑑 × 768, 𝑊∈𝑅1 ×𝑑 are learnable parameters. It should be highlighted that this architecture helps to get interpretable results as the attention scores 𝑎𝑘 can explicitly rather than implicitly display the process of feature aggregation. More importantly, it’s a better design to fit positive foreground sparse input like cytology cervical data. In the first stage, we use position embedding to model the position order of patches in ViT, but do not use it in the second stage because there is no specific order in feature sequence. This design also helps to process length-free input. Pretraining method To support a stable pretraining on our Att-Transformer, we design two stage SSL strategy. We first train the encoder the same as mask auto-encoder34 on unlabeled LR dataset. We use 50% mask rate and ImageNet pretrain weight to initialize the model. We pretrained the model on 239k unlabeled instances for 100 epochs. Then, we add the Gated-Attention module after the encoder to further pretrain the model. {ℎ1, ℎ2, … , ℎ196} = 𝐸𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑑𝑒𝑟(𝑃𝑜𝑠𝑖𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛(𝐸(𝐼𝑖𝑛))) (3) {𝑎1, 𝑎2, … , 𝑎196}, ℎ0 = 𝐺𝑎𝑡𝑒𝑑({ℎ1, ℎ2, … , ℎ196}) (4) {𝑧1, 𝑧2, … , 𝑧196} = 𝐷𝑒𝑐𝑜𝑑𝑒𝑟(𝑃𝑜𝑠𝑖𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛({𝑣0, ℎ0})) (5) 𝐿𝑜𝑠𝑠= 𝑀𝑆𝐸(𝑅𝑒𝑐𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟({𝑧1,𝑧2, … , 𝑧196}), 𝐼𝑖𝑛) (6) 𝐸𝑛𝑐𝑜𝑑𝑒𝑟, 𝐷𝑒𝑐𝑜𝑑𝑒𝑟 denote to ViT backbone of MAE encoder and decoder respectively. 𝐸, 𝑃𝑜𝑠𝑖𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 denote to embedding layer and position embedding which converts image 𝐼𝑖𝑛 into token sequences {ℎ1, ℎ2, … , ℎ196}. 𝐺𝑎𝑡𝑒𝑑 denotes to Gated-Attention module which get merged feature ℎ0 and attention scores {𝑎1, 𝑎2, … , 𝑎196} for patches. After inserting the Gated-Attention module, the output embeddings are weighted summed as ℎ0. As a result, the input 196 tokens of decode become ℎ0 and 195 mask tokens as 𝑣0. 𝑅𝑒𝑐𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟 denotes dimensional transformation from feature sequence to image. We use MSE loss between original whole image and the recovered image for the second stage pretraining which means we do not mask any token. The second stage number of epoch is also 100. Fine-tune method Our instance classifier is composed of an embedding layer, ViT backbone, Gated-attention layer and a linear layer. We use the SSL pretrained weights to initialize the model. We first finetune our model on LR and HR mixed dataset as mentioned in description of datasets by cross entropy loss. During the training process, the data of each epoch consists of LR instances, HR instances, and hard samples which are acquired by hard sample mining which will be mentioned later. We dynamically adjust the proportion of the training data to obtain a stable model. We keep the balance of positive and negative samples, and control a small proportion of hard samples (< 5%) to prevent the model from collapsing. Train slide classifier For slide classifier, the Att-Transformer is without position embedding as there is no specific order in feature sequence. We first acquire the top 200 instances of each LRWSIs in train set without using data augmentation. Then, we enhance instances by data augmentation before extracting merged feature of each instance when training slide classifier. The input of our slide classifier is the merged feature of top 200 instances in a slide. We simply use cross entropy loss to optimize the model. Hard sample mining For whole slide images, positive instances are much valuable than negative samples. The negative samples can be easily cropped from negative slides. At the same time, the positive instances are usually rare in positive slides. More importantly, there is a gradual change between normal cells and typical cancerous cells. As a result, there are always false positive instances when we use well- trained model to infer all the instances in a negative slide. These false positive instances are called hard samples in our work. In order to make full use of the large number of negative instances to draw a more efficient classification boundary and further train a robust instance classifier, we use hard sample mining strategy. We first train our model on labeled instance dataset to get a primary classifier. We then infer instances from negative slides and collect the hard samples. These hard samples will be used as training data for next stage training. We found it’s a good strategy to build a more robust model on negative slides. Data augmentation We use augmentation in our former work25 for every stage of training which contain two pretrain stages, supervised training stage and slide level classifier training. We do not use augmentation in the ablation experiments comparing three different feature extraction methods. We also do not use data augmentation for other methods in the ablation experiments comparing different feature aggregating methods. The augmentation used is based on our former work25 which is composed by RandomScale, RandomSharp, RandomGaussainBlur, RandomHSVDisturb, RandomRGBSwitch, RandomRotate and RandomFlip. We do not use any crop and resize style augmentation as the relative size is an important feature for detecting abnormal cervical cells. Computational hardware and software The operation system of the server is Linux version 5.4.0-150-generic. The server is equipped with four NVIDIA TITAN V graphics processing unit (GPU) and Intel(R) Core (TM) i9-9940X CPU @ 3.30GHz. CUDA Version is 11.4. The python version is 3.8.16. Other libraries mainly are torch 2.0.1, torchvision 0.15.2, opencv-python 1.2.0, pillow 9.5.0 and openslide-python 1.2.0. Comparison methods We compared our method with CALM (GitHub - mahmoodlab/CLAM: Data-efficient and weakly supervised computational pathology on whole slide images - Nature Biomedical Engineering) and TransMIL (GitHub - szc19990412/TransMIL: TransMIL: Transformer based Correlated Multiple Instance Learning for Whole Slide Image Classification). For CALM, we choose the single- attention-branch version for experiments. We also make used of the encoder on CLAM project to extract the ImageNet feature of instances. Data availability The original LRWSI and annotation data are private and are not publicly available since the protection of patients’ privacy in cooperative hospitals. We released demonstration data, which can be checked at ShenghuaCheng/high-risk-Cervical-Precancerous-Screening (github.com). All data supporting the findings of this study are available on requests for non-commercial and academic purposes from the primary corresponding author (xlliu@mail.hust.edu.cn). Source data are provided with this paper. Code availability We release code, demonstration data and well-trained weights for the first and second stage model in Python using PyTorch as the primary deep-learning library, which is available at ShenghuaCheng/high-risk-Cervical-Precancerous-Screening (github.com). All source code has been released under the GNU GPLv3 free software license. Acknowledgements This work is supported by the NSFC projects (grant 62375100, 62201221), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (grant 2021M701320, 2022T150237), Guangzhou Science and Technology Project (grant 2024A04J4960) and Southern Medical University Research Start-up Foundation. X.L.L., G.R., and N.L. scanned the slides. S. C., J.B., H. Y. and X. L.L. managed the data. J.B., H.Y., S. C., X.L. and S.L. performed the image analysis and processing.
