Read Full License Additional Declarations: No competing interests reported. 1 Learning Neighborhood-Scale Cross-Dependencies Among Air Pollutants, Meteorology and Land Cover Using Mobile Sensing and Transformers D. Nissenbaum1, S. Bagon2, R. Sarafian1, C. C. Womack3,4, O. Sapir1, S.S. Brown4,5, Y. Rudich1 1Earth and Planetary Sciences Department, Weizmann Institute of Science, Rehovot, 7610001, Israel 2Core AI Research Center, Weizmann Institute of Science, Rehovot, 7610001, Israel 3Cooperative Institute for Research in Environmental Sciences, University of Colorado, Boulder, CO 80309, USA 4Chemical Sciences Laboratory, National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, Boulder, CO 80305, USA 5Department of Chemistry, University of Colorado, Boulder, CO 80309, USA Correspondence to: Y. Rudich (yinon.rudich@weizmann.ac.il). Abstract. This study integrates high-resolution mobile air-quality sensing with a transformer-based masked autoencoder to explore fine-scale spatial relationships among pollutants, meteorology, and land cover across the Weizmann Institute campus. Using a custom-built miniaturized broadband cavity- enhanced spectrometer (mBBCEAS) for NO₂, along with PM₁, PM₂.₅, O₃, and meteorological sensors, the authors conducted 66 mobile surveys across the study area. A transformer-based masked autoencoder, pretrained on synthetic data, accurately reconstructed full pollutant and meteorological fields from only 25% of the data (R²=0.89) and correctly classified concentration and meteorological intensity levels into ten categories (F1=92.9% with one-bin tolerance), revealing that local land cover and wind conditions strongly modulate pollutant gradients over tens of meters. Attention analysis uncovered previously hidden spatial patterns and key variables, offering a data-driven basis for optimizing urban air-quality sampling strategies. 1. Introduction Air pollution ranks among the leading causes of premature mortality globally, accounting for millions of deaths annually and contributing to severe respiratory disease, cardiovascular illness, and adverse pregnancy outcomes.1,2 Nevertheless, precise estimation of air pollution temporal and spatial distribution is challenged by the high complexity of atmospheric chemistry and dynamics, such as sources and sinks that are difficult to accurately quantify and uncertain meteorology estimations, especially at fine spatial scales.2,3 For example, fossil fuel combustion is a typical large local source of Nitrogen Oxides (NOx = NO, NO2), and organic compounds.4 NO2 and VOC interact in the presence of UV radiation and produce ozone, which will react with either NO to produce more NO2, or with NO2 to produce the highly reactive NO3 under limited light conditions.4,5 These species can further react with other atmospheric constituents in highly temperature-dependent reactions, making their exact quantification sensitive to the source pollution characterization and environmental conditions. Particularly at the neighborhood scale, where local factors such as vegetation, roads, and buildings can substantially influence pollutant distributions over tens of meters, typical uncertainties in state-of-the- art methods can result in errors exceeding 40% in estimated concentrations and R2 of 0.6 or lower.6–10 For example, urban and suburban variations in particulate matter have been linked to localized removal processes, such as deposition on vegetation canopies and other available surfaces, which can vary substantially across different urban settings.11 For NOₓ, pollutant distributions are dominated by the proximity and intensity of emission sources, such as major roadways, rather than by removal processes like deposition. Nonetheless, studies comparing urban and rural sites found that areas with greater impervious and vegetated surface area exhibit enhanced deposition of nitrogen and sulfur species, 2 highlighting the additional role of fine-scale surface and canopy properties in shaping overall pollutant dynamics.12 The current main mapping strategies at fine scales involve measurements from either a stationary network of low-cost sensors or mobile platforms, and predicting continuous pollutant concentration fields based on statistical, machine-learning or physical models integrating the measurements.3,8,9,13–15 However, stationary networks often require frequent calibrations,3 while mobile monitoring requires repeating surveying and is often limited by the measurement system’s performance. Concretely, there are currently no lightweight and affordable commercially available NO2 sensors able to measure in less than 1 ppb precision and 1 Hz temporal resolution, as often required for mobile platforms.16 As demonstrated in other studies, an optical miniaturized broadband cavity enhanced spectrometer (mBBCEAS) can be used for measuring NO2 with such performance.16–18 In addition to exposure analysis, highly resolved mapping of pollutant distributions is important for studying their complex dynamics and interdependencies. For example, unexpected hotspots can point to important local sources, and spatial correlations can reveal coupled interactions. Common ways to explore such site-specific dependencies are to observe pollutant statistics and either correlate them or train simple regression models to predict study pollutants based on the others.19–21 Often, the models used are insufficiently complicated and capture only linear or limited inter-pollutant dependencies, which might conceal important non-linear interactions. In contrast, deep-learning models can capture such complex interactions,22 but they are often considered “black-boxes” as their interpertation can be difficult. Transformer-based artificial neural networks,23 often used in Large Language Models (LLMs) and computer vision (ViT) tasks,24–26 offer convenient explainability based on their propagated attention.27–29 In many implementations, transformers acts as autoencoders that encode a sequence of input tokens to predict a corresponding sequence of targets, allowing effective learning even when most of the input (typically 50–75%) is masked, as in image reconstruction tasks.30–32 Attention-based interpretability techniques enable examination of the model’s learned attention patterns, providing insights into the information the model considers most relevant for prediction.29 However, a major challenge of transformer networks is their typical requirement for a huge amount of data.26,33 In this study, we developed an mBBCAS instrument for NO2 measurements, integrated it with a mobile monitoring system,34 and repeatedly surveyed the Weizmann Institute campus as a test site across three different seasons. Using a transformer-based network trained to impute pollutant concentrations and meteorological variables for 75% of the monitoring locations from only 25% of the observations, we quantify hyper-local pollutant distribution and interdependencies in the site. To address the limited number of surveys, the model was tailored for an imputation task involving patch encoding, cross-token reasoning, and image-like decoding. Pretraining on synthetic surveys accelerated learning of the encoding and decoding stages, allowing fine-tuning on real data to focus primarily on the reasoning of real-world physical relationships. The results highlight the potential of mobile measurements followed by transformer analysis for the study of city-scale air pollution assessment. 2. Results and Discussion A total of 66 repeated mobile surveys were conducted across the Weizmann Institute of Science campus, Israel, during three seasons of 2024 using a mobile monitoring system installed on a compact electric golf car (Fig. 1).34 The system includes the custom NO₂ mBBCEAS,16,18 ozone monitor (POM),35 a particulate matter counter (POPS)36 and meteorological sensors. Each survey covered 17 predefined stops across the 1.1 km² campus, collecting over 180,000 georeferenced observations. Land-cover features were extracted from aerial imagery using a pretrained semantic segmentation transformer to quantify buildings, roads, vegetation, and open terrain around each point. These measurements were then used to train a transformer-based masked autoencoder designed to impute missing pollutant and 3 meteorological patches from only 25% of the observed data (see Methods). Inter-variable relationships were analyzed through mean patterns, correlations, and model-derived attention-based relevancy maps, which reveal where and how interactions occur across the campus, emphasizing fine-scale land-cover effects on the distributions of short-lived species. Figure 1: Campaign Details. Top: the miniaturized broadband cavity enhanced spectrometer (mBBCEAS) design. Bottom, left: high-resolution aerial image of the site, including the 17 stops and 2 construction sites (red circles). The map scale in meters is shown in the bottom-left corner. Bottom, right: the system installed on the golf-car, at one of the stops. 2.1. General Spatiotemporal Distributions Figure 2 illustrates survey-wise means and standard deviations at each route point, revealing spatial distribution patterns and temporal variability. Example surveys and the detailed meteorology maps are shown in Fig. S1, showing uneven wind distributions (up to ±2ms-1 deviations), possibly due to car- related turbulence, local topography, and weak winds at the stops. As shown later, despite the inherent noise in these measurements, the wind data retain sufficient signal to enable the extraction of meaningful information using advanced deep learning models. 4 Figure 2: Measured Spatiotemporal Variations. Survey-wise averages (top) and standard deviations (bottom), showing (left to right): NO2, O3, Ox=NO2+O3, PM2.5, PM1 distributions across the map, with GPS coordinates indicated along the axes. The rightmost panels show meteorological summaries: relative humidity (RH) maps, annotated spatial temperature and pressure averages, as well as winds at the 17 stops (reference arrow = 1 ms-1). Grid lines are spaced 200 m apart. Figure 2 shows the spatial distributions of the surveys’ average (first row), describing the average spatial distributions, and standard deviation (second row), showing the temporal variance of the maps. Each column shows a different pollutant, and the meteorology is compactly shown on the rightmost column with the spatial means of both temperature and pressure annotated in the boxes. The wind vectors are shown at the 17 stops defining the repeating route (Fig. 1). Ox is calculated from O3+NO2 mixing ratios. Figure S2 shows the same maps but binned into three-hour segments. NO2 shows high spatial distribution, where the concentrations in some areas are two times higher on average, mostly near the city’s main street to the west. The early morning north-east side (Fig. S2) is typically very polluted (10-15 ppb), possibly corresponding to high traffic on the highway or emissions from a nearby cement production plant and typical morning weak eastwardly winds. The city traffic intensifies later in the west (Fig. S2, second row), enhancing NO2 and PM2.5 concentrations in adjacent regions. NO2 concentrations remain relatively low after these hours as ozone concentrations rise, likely due to the photolytic conversion of NO2 to NO+O3. Ozone always presents a relatively uniform spatial distribution, but some areas show stronger temporal variances, such as the northern parts. As the winds are typically weak across the entire site, these variations can be the result of local sources and the adjacent highway and roads. Ozone concentrations significantly rise until noon, as expected.4,37,38 Ox levels are relatively uniform in space but exhibit a diurnal range of ~20 ppb, suggesting O3 production without loss of NO2, possibly through VOC oxidation pathways, particularly around noon. This supports odd-oxygen use as a proxy for ozone generation that integrates over short-term and small-scale fluctuations. Aerosol concentrations, and particularly PM2.5, exhibit two localized hotspots that correspond to the 2 construction sites (Fig. 1). Interestingly, when the relative humidity is high (usually during the early mornings), these hotspots dissipate. It is possible that, as observed during the surveys, construction-related sand piles became moist during the humid nights, making them less prone to wind- driven resuspension, in addition to the lack of activity at these sites during nighttime. PM₁ levels are typically higher in the morning, especially toward the northern area during the early hours. Concentrations of both particle modes decrease from noon to evening, even as wind speeds increase. The results point to mixed influences: local commercial, train and traffic-related emissions resembling those causing the northern NO₂ enhancement, nighttime accumulation from the east potentially linked to the nearby Nesher cement plant (~8 km east), and later dilution driven by strengthening westerly winds. While winds are generally weak throughout the site, the relative humidity exhibits a clear spatial 5 pattern that varies mainly over time in the northern region, possibly due to vegetation and irrigation. The spatially averaged diurnal trends of pollutants and meteorological variables (Fig. S3) are generally consistent with those observed at nearby regional stations (see Methods), with NO₂ exhibiting the largest deviations, reaching mean differences exceeding 50%. This highlights the impact of local, small- scale sources, especially for short-lived species. Land cover indices were obtained using a pretrained transformer network for semantic segmentation (Fig. S4, Methods section), revealing the diverse land- use types across the study area. Vegetation indices are higher in the southwest and east of the study area. Figure 3: Pair-Wise Pearson Correlations. Spatiotemporal correlations of the variables calculated across all surveys. We further analyze the pair-wise correlations (Eq. S3) of the locally measured variables (Fig. 3), with additional regional correlations shown in Fig. S5. The correlations are computed using all valid data points from all surveys. NO2 shows the expected anticorrelation with O3,4,38 while NO₂ and PM share some spatial features and possible sources. Higher relative humidity correlates with lower O₃ and NO₂ but higher PM, and the PM–RH correlations, stronger for PM₁, indicate hygroscopic particle growth under moist conditions. Winds show very weak correlations due to high variability and noise. The regional eastward component (U, Fig. S5) correlates positively with radiation, ozone, and temperature, and weakly anticorrelates with NO₂, suggesting that photochemical formation and sea-breeze transport enhance daytime ozone and promote NO₂ depletion, alongside local deposition to vegetation. Local NO₂ correlates strongly with regional NO and NO₂ (Fig. S3 and S5), though less so than local ozone is with regional ozone, reflecting its shorter lifetime and spatial variability driven by local sources. PM₁ correlates more strongly with meteorology than PM₂.₅, and both show strong associations. A spatial breakdown (Fig. S6) shows strong local–regional NO₂ correlations, weakening near emission sources and construction sites, with similar patterns for NO₂–PM and NO₂–O₃ anticorrelations. While the correlation analysis shows no linear relationships between land-cover indices and measured variables, the following transformer analysis reveals that these indices encode fine-scale information relevant to pollutant dynamics. 2.2. Deep Learning Model Analysis To reveal complex non-linear and cross-variable relationships influencing the fine-scale spatiotemporal distribution of pollutants, we trained a transformer model to reconstruct target feature maps from input feature maps observed at only 25% of the locations. Analysis of the trained model reveals intricate non- linear interdependencies among variables, made accessible through the deep learning–based approach. A detailed description of the model and analysis is provided in Methods. 6 Figure 4: Sample Model Outputs. A sample survey task from the validation set is shown for representative features. Each row presents: input sparse patches (top), target maps (middle), and model output maps (bottom). Columns correspond to different features, from left to right: NO₂, O₃, PM₂.₅, PM₁, and RH. A sample input–output task is shown in Fig. 4, where 25% of the map patches per feature serve as inputs (top row), the remaining 75% as targets (middle), and the reconstructed outputs appear below. Thirteen input and seven target features were used (see Methods). The model successfully reconstructs most spatial details using only 25% of data, with prediction quality varying by feature and spatial region. For example, PM₁ is well predicted in the north-central area (31.908° N, 34.814° E), where NO₂ tends to be overestimated. Local sources such as the northern construction site (31.910° N, 34.811° E) are less predictable. Additional examples (Fig. S7) show successful long-path reconstructions (e.g., RH) and challenges near anomalies such as low O₃ by the train station or PM₂.₅ near the construction site. Some errors may stem from the POM’s cross-sensitivity to highly-concentrated aromatic compounds (e.g., benzene, phenol) emitted from diesel train fuel, introducing measurement noise beyond the model’s ability to learn.35 Model performance was evaluated using both the coefficient of determination (R2, Eq. 2), quantifying how well the continuous predictions capture variance in the observations, and precision, recall, and F1 scores from a 10-bin discretised classification task. These scores evaluate how well the model classifies “low” to “extreme” values across ten quantile-based bins (Eq. 3-5). The confusion matrix in Fig. 5 (top) summarizes predicted versus true intensity categories from ~180,000 validation samples, while the per- feature relative error maps (bottom) highlight the most challenging regions. The sharp diagonal in the confusion matrix shows that most predictions match their true classes, with errors usually within one neighboring class and no clear bias. Slight unevenness in class counts reflects the quantile-based definitions and small dataset size. Despite having different distributions, the model performs robustly across datasets, with precision, recall, and F1 of 80-81%, 93% with one-class tolerance and R²=0.89. Table 1 (Methods) summarizes results for different architectures and training schemes. Fig. 5 (bottom) shows per-feature error maps comparing predictions with true values, where red indicates overestimation. Although NO₂ exhibits moderate linear correlations with other variables (Fig. 3), it remains the hardest pollutant to predict. Systematic underestimations occur in specific areas, while overestimations are more scattered, reflecting the rapid spatiotemporal variability of NO₂ driven by local emissions, chemical interactions, and surface deposition. Expanding the dataset should reduce these errors, as infrequent instrument malfunctions limited NO₂ observations. For PM2.5, additional underestimations appear at the construction sites, contrasting with the model’s stronger performance for PM₁. The surveys spanned different periods, some preceding construction activity, which may explain inconsistencies at those sites. Differences between particle modes likely reflect the distinct physical and chemical properties of construction dust, highlighting the limitations of interpreting size distributions 7 alone without complementary information on particle morphology or composition. Other features are more predictable, especially the meteorological variables. On average, the model shows no spatial bias and it performs consistently across the surveyed area. R2 maps (Fig. S8) show similar patterns. These results underscore the importance of fine-scale monitoring, as localized conditions strongly influence pollutant behavior and variability. Figure 5: Model Performance. Top: confusion matrix, indicating classification counts of predictions versus target values discretised into 10 magnitude values for all patch-feature outputs in the validation set. Bottom: continuous relative errors showing the per-feature performance and failure points of the model, where negative values are under-estimations (blue) and positive are over-estimations (red). The top-left panel shows the average relative error across all feature predictions. The performance is evaluated over the full validation dataset. 2.3. Attention-Driven Interdependencies In this section, we analyze input feature relevancies for the target prediction task. A key advantage of transformer networks is their attention-driven interpretability, which reveals how attention propagates through layers. Conceptually, the attention matrix answers: “When predicting a target at row i, how much does the model attend to an input at column j?”. Using the method detailed in Methods, we compute the layer-propagated attention and express it as relevancy scores. The resulting relevancy is represented as a matrix of shape [ܨ௧௚⋅ܰ௧௚, ܨ௜௡⋅ܰ௜௡], where ܨ௧௚and ܨ௜௡are the numbers of target and input features (7 and 13, respectively), and ܰ௧௚and ܰ௜௡are the numbers of patches on the map (both 202 for the validation set, yielding a matrix of 1,414 × 2,626). This matrix quantifies how relevant each input feature-patch is for predicting each target feature-patch. This is rearranged to a tensor of shape [ܨ௧௚, ܰ௧௚, ܨ௜௡, ܰ௜௡]. Examples for three target patches are shown in Fig S9, illustrating how the model captures fine-scale spatial and cross-variable dependencies. For instance, localized PM₁ patterns contribute to PM₂.₅ prediction and spatial temperature structures provide context for NO₂ variability. The scores are first averaged over the full dataset and then spatially across the target dimension to obtain generalized relevancy scores. We then average across the input spatial dimension to produce a [ܨ௧௚, ܨ௜௡] 8 matrix representing the overall relevance of each input feature to each target feature (Fig. 6, top). Figure 6 (bottom) presents the input relevancy maps before spatial averaging, illustrating the spatial distribution of input relevance for the general prediction task. Figure 6: Feature Relevancies. Top: averaged across the spatial dimensions, showing the relevance of input features (columns) for predicting target features (rows). Bottom: averaged across the targets, showing the average spatial relevancy of each input, with the top-left being the average map of all input features. Figure 6 (top) shows the spatially averaged relevancy tensor, indicating which input features (columns) the model attends to when predicting each target (rows). A strong diagonal pattern indicates that most information is contained within the same variable. However, despite having weak linear correlations (Fig. 3), land-cover indices and winds emerge as highly influential, encoding fine-scale information essential for describing pollutant distributions. This example highlights the added value of attention- based analysis compared to traditional correlation methods. Roads and winds are broadly relevant, vegetation is most important for short-lived NO₂ and PM₂.₅, and buildings mainly for PM₂.₅. This suggests that the model effectively encodes detailed land-cover characteristics within each patch and its surrounding context, capturing fine-scale spatial information that contributes to its predictive accuracy. The open terrain index was found to be less relevant. Given the use of a single static land-cover map, the indices may implicitly supply physics-aware context that enables the model to differentiate between otherwise similar patches. Figure 6 (bottom) shows the spatial distribution of input relevancy, highlighting information hotspots per feature and combined (top-left). The model attends to specific patches more than others (e.g. the southern residential areas and the northern construction site), where small variations may carry important information. High NO2 relevancy is given to the eastern region, associated with higher average concentrations and high variance (Fig. 2). Ozone relevancy appears in three distinct latitude bands across the site (34.811°E, 34.816°E and 34.819°E). The eastern region, with low mean but high temporal variability, likely becomes important during less frequent or transient events with strong deviations. PM2.5 concentrations at the construction sites are less relevant, probably due to their high variability that makes them unpredictable, while PM1 remains informative throughout 9 the map, highlighting size-related differences. Winds are surprisingly informative despite their inherent noise, with relevancy concentrated mainly at the stops but extending beyond them. This underscores the value of adding stationary stops along the route and demonstrates the feasibility of using wind sensors on a moving vehicle, provided appropriate noise filtering is applied. To further investigate the factors driving these relevancies, we correlate them with the spatial magnitudes (per-location survey-wise averages and standard deviations) of the features (Eq. S1–S5), as shown in Fig. 7. Figure 7: Relevancy Correlation with Feature Magnitudes. Top: spatial correlations between the average feature relevancies and the feature means (left) or feature standard deviations (right). Bottom: spatial correlations of relevancies computed separately for each target feature (Eq. S1-S5). Figure 7 shows how relevancies vary with survey-wise means (left) and standard deviations (right), indicating whether input features are most informative under high magnitudes or variability. For NO₂, PM₂.₅ is most relevant where its concentrations and temporal fluctuations are high, and temperature is most informative in cooler and more variable regions. To predict ozone, relevant patches are where NO2 and temperature are low, humidity is high and wind arrives from the east. Interestingly, these differ from typical high-ozone conditions of low humidity and high temperature (Fig. 3), as seen often.34 This suggests the model detects informative regions where typical correlations weaken, revealing local effects. Patches with high PM₂.₅ concentrations or variability are highly relevant for predicting NO₂ and pressure, while PM₁ relevance correlates with its variability rather than its concentration, indicating that fluctuating regions carry finer predictive information. Higher land-cover index values (e.g., vegetation or roads) correspond to higher relevancy, while their standard deviations show no effect since the maps are static. Strong anticorrelations with wind variability indicate that the model filters out noisy wind patches while still capturing the overall importance of wind features (Fig. 6). Figure 7 (top) provides a general overview of these correlations. Higher land-cover values correspond to greater relevance, emphasizing the contribution of remote sensing features. Anticorrelation with the eastward wind component (U) suggests westward winds carry key pollution information, consistent with nighttime transport, while higher wind variability reduces relevance The absence of a strong diagonal pattern indicates that feature relevance is largely independent of magnitude, aligning with the model’s unbiased predictions (Fig. 5). The lack of other strong correlations highlights the complexity of pollutant fine- scale distributions and their underlying drivers. 10 2.4. Optimal Measurement Strategy Beyond revealing where and why the model focuses through layer-propagated attention, the transformer’s flexibility in handling variable sequence lengths allows prediction from any subset of input patches. This enables optimal measurement strategies under limited resources.39 By identifying the most informative locations and features, such strategies can be tailored for previously mapped sites, as demonstrated here. Instead of randomly selecting 25% of patches, we used a small set of high- relevancy locations to represent the entire fields and imputed the rest. Figure S10 compares our model with a benchmark K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) model, which computes spatially weighted averages of the K nearest patches (K=6 yielded the best results), as a function of the number of selected patches per feature (M). Two selection strategies were tested: choosing the M most relevant patches per feature, and selecting M patches from the averaged relevancy map (Fig. 6, bottom, first panel). In terms of R2, our model outperforms the benchmark in both cases, with the uniformly selected patch approach performing best. The 1-bin tolerance F1 score is also higher for the uniform strategy up to about M=25 patches, beyond which both strategies marginally surpass the benchmark. A representative case with M=15 (Fig. S11) demonstrates that strong performance is maintained even with only 15 points across the site. In this study, we developed and deployed a mobile monitoring system integrating a custom-built miniaturized broadband cavity-enhanced spectrometer (mBBCEAS) for NO₂ alongside complementary sensors for O₃, PM₁, PM₂.₅ and meteorological variables. Sixty-six mobile surveys across the Weizmann Institute campus yielded over 180,000 georeferenced observations spanning three seasons. A transformer-based masked autoencoder was trained to impute 75% missing data, enabling analysis of fine-scale interdependencies among pollutants, meteorology, and land cover. Key findings include: 1. Fine-scale spatial variability of pollutants, particularly the short-lived NO₂ and PM, shows sharp gradients of up to a factor of two over tens of meters. 2. Land cover and local wind conditions strongly influence pollutant distributions, with buildings, roads, and vegetation indices being the most influential factors across the test site. 3. Diurnal variations of pollutants show strong meteorological dependencies, with NO₂ peaking during early-morning traffic and decreasing through daytime photochemistry, O₃ rising toward midday, and PM₁ concentrations increasing under humid morning conditions. 4. A transformer model imputing missing data reconstructed fine-scale pollutant and meteorological maps from only 25% of inputs (R² ≈ 0.89) and classified intensity levels with high accuracy (F1 ≈ 92.9% within one-bin tolerance). Attention analysis revealed physically meaningful feature relevance even with fewer than 100 surveys. 5. The transformer captures complex non-linear dependencies among pollutants, meteorology, and land-cover despite weak linear correlations, while filtering noisy inputs. 6. Relevancy scores highlight the value of stationary stops for wind measurements and the potential of portable wind sensing with proper noise filtering. 7. Synthetic pretraining sped up convergence (from >1,000 to tens of epochs) and improved generalization, compensating for limited field data. 8. Attention-based relevancy maps enable efficient monitoring by pinpointing the most informative sampling locations while maintaining high predictive performance. The combination of mobile sensing with transformer-based analysis demonstrates strong potential for capturing fine-scale air pollution variability and cross-variable dependencies. However, the current study is constrained by several practical limitations. The dataset was relatively small compared to those typically required for training state-of-the-art transformer architectures, limiting coverage for some features, and the use of a single static land-cover map restricted examination of seasonal or temporal 11 variations in surface characteristics. Spatial averaging to a fixed grid emphasized smooth patterns but may have reduced sensitivity to finer gradients. Future work should expand the dataset in both time and space and add dynamic remote-sensing imagery (e.g. drone-based). Additional sensors capable of measuring VOCs and NO would be valuable for achieving a more complete chemical characterization, highlighting the need for continued development of compact instruments for these species. Shorter survey routes and regional data could further improve temporal and spatial coverage. Further analysis of particulate number-size distributions and seasonal trends may also provide deeper source insights. Synthetic data generation could be refined using physics-based dependencies or varied feature sets. The approach can be extended to temporal forecasting, multi-site generalization, and real-time route optimization. This work demonstrates that transformer-based masked autoencoders, along with high- resolution mobile sensing, provide a powerful and interpretable framework for studying neighborhood- scale pollutant interdependencies and optimizing urban air-quality monitoring strategies. 3. Methods The methods used in the work combine in-situ mobile mapping with deep learning analysis to resolve neighborhood-scale interdependencies analysis, with the approach designed to emphasize model interpretability. Survey data were spatially averaged per survey using a 5 m Gaussian kernel to reduce route-dependent variability and generate aligned input maps. A transformer-based masked autoencoder was pretrained on synthetic data and then fine-tuned on field surveys to impute missing values. Attention analysis was used to interpret the model’s learned dependencies. 3.1. Data Acquisition: Portable NO2 BBCEAS, Site Surveying and Remote Sensing The portable monitoring system is composed of a customizable suite of instruments installed on a compact electric golf car, described in previous work.34 For this project, we measured ozone (POM),35 aerosol size distributions for PM1 and PM2.5 (POPS),36 temperature, pressure and humidity (iMet- XQ2),40 horizontal wind vector (FT742, designed for portability),41 and a GPS.42,43 Mass concentrations were calculated using typical densities of 1.5 g/cm3 measured at our lab.44 The wind vector is calculated by subtracting the measured velocity from the measured wind. The maximum speed of the car was 25 kmh-1. In addition, we developed a portable, highly precise NO2 optical sensor (Fig. 1, top), with 0.43 ppb precision at 10 seconds integration time. The apparatus of operation is an inline BBCEAS,16,18 composed of a pair of 450nm-centered mirrors with reflectivity of above 99.994% at 450nm (FiveNines Optics),45 a 0.1 liters volume PTFE cavity of length 15.9 cm and cavity air being replaced every 3 seconds, a portable spectrometer (QEPro),46 and a 1-Watts 455nm-centered LED source (M455L4),47 current-controlled (LDD-1321)48 and thermally controlled (TEC-1019).49 The instrument structure is composed of PLA 3D-printed parts and carbon fiber rods, it weighs less than three kg and is designed for drone-based portable monitoring. In addition, odd-oxygen (Ox=O3+NO2) is often used as a proxy for ozone production quantification,50 and we explore its spatiotemporal distribution as well (Fig. 1, S1, S2). We additionally use reference freely available stationary datasets acquired by the Israeli Environmental Protection Ministry at the closest Rehovot station (NO, NO2, O3, and Ox=NO2+O3), and by the Israeli Meteorological Service (global radiation consisting of both scattered and direct radiation, wind vector, P, T and RH). The test site (Fig. 1, bottom) is at the Weizmann Institute of Science campus, Rehovot, Israel, located between Rehovot city (to its west and south), commercial area and a highway (to its north and northeast), and agricultural fields (to its east). The campus spans 1.1 km², with approximately 0.27 km² designated as built area. Two construction sites were active (red circles annotated with hammers), with the eastern site beginning to operate later in the campaign. Surveys were conducted across three 2024 seasons, covering both weekdays and weekends within the same weeks: 11–13 and 15–16 March; 22– 23 and 26–28 July; and 19–22 September. The surveys were carried out in 1 to 4 hours intervals with 12 each lasting up to 42 minutes at 1-Hz resolution, yielding 69 surveys in total. Three surveys with incomplete routes caused by system failures were excluded, leaving 66 valid surveys in the dataset. Figure S12 presents the hourly and weekday/weekend breakdown of the surveys and the 182,000 accumulated observations. Each survey followed a route comprising 17 predefined stops, designed to cover most of the campus within minimal time (Fig. 1, S13a). The aerial photo shown in Fig. 1 is a high-resolution geolocated image taken three weeks before the March campaign. To extract useful information from the remote sensing image, we use a deep-learning semantic segmentation model,51 that computes land cover usage scores per pixel. Since we have only one map available with no labels, and since there already exist available pretrained models for that task, we chose a model that was pretrained on several land cover classes that yields reasonable segmentation scores for our aerial photo, based on SegFormer architecture (transformer model designed for semantic segmentation of satellite images),52 with pretrained weights available at HuggingFace.53,54 It was pretrained to classify 8 different classes: Ignore, Background, Building, Road, Water, Barren, Forest and Agricultural. To adapt the model to our site, we defined four new land-cover indices derived from the class scores assigned by the pretrained model: Buildings (same as the pretrained Building index), Roads (same as Road), Open Terrain (corresponding to the Barren class) and Vegetation (average of Forest and Agricultural indices). Example results are shown in Fig. S13b. To simplify the following imputation task, each point on the path was assigned 4 land cover scalar-features, calculated as the mean of these new indices within a ~200×200 m patch centered on the point. Example results are shown in Fig. S13c. 3.2. Surveys Imputation Using Masked Autoencoding Transformers To study the pollutant-meteorology interdependencies, we train a transformer model to fill in (impute) missing data based on a limited number of observations. All input and target features are local values that are either measured or calculated from remote sensing information (e.g. vegetation, buildings, etc.), per point on the path. The model takes in a sequence of input features at random locations on the map and predicts the values of a sequence of target features at the other locations. We define the following 13 features as input, composed of pollutants, meteorology and remote sensing indices: NO2, O3, PM2.5, PM1, T (temperature), P (pressure), RH (relative humidity), U (towards-east wind component), V (towards-north wind component), and the four Building, Roads, Open Terrain and Vegetation land cover indices. The seven target features are: NO2, O3, PM2.5, PM1, T, P, RH. Because the survey routes include overlapping path segments and minor GPS deviations, we spatially smooth the data to enhance statistical consistency per location. Measurements are smoothed using a Gaussian kernel (radius = 5 m, threshold = 10 m), after which we select the most recurrent and spatially unique coordinates across surveys. This pre-processing results in a set of available 1,248 coordinates (consistent for all valid surveys), each is a vector of 13 features. Out of the 66 valid surveys, 13 are randomly chosen for validation and left out of the training. The other set of 53 surveys define the training set, with a ratio of 80% training to 20% validation. Normalization is applied based on the training set’s spatial mean and standard deviation, per feature, to produce zero-mean, unit-variance distributions, while values above the 97.5th percentile were excluded. The training objective is to impute missing target feature maps from sparsely observed input data. During training, 25% of the maps are visible (input) and 75% are masked (to be predicted). Although a challenging task, a transformer masked auto-encoder should be able to solve it.31 Such a trained model can reveal how information about one variable is embedded in others and identify where cross-variable dependencies reside within the input maps. Considering this goal, we separate each vector of 13 input features and 7 target features into scalars that are separately introduced to the model, allowing for per- feature, per-location dependency analysis. Because transformers operate on embedded tokens rather than scalar values, one of the main challenges was designing an effective tokenization scheme to represent the separated features according to their spatial locations. Several unsuccessful attempts are discussed below, while a strategy that does work is to split the map into spatial, image-like patches on a grid and then project them using a partial convolution. We use a grid of 5-meters resolution and test 13 several patch sizes of 4, 8, 16 and 32 (corresponding to 20, 40, 80 and 160 meters). A patch size of 4 yields the best results in terms of both resolution and performance (Table 1), and Fig. S14 shows examples of these patches. Patches with less than 2 on-route points are omitted, resulting in 202 available patches for a patch size of 4 for the validation set’s grid. We explore the use of augmentation of that grid (e.g. re-gridding the maps with small random offsets before generating the patches per epoch) and show that it helps generalizing the model when followed by enough finetuning on the fixed validation grid. Out of the available patches, 25% are randomly used as input while the rest 75% are the target. As the GPU memory requirement grows with the square of the sequence length due to the attention mechanism, we use a deep transformer-encoder and a shallow transformer-decoder, allowing for better expression of the input information.31 This results in a typical input sequence length of 13×202×0.25≈656 patches and 7×202×0.75≈1,060 target patches. We introduce 1% uniformly distributed noise for further augmentation during training. The architecture shown in Fig. 8 is carefully designed for solving the task based on limited observations. Figure 8: Model Architecture. The transformer model consisting of 3 parts: a shared feature-patch tokenizer, consisting of a partial convolution and fully connected layers; the transformer block, operating on both the encoded input tokens and empty targets, scaled along with positional encoding and feature embeddings; and the shared token decoder, translating the imputed tokens back into image-like patches. Green boxes denote fully connected layers (bright green for the special partial layer), orange and brown represent input and target embeddings, and blue indicates normalized raw patches (0-mean, 1-std). 14 The input can be described as tensors of shape [B, Seqin, P × P], where B is the batch size and Seqin = ܨ௜௡∙ܰ௜௡, the sequence length that equals the number of input features (13) times the number of valid selected patches (typically totals to 656), and P × P = 16 for a flattened patch of size 4. The model consists of three main components. The first part is a shared 4-by-4 patch tokenizer that outputs [B, Seqin, T], composed of a partial-convolution layer for scaling with invalid pixels,55 followed by additional 2 fully-connected layers with a hidden dimension of 256 and final token size of T=512, separated by an intermediate LeakyReLU activation.56 This part is both sequence item- and feature- agnostic, working in parallel and separately for each patch in the batched sequences. As the tokenizer is shared with all features and patches, it projects any patch of arbitrary path and feature to the same embedding space seen by the following transformer. This approach allows for learning general and comparable representations of all paths and features, supporting the following reasoning step. To inject context information, both positional encoding and feature embedding are added to the encoded patches, so the transformer can differentiate between features and patch locations. We follow the NeRF high- dimensional positional encoding (PE) composed of sine and cosine pairs at increasing frequencies that proves to encode fine positional nuances.57 To account for the observable field of view each patch spans, we use the average positional encoding of the 4 patch corners, inspired by the Mip-NeRF approach.58 Feature embeddings (FE) are [13+1 ,512] learnable weights, 13 for the possible amount of input and target features and one additional “Empty” token for invalid patches. The positional encoding and feature embeddings are modulated by a learnable weight each, and the resulting summation is scaled by the squared root of the token size. This scaling empirically encouraged the encoder to learn meaningful embeddings other than trivially copying the input. The heart of the model is composed of transformer encoder-decoder blocks. The encoder part is a masked 16-headed, 8-layered transformer- encoder,23 which computes input embeddings from the feature-location-stamped tokens, also of shape [B, Seqin, T]. These tokens enter the 4-headed, 4-layered masked transformer-decoder block as its memory. The decoder targets are empty tokens constructed from learnable empty-token embeddings (different than the “Empty” feature embeddings), combined with similarly scaled target positional encoding and target feature embeddings, that are sequentially scaling by √ܶ, of shape [B, Seqtg, T] (typically 1,060). The transformer-decoder imputes the missing Seqtg tokens, which are than fed into the last part of the network: a shared token-decoder consisting of two fully connected layers with a hidden dimension of 256 and output dimension of P × P = 16, separated by a LeakyReLU activation. Here as well, the shared decoder decodes patches regardless of the patch they describe, making it possible to decode full patches at areas unseen by the network. This feature cannot be utilized on the real data since there are no targets in the full patch for training upon, but we exploit this feature in the pretraining phase where we generate synthetic data. See Table S1 for a summary of the different shapes for different patch sizes. Fig. S15 shows the resulting different feature embeddings on a 2-component PCA space, showing how the model differentiates between the features. The embeddings show distinct separation between the features, with P and PM2.5 appearing notably close in the embedding space. This may be explained by the high correlation PM2.5 has with its relevancy for predicting P (Fig. 7), even though they weakly anticorrelate, highlighting the model’s ability to capture site-specific relationships beyond simple linear trends. We use per-pixel MSE (mean squared error), following Masked-Autoencoder for 75% patch masking,31 as our loss function: Eq. (1) ܮ݋ݏݏ= ܯܵܧ= 1 ௡∑ (݌௜−ݐ௜)2 ௡ ௜=1 where ݌௜, ݐ௜ are the model’s continuous (regression task) predictions and targets, respectively and for all ݅∈[1, ݊] pixels in all patches and features of a dataset. The model is trained until convergence (about ~3,000 epochs), regenerating random masks in each epoch, with AdamW optimizer,59 learning rate of 5 · 10−5 and weight decay of 10−3, and a Reduce-On-Plateau scheduler with factor 0.75 and patience of 500 epochs.60 The chosen model that yielded the best results was first trained using these parameters 15 but with grid-augmentation, i.e. the grid is being redefined each epoch in an offset belongs to ± one patch size, following retraining using the same parameters but on a fixed grid. To evaluate our model, we use R2 score,61 defined as: Eq. (2) ܴ2 = 1 − ∑(௣೔−௧೔)2 ೔ ∑(௧೔−௧̂)2 ೔ where ݐ̂ = 1 ௡∑ݐ௜ ௜ is the mean of all targets. This score can indicate how well the continuous regression captures variance in the true values.61 To further evaluate the results and to address categorical correctness, i.e. how well can the model classify “low”, “moderate” or “extreme” cases, we discretise the continuous values and divide them into 10 quantile-based categories, from 0 being the lowest and 9 the highest. We can then define the common classification scores precision, recall and F1: Eq. (3) ܲݎ݁ܿ݅ݏ݅݋݊= ௧௣ ௧௣+௙௣ Eq. (4) ܴ݈݈݁ܿܽ= ௧௣ ௧௣+௙௡ Eq. (5) ܨ1 = 2 ௉௥௘௖௜௦௜௢௡−1+ோ௘௖௔௟௟−1 where ݐ݌, ݂݌, ݂݊ stand for true-positive, false-positive and false-negative result counts. In other words, for a given class, the precision is defined as the ratio of correctly predicted results out of all predictions of that class, or how often is the model correct when it predicts a given class. In that context, the recall is the ratio of true predictions out of all instances of that class, or how often does the model successfully classify that class. The later F1 score is defined as the harmonic mean of the previous scores. We generally aim for scores of 1 (or 100%) for all 3. We can compute these scores for each predicted class separately by counting the discretised values, shown in Fig. 4 (top). We also computed a relaxed metric, the F1 score with a ±1 class tolerance, which counts a prediction as correct if it falls within one class of the true value (e.g., for a target class of 5, predictions of 4, 5, or 6 are considered correct). 3.3. Pretraining on Synthetic Data, Ablation Study Conceptually, the imputation task comprises three steps: encoding spatial patches into tokens to represent the input within the transformer’s feature space; reasoning across these tokens to infer the target representations; and decoding the predicted tokens back into image-like patches. To ensure successful predictions in face of the limited number of surveys available, each step has to be carefully designed, as shown in Fig. 8. But the first and last sub-tasks for tokenization and decoding back to images can be learned on synthetic data that does not have to be physically accurate, as the physics of the site is learned in the reasoning task. In other words, to overcome some of the dataset limitations we can break down the imputation task into two steps. First, we train the tokenizer and decoder modules (alongside the transformer’s ability to model general interdependencies) using a large synthetic dataset. This approach allows us to utilize datasets as large as our computational resources permit. Additionally, in this stage we can introduce controlled paths to the tokenizer, allowing for efficient training of all weights inside the partial-convolution layer. In the same way we can also train the token decoder to decode the full patches, allowing for efficient training of all its weights as well. The next step is training the pretrained model on real data, allowing the transformer part to focus on the real interdependencies once the other parts generally work (beside minor finetuning on the real data). We compare the performance of training a model from scratch versus pretraining on synthetic data (Table 1) and show that the pretraining approach significantly improves the performance. Moreover, the pretrained models converged within only a few tens of epochs, whereas the models trained from scratch required over a thousand epochs. 16 Figure 9: Synthetic Data Example. Random base fields are generated together with additional non-linearly dependent features and a simulated survey path. Unlike the real dataset, the model is trained here to reconstruct complete patches for improved efficiency. Example shown for a patch size of 16. We first generate several base fields composed of random Gaussian structures with randomly varying covariances and then create artificial “features” as linear and non-linear combinations of these base fields, producing multiple interdependent synthetic variables. A randomly generated route is then defined across all fields to represent a simulated survey map. The path can be of arbitrary complexity with random number of curves, widths, etc. We then extract spatial patches from the simulated maps and use them as input for the model to impute the missing regions. Examples are shown in Fig. 9. A full description of the synthetic features is given in Text S1. The models were trained on synthetic data until convergence (typically ~30k epochs, regenerating random data in every epoch), with AdamW optimizer,59 learning rate of 10-4 and weight decay of 10-3, and a slow Reduce-On-Plateau scheduler with factor 0.75 and patience of 10k epochs.60 The different patch sizes tested are 4,8,16, and 32 pixels (Table 1). The loss function is the same per-pixel MSE loss described for the real-data task (Eq. 1). To improve training efficiency, we add an auxiliary auto-encoding task, i.e. predicting the input patches as well, for training over 100% of the patches in each epoch. The autoencoding loss is weighted at 4/5 and the target-imputation loss at 1/5, a ratio found empirically to yield efficient training. After pretraining, the feature embeddings are randomly re-initiated to forget synthetic interdependencies. Moreover, we used a different number of input features (10 synthetic features vs. 13 real features) to encourage the model to forget any synthetically generated false physics. Because the primary goal of the model is to generate scientific insights rather than to achieve the best possible imputation accuracy, we adopted an approach optimized for interpretability. We expect that other synthetic data generation approaches could yield similarly good performance. Other model architectures were also explored. For instance, instead of tokenizing image-like patches, we tested tokenizing individual scalar feature points using multi-layer fully connected projections into the embedding space, as well as tokenizing one-dimensional path segments. However, these alternatives did not exceed an F1 score of 68%, and we therefore adopted the image-like patch tokenization approach. Table 1 presents the ablation study results, summarizing evaluation scores for different patch sizes, training strategies (from scratch vs. with pretraining), and the use or omission of grid-based data augmentation. All tasks are conducted on the same fixed validation set with 4 number of samples per survey, totalling to 52 samples and 180,901 predicted points, same random seed for all randomizations and a fixed grid. The evaluation scores are F1 (Eq. 5), F1 within 1-class tolerance, and R2 (Eq. 2). The models were saved when reached minimal validation loss. The selected model achieved the best overall performance and provided the highest spatial resolution, aligning with the goals of this work. 17 # Dataset Pretrained GA (1) GA (2) P F1 F1, 1-tol R2 A Synth — — — 4 0.7672 0.9113 0.8562 B — — — 8 0.7791 0.9166 0.8599 C — — — 16 0.7251 0.8934 0.8543 D — — — 32 0.6961 0.8777 0.8304 1 Real ✗ — — 4 0.7458 0.9052 0.8125 2 ✓ ✗ — 4 0.7329 0.8976 0.8585 3 ✓ ✗ ✗ 4 0.7351 0.8992 0.8578 4 ✓ ✓ — 4 0.6634 0.8699 0.7509 5 ✓ ✓ ✗ 4 0.8042 0.9286 0.8901 Table 1: Training Ablation Test. Rows represent the different tested configurations, while columns indicate whether the model was pretrained, grid-augmented (GA) on the first run (1) or the second (2), the patch size (P), and the corresponding performance metrics (F1 score, 1-class-tolerance F1, and R²). The selected model (bold) achieved the best overall performance, combining the smallest number of parameters with the highest spatial resolution. Table 1 summarizes the evaluation scores of the different models. Rows A-D correspond to the synthetic data pretraining phase, while rows 1–5 represent the real-data fine-tuning phase. Each column indicates whether the model was pretrained or trained from scratch (e.g. model 1), grid-augmented (GA) as described earlier on the first run (1), grid-augmented on a re-run (2), the patch size, F1 score, relaxed F1 1-class tolerance score and R2. Performance on the synthetic data generally decreased with increasing patch size, except for patch size 8 (model B), which showed a slight improvement. However, given the objectives of this study, we chose the higher-resolution configuration (patch size of 4), which maintained strong performance despite the smaller patch size while using less than half the parameters (49.8M, Table S1). The pretrained model (2) shows significantly improved R2 score compared with the from-scratch model (1), with much faster convergence. Augmenting the grid and then fixing it yielded the best results (5). 3.4. Attention-Driven Feature Relevancy Once the model is trained, we explore the feature relevancies to reveal subtle interdependencies that remain hidden in conventional correlation analyses (Fig. 3). The model predicts a sequence of spatially distributed patches of target features. Each input patch, containing local information from the input feature maps, carries a certain level of relevancy for predicting every individual target patch. By assessing the relevancy of each input item to the model’s predictions, we produce spatial relevancy maps that reveal which locations contribute most to prediction accuracy. These maps can be constructed globally, per target feature, or for individual target points. We investigate the factors contributing to the observed relevancy scores by examining how they correlate to the magnitudes of the associated features. We first define an input feature-patch relevancy by the propagated attention score it receives for computing a target feature-patch, inspired by other commonly used relevancy scores.29 In our model, each input feature patch is tokenized and passed through the transformer encoder, where it undergoes several self-attention layers to produce an encoded input token. A target token is then processed through the transformer decoder, first by self-attention among target tokens and subsequently by cross-attention with the encoded input tokens. For simplicity we approximate the cross-attention layers propagation by averaging them, which shows very similar results to the full-path calculation. We first calculate propagated attention for each batch sample, which varies in its patch sequence, and then pool these scores onto a fixed global grid defined by the validation set. To reduce variance, we aggregate over both training and validation data; using validation alone yielded similar maps. With 16 samples per survey, we average over 1,056 samples. 18 We compute the relevancy by the following steps per sample and then average them over the entire dataset. Let ܶ௘= ܨ௜௡ܰ௜௡, ܶௗ= ܨ௧௚ܰ௧௚ the number of input and target feature-patches, respectively. For each layer ݈ and head ℎ, let Aா (௟,ℎ) ∈ℝ்೐×்೐, A஽ (௟,ℎ) ∈ℝ்೏×்೏ and A஼ (௟,ℎ) ∈ℝ்೏×்೐, the encoder self- attention (E), decoder self-attention (D) and decoder cross-attention (C) matrices, respectively, of that layer and head for the encoder. We define ܩா (௟,ℎ) = డℒ డAಶ (೗,ℎ), and ܩ஽ (௟,ℎ), ܩ஼ (௟,ℎ) for the corresponding gradients of the attention values w.r.t the MSE loss function (Eq. 1). In that context, negative gradients contribute to reducing the loss function, i.e. improving the prediction and hence correspond to important signals. We first compute the gradient-weighted attention values Ã(௟) of each layer of all attention blocks by: Eq. (6) ܣ̃ா (௟) = 1 ு∑ ܴ݁ܮܷ(−ܩா (௟,ℎ)) ⊙Aா (௟,ℎ) ு ℎ=1 where ܪ is the number of heads, ܴ݁ܮܷ is the rectified linear unit function yielding only the positive parts of the inverse gradients −ܩா (௟,ℎ) thus weighting by the strong contributing signals, and ⊙ is elementwise multiplication. We then remove the diagonals of the self attention matrices to clear trivial self-attending values, add the identity ܫ to account for skip-connections,27–29 and then row-normalize to yield values adding to 1 for each row: Eq. (7) ܣ̅ா (௟) = ܣ̃ா (௟) −݀݅ܽ݃(ܣ̃ா (௟)) + ܫ Eq. (8) ܵா (௟) = Λ (ܣ̅ா (௟)), [Λ(ܯ)]௜௝= ெ೔ೕ ∑ெ೔ೖ ೖ +ఢ for a small ߳, and similarly done for ܵ஽ (௟) = Λ (ܣ̅஽ (௟)). For simplicity, we approximate the cross-attention propagation by averaging over the layers: Eq. (9) ܣ̅஼= 1 ௅ವ∑ ܣ̃஼ (௟) ௅ವ ௟=1 , C = Λ(ܣ̃஼) ∈ℝ்೏×்೐ Next, we calculate the rollout self attentions by the ordered product of the per-layer maps: Eq. (10) ܧ= ∏ ܵா (௟) ௅ಶ ௟=1 ∈ℝ்೐×்೐, ܦ= ∏ ܵ஽ (௟) ௅ವ ௟=1 ∈ℝ்೏×்೏ Finally, we propagate the cross (self-target, self-input) attention pass by defining the relevancy as: Eq. (11) ܴ= ܦܥܧ∈ℝ்೏×்೐ which describes how any possible input feature-patch of column ݆∈[1, … , ܶ௘= ܨ௜௡ܰ௜௡] is relevant for predicting any possible target feature-patch of row ݅∈[1, … , ܶௗ= ܨ௧௚ܰ௧௚] in the full dataset. The results are similar when replacing the approximated cross attention with the exact multiplication of the decoder-self, decoder-cross passes per layer, so we use the approximated calculation for simplicity. We found that without the gradient-weighting step, the results are obscured by less important attention values. In addition, the results are similar when using the validation set alone instead of the combined training-validation set, but using the full dataset yields smoother results as we average over more samples. To analyze the relevancy matrix, we can observe the average scores per target features, per input features and per spatial location. For that, we rearrange the matrix to the tensor of shape [ܨ௧௚, ܰ௧௚, ܨ௜௡, ܰ௜௡] and then average by the dimension of interest. For example, averaging across the spatial dimensions (ܰ௧௚, ܰ௜௡) reveals the [ܨ௧௚, ܨ௜௡] matrix describing the relevancies of input features for predicting target features anywhere on the map, shown in Fig. 6 (top). Averaging instead across the target dimension 19 (ܨ௧௚, ܰ௧௚) produces overall input relevancy maps of shape [ܨ௜௡, ܰ௜௡], describing the spatially distributed relevancies for predicting any target feature-patch in general, shown in Fig. 6 (bottom). To better understand what drives relevancies, we further analyze the correlations between the relevancy scores and the feature values themselves. We correlate the scores across the spatial dimensions of either the mean patch value of the features or the patch’s standard deviation. This approach allows us to assess whether an input feature’s predictive relevance arises in regions of high magnitude or high variability. Formal definitions of these correlations, shown in Fig. 7, are provided in Text S2. 4. Data and Code Availability All data that support the findings in this study is either freely available or available from the corresponding authors on request. Regional data is available at https://air.sviva.gov.il/ and https://ims.gov.il/he. The code used in this work is available from the corresponding authors on request. 5.
