To view a copy of this licence, visit http://​creat​iveco​mmons.​org/​licen​ses/​by/4.​0/. Cybersecurity MAE‑based image inpainting‑steganography method Chunying Zhang1, Rui Sun1, Jing Ren1*, Lu Liu1, Liya Wang1 and Jiang Ma1 Abstract To address the current issue that existing steganographic techniques are highly dependent on the quality of carrier images and have insufficient adaptability to damaged images, we propose an MAE-based inpainting-steganography framework, which realizes steganography and inpainting for 2D color damaged images through multi-module col- laboration. The encoder divides the original 244 × 244 images into non-overlapping patches and performs random masking to generate their feature sequences. The steganographic point prediction module locates suitable stegano- graphic points by analyzing feature fluctuations during the inpainting process. The steganography module embeds information by introducing feature offsets at specific positions in the feature sequences according to the suitable steganographic points. The decoder completes the inpainting of masked regions to obtain stego-inpainted images. And the extraction module recovers the secret information through feature alignment. Experimental results show that with a feature fluctuation amplitude threshold of 0%, an offset of 3%, and an embedding capacity of 30 bits, the verification success rate reaches 96.67%, and remains at 95.27% even when the capacity is increased to 1000 bits. In terms of anti-steganography detection capability, evaluation results based on mainstream steganalysis models show that the detection accuracy of the proposed method is 49.79%. This accuracy is close to the level of random guessing, which indicates the excellent anti-detection performance of the method. Moreover, under the combined perturbations of JPEG compression, Gaussian blur, and noise interference, the information extraction accuracy still reaches 94.68%, demonstrating that the feature-domain embedding mechanism has good robustness. In addition, adding steganography under different masking ratios has no significant impact on image inpainting performance, and some metrics even outperform pure inpainting algorithms. While maintaining the quality of image inpainting, this framework achieves highly reliable information embedding and extraction, and maintains a high verification success rate in the complex scenario of damaged carrier images, providing a new idea for steganographic techniques in practical applications. Keywords Image steganography, Deep learning, MAE, Inpainting, Inpainting-steganography Introduction As an important branch in the field of information secu- rity, image steganography technology aims to achieve lossless embedding and covert transmission of mes- sage by transforming the pixel or feature space of the carrier image. With the development of digital media dissemination and network communication technology, the demand for this technology in scenarios such as sen- sitive information protection, copyright traceability, and privacy enhancement has grown exponentially (Fu et al. 2020). Traditional steganography methods rely on artifi- cially designed feature operations to achieve informa- tion embedding. Typical schemes include pixel domain replacement based on the least significant bit (LSB) (Huda Ismail Olewi et al. 2024; Njoum et al. 2024), fre- quency component adjustment in the discrete cosine *Correspondence: Jing Ren renjing@ncst.edu.cn 1 College of Science, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan 063210, China Page 2 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 transform (DCT) domain (Fu et al. 2021; Kapoor and Shivani 2024), and scale feature modification in the dis- crete wavelet transform (DWT) domain (Huang and Zhao 2018; Khandelwal and Sharma 2024). Such meth- ods are limited by fixed feature space and preset rules, and have significant bottlenecks in anti-detection ability and robustness. Although texture analysis-based steg- anography (Wu et al. 2021; Liao et al. 2022) achieves steg- anography by integrating information with the natural texture of the image, its complex feature matching pro- cess and strong texture dependence limit its universality. In recent years, deep learning technology has promoted a paradigm shift in the field of steganography. Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) (Zhang et al. 2019; Zhu et al. 2024) have achieved the indistinguishability of the visual features of stego images from real images through adversarial training mechanisms. Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (cGANs) (Lei et al. 2021; Garg et al. 2023) have further improved the semantic consistency of the carrier image through semantic conditional con- straints. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) (Srini- vasu and Veeramani 2022; Ahmad et al. 2024) achieve high-dimensional embedding of message in the abstract semantic space of images through hierarchical feature extraction, effectively avoiding the detection risk of pixel- domain operations. Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) (Wei et al. 2020; Bui et al. 2023) provide a more robust feature representation space for information embedding by constructing the latent feature manifold of images. It is worth noting that deep learning models are not only used for information embedding, but also applied to enhance steganalysis resistance (Apau et al. 2024), and improve the security of steganographic systems through adversar- ial training. However, existing research mainly focuses on the steganography of high-quality images, natural images without obvious noise or damage, and pays insufficient attention to damaged images. Due to pixel loss or seman- tic fragmentation, traditional steganography methods are difficult to balance the integrity of restoration and the concealment of information embedding. As an intersection of computer vision and informa- tion security, the restoration of damaged images by restoring the integrity of the carrier and realizing secret transmission by deeply coupling the information embed- ding process with the image inpainting process. Early studies such as the semantic restoration steganography framework proposed by Liu et al. (2023) use a two-stage mode of "pre-embedding-post-restoration" to complete the semantic reconstruction of the secret image, but its pixel domain embedding strategy is easily disturbed by the semantic diffusion of the restoration process; Nichal et al. (2021) use the alpha channel to separate and embed authentication data to improve the anti-tampering ability, but there is a limitation of image format dependence. The deep coupling framework proposed by Liu et al. (2018a, b) breaks the stage segmentation mode and completes information embedding synchronously during the fea- ture propagation process of the restoration network, laying a foundation for this field. Embedding message during the restoration process has unique advantages. The restored image lacks the original control sample, which makes the steganographic detection method based on differential analysis invalid, thus naturally enhanc- ing the concealment of information. This technology has important application value in scenarios such as digi- tal restoration of damaged artworks (Kumar and Gupta 2023), digital preservation of historical documents (Shen et al. 2024), and semantic enhancement of old photos (Shi et al. 2024). It can not only restore the visual integ- rity of the carrier, but also realize the secure transmission of key data through invisible information embedding. However, the current research on steganography of dam- aged images is still in its infancy. On the one hand, the semantic uncertainty of the damaged area increases the difficulty of selecting the embedding point, and it is nec- essary to dynamically identify the stable feature space during the restoration process; on the other hand, most of the existing steganographic algorithms are designed for complete images and lack the ability to model the fea- ture diffusion effect during the restoration process. The evolution of image inpainting technology pro- vides key support for restoration steganography. Early methods based on convolutional neural networks achieved arbitrary shape hole restoration by dynami- cally adjusting the convolution kernel (Sun et al. 2023), but their pixel-by-pixel update mechanism resulted in low computational efficiency, and the local perception characteristics made it difficult to model the semantic association between the inpainted area and the global structure, which may cause the risk of semantic diffu- sion of embedded information during steganography. Although gated convolution (Wang et al. 2022) and mask-guided convolution (Yu et al. 2023a, b) improve the free-form mask processing capability by introduc- ing the attention mechanism, they are limited by the local receptive field of the convolution operation and have insufficient structural inference capabilities for large-scale missing regions, which can easily lead to dramatic fluctuations in features around the stegano- graphic points and affect the robustness of information extraction. With the rise of the Transformer architec- ture, inpainting models based on the self-attention mechanism have shown unique advantages. The MAT proposed by Li et al. (2022) achieves a balance between texture synthesis authenticity and convolution com- putational efficiency in large hole inpainting through Page 3 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 a dynamic mask perception module and a multi-head attention mechanism; the TransInpaint model of Sham- solmoali et al. (2023) solves the semantic separation problem between the inpainted area and the original image by fusing jump features and generative features through a texture enhancement network; the LaMa model of Roman Suvorov et al. (2021) shows excellent generalization ability in large-scale occlusion inpaint- ing through a fast Fourier convolution module and a perceptual loss function; however, these models still face common challenges. The inpainting process does not explicitly consider the steganographic require- ments, the feature space lacks robust design, and relies on explicit supervision signals to locate the stegano- graphic area, which has a high computational cost. The unsupervised representation learning characteris- tics of the Masked Autoencoder (MAE) (He et al. 2021) provide a new solution to the above problems. Through the asymmetric encoding and decoding architecture and high mask rate training strategy, the model can learn the high-level semantic features and structural priors of the image. The output high-level features are insensitive to local pixel perturbations and are suitable as a robust fea- ture space for steganography. Yu et al. (2023a, b) applied the improved MAE to military target occlusion comple- tion, effectively solving the semantic fusion problem of the target and the environment in the reconnaissance image; Chen et al. (2024) combined the domain adapta- tion module with MAE pre-training to achieve feature mining under few-sample conditions in rolling bearing fault diagnosis; Xu et al. (2023) proposed the Swin MAE, which showed a transfer ability that surpassed the super- vised learning model in the semantic feature learning of medical images. These studies show that the feature learning ability of MAE can effectively capture the poten- tial structural information of damaged images and pro- vide semantic anchors for steganographic point selection and feature embedding. Based on the above analysis, a two-dimensional color image inpainting steganography model based on MAE, MAE-InpaintSteg, is proposed to achieve high-quality inpainting of color damaged images and embedding of message. The main contributions of this study include: • Inpainting-Steganography Model ArchitectureThe proposed framework integrates steganographic func- tionality with an MAE-based masked reconstruc- tion mechanism. Through an asymmetric encoder- decoder architecture, it achieves joint optimization of damaged image inpainting and secret data embed- ding. Leveraging global semantic features from visi- ble regions, the system synchronously completes data embedding during mask region inpainting, forming a unified "inpainting-steganography" processing pipe- line. • Steganographic point selection method The pro- posed approach analyzes fluctuation amplitudes between decoder-generated feature sequences and original image features to dynamically identify low- fluctuation regions optimal for data embedding. This feature-space analysis effectively resolves the blind embedding problem in damaged image scenarios. • Steganography strategy design Proposing a dual- mechanism framework integrating feature-offset embedding and obfuscation allocation, enabling semantically lossless information embedding in fea- ture spaces while confusing embedding traces via adaptive noise perturbation to enhance steganalysis resistance. • Extraction module Leveraging high-dimensional semantic features from the inpainted image, this module achieves accurate message decoding while addressing traditional pixel-domain extraction meth- ods’ sensitivity to inpainting-induced distortions. Basics Image steganography Image steganography is an interdisciplinary technology based on information theory and computer vision. It achieves semantic concealment of information existence by constructing a covert transmission channel for mes- sage in the redundant feature space of digital images. Its core goal is to establish a nonlinear mapping relationship between message S and carrier image X under the prem- ise of satisfying visual imperceptibility constraint: where X∗ is the secret image and ε represents the stego- coding function. Steganography embeds information into visually insensitive areas through feature space perturba- tion theory, making the secret image difficult to distin- guish from the original image at the visual level. With the development of steganalysis technology, cur- rent research has shifted from pixel-level embedding to deep learning feature space steganography (Wang et al. 2019) By designing a generator network, binary message is mapped into feature offsets F and embedded into the feature map F output by the encoder to form a new fea- ture map F∗. At the same time, adversarial training is used to mini- mize the detection error of the steganalyzer, thereby (1) X∗= ε(X, S) (2) F∗= F + F Page 4 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 improving the concealment and enhancing the anti- detection ability. The performance evaluation of image steganography technology mainly focuses on three aspects: conceal- ment, robustness and capacity. Stealthiness measures the similarity between the stego image and the original image, and is usually evaluated through indicators such as peak signal-to-noise ratio and structural similarity index; robustness measures the ability of the stego image to extract message after compression, cropping, filtering and other processing; capacity measures the amount of information that can be hidden in the stego image. Image inpainting Image inpainting technology is an inverse problem solv- ing paradigm in the field of computer vision. It aims to reconstruct the complete image ˆX through the dam- aged image X and the mask matrix M , so that ˆX is as similar to the original image as possible. Its mathemati- cal essence is to find the optimal solution that minimizes the perceptual loss function in the constrained solution space. Modern image inpainting algorithms usually adopt an encoder-decoder architecture. First, multi-scale con- text features of the damaged image are extracted through CNN or Transformer (Iizuka et al. 2017). Then, the semantics of the visible area are diffused to the missing area to generate filling features using the dilated convo- lution (Liu et al. 2018a, b) or the cross-attention mech- anism of Transformer. Finally, the inpainted image is reconstructed through the deconvolution network. Current research faces challenges such as difficulty in inferring the structure of large-scale missing areas and alignment of cross-modal data features. In the future, we will explore guided inpainting based on global semantic priors, lightweight network design, and joint optimiza- tion with steganography technology, and build a safe and reliable intelligent inpainting system through the “inpainting-steganography” joint loss function. The evaluation of image inpainting technology mainly focuses on three aspects: inpainting quality, computa- tional efficiency, and robustness. The restoration quality measures the similarity between the restored image and the original image. Common evaluation indicators include peak signal-to-noise ratio and structural similar- ity index. Computational efficiency measures the running time and resource consumption of the image inpainting algorithm, which is particularly important when process- ing high-resolution images. Robustness measures the ability of the image inpainting algorithm to effectively restore the image under different types of damage and noise. MAE model architecture In recent years, MAE has become one of the core self- supervised learning paradigms in the fields of computer vision, natural language processing, and multimedia analysis due to its excellent feature abstraction ability and cross-modal generalization potential (Tong et al. 2022). As an unsupervised representation learning framework based on generative pre-training, its core design idea is to force the model to learn contextual dependencies from the remaining visible area by randomly masking part of the input data, and then reconstruct the semantic infor- mation of the complete input. The MAE infrastructure follows the asymmetric encoding and decoding para- digm, as shown in Fig. 1, and is mainly composed of an encoder and a lightweight decoder. The encoder adopts a hierarchical feature extraction architecture based on ViT (Vision Transformer). The main body is composed of a cascade of multi-layer Trans- former encoder blocks. Through the multi-head attention mechanism and the feedforward neural network, the vis- ible rectangular blocks are cross-layered feature inter- action is performed, and finally the high-level feature representation containing global semantic associations is output, which is expressed as: where R represents a real number set, (1 −ρ)N and D are the number of rows and columns of the matrix respectively. (3) Z ∈R(1−ρ)N×D Fig. 1 MAE model architecture Page 5 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 The decoder adopts an asymmetric reconstruction architecture and receives the feature representation out- put by the encoder and the position information of the mask block. The main body is composed of slice-level Transformer blocks, which fuse the features of the vis- ible area and the predicted information of the mask area through the cross-attention mechanism, and finally out- put the inpainted image through the linear projection layer and the pixel space reconstruction module. Compared with the symmetric architecture of the tra- ditional autoencoder, MAE reduces the computational complexity through the lightweight decoder design, and drives the encoder to focus on high-level semantic fea- ture learning with a high mask rate to avoid shallow pixel redundancy. This feature is consistent with the needs of steganography. The high mask rate inpainting process simulates the image damage scene and provide an ideal carrier for inpainting-steganography modeling; and the semantic diffusion ability of the decoder helps to inte- grate message into the global structure and enhance robustness. In recent years, MAE has derived multimodal versions, such as Multimae (Bachmann et al. 2022) and the medical imaging-specific variant Medmae (Gupta et al. 2024). Its feature learning mechanism provides a new direction for the development of cross-domain steg- anography technology. MAE‑inpaintsteg model Overall architecture MAE-InpaintSteg performs steganography on the high- dimensional feature layer of a two-dimensional color image. It not only uses the secret sequence after steg- anography to accurately re inpainting the image, but also maintains the richness and visual appeal of the image content to further enhance the concealment of the steg- anography. MAE-InpaintSteg is based on original MAE. It randomly masks certain areas of the image with masks of different proportions to simulate different degrees of image damage. During the training process, the model learns how to adapt to various damage situations and master the skills of effectively embedding message and efficiently inpainting images under these conditions. The model architecture is shown in Fig. 2. It mainly consists of an encoder, a steganographic point predic- tion module, a steganography module, a decoder, and an extraction module. • Encoder The original MAE encoder architecture based on ViT (Vision Transformer) is adopted. First, the input image is adaptively resized through a stand- ardized preprocessing module, the spatial resolu- tion is unified to 224 × 224 pixels, and a visual token sequence that meets the Transformer input specifica- Fig. 2 MAE-InpaintSteg model architecture Page 6 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 tion is constructed. Then, the non-overlapping block engine is started to divide the regularized image into fixed-size n × n pixel patches, which are mapped to embedding vectors through a linear projection layer, and the initial feature sequence is formed after super- imposing the position encoding. In order to achieve the dual-task coupling of steganography and inpaint- ing, the embedding vector is processed by a random mask generator to generate a binary mask matrix with a preset mask rate, and the feature sequence is divided into visible areas and mask areas. The for- mer is used as a message carrier, and the latter simu- lates the semantic missing area of the image. Finally, through the self-attention mechanism and feedfor- ward neural network of the multi-layer Transformer encoder block, the features of the visible area and the mask area are cross-layered to generate a com- posite feature sequence containing multi-scale con- text associations, which provides a semantic anchor for the prediction of the steganographic point and retains the structural prior for image inpainting. • Steganographic point prediction module This module precisely identifies optimal embedding locations for covert message hiding. The process begins by decod- ing the encoder’s feature sequence to produce both an inpainted image and its corresponding inpainted sequence. A high-precision differential measurement algorithm then quantitatively evaluates the variation between inpainted and original feature sequences. Each candidate point is rigorously evaluated against a predefined variation threshold—points with vari- ations below the threshold are selected as suitable steganographic points that can carry secret data without causing perceptible quality degradation, while those exceeding the threshold are discarded. Ultimately, the generated point sequence serves as a crucial guide, functioning both as a key basis for the subsequent steganography module to perform embedding operations and as a core reference bench- mark for the extraction module in recovering secret information. • Steganography module It serves as the core pro- cessing unit of the MAE-InpaintSteg, specializing in seamless message embedding within image fea- ture sequences. By establishing a precise mapping relationship between binary sequences and feature value offset ratios, the module precisely controls embedding amplitudes at predetermined loca- tions identified by the steganographic point predic- tion module. This sophisticated approach enables the encoded binary message to be imperceptibly integrated into the feature sequence, generating a stego-feature sequence while perfectly preserving visual quality. The feature-offset-based embedding strategy fundamentally ensures both the conceal- ment and security of hidden information through its mathematically controlled transformation of fea- ture space representations. • Decoder A lightweight reconstruction decoder based on the original MAE architecture takes the secret feature sequence as input, restores the one- dimensional feature sequence to a two-dimensional feature map through the inverse block projection module, and then upsamples layer by layer through a multi-layer deconvolution pyramid, combined with the multi-stage features of the jump connec- tion fusion encoder to achieve progressive restora- tion of image details. In the core processing flow, the nonlinear activation function group is used to restore the nonlinear mapping relationship of the pixel space, and the optimization process guided by the structure-aware loss function ensures the perceptual similarity between the stego-inpainted images and the original image in the RGB color space. This module adopts an asymmetric encoding and decoding architecture consistent with MAE, while ensuring the robustness of the message and maintaining the restoration efficiency through a lightweight design. • Extraction module The extraction module is based on the principle of reverse engineering, whose core function is to accurately extract the hidden secret information from the stego-inpainted image. The inputs of this module include the stego-inpainted image and the point sequence. The point sequence not only identifies the positions of suitable stegano- graphic points determined by the steganographic point prediction module but also records the original feature values corresponding to each steganographic point, providing a key comparative benchmark for subsequent secret information extraction. In the spe- cific extraction process, the bit information (either 0 or 1) is inferred and recovered by comparing the differences between the feature values of the stego- inpainted image and the original feature values at the corresponding positions in the point sequence, com- bined with the set threshold. It should be emphasized that the original feature val- ues recorded in the point sequence are only used for analyzing the trend of feature fluctuations in extrac- tion discrimination. They are neither used as input for model training nor involved in back-propagation. Their recording scope is strictly limited to a very small number of selected points in the image, containing neither full- image information nor the ability to restore the image. Page 7 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 Therefore, this mechanism does not pose a risk of infor- mation leakage. The main innovative work of the overall architecture of the MAE-InpaintSteg model lies in steganographic point prediction, steganography and message extraction, so these three are explained and analyzed in detail. Steganographic point prediction module In the process of damaged image inpainting based on the MAE masking mechanism, the decoder’s recalculation and integration of image features inevitably induce fea- ture fluctuations, which in turn affect image quality. To address this challenge, the steganographic point predic- tion module is designed, with its core insight being that points with small feature value fluctuations before and after image inpainting possess higher stability in their structural and semantic information during the decod- ing-encoding process. When message is embedded in these points, it is less susceptible to damage, resulting in superior steganographic robustness. Meanwhile, the fea- ture deviation of such points causes less interference to image quality, which can effectively avoid obvious arti- facts in the reconstructed image and improve stegano- graphic concealment and visual quality. Based on this, the module accurately screens suitable steganographic points by comprehensively evaluating the fluctuation amplitude of image features before and after inpainting, thereby minimizing the negative impact of image inpaint- ing on steganographic performance and contributing to the enhancement of steganographic robustness and concealment. The steps of stegographic point prediction are: (1) Obtain the feature sequence F output by the encoder: where fk is the eigenvector of the k-th matrix block, 1 ≤k ≤n , expressed as: where fkj is the j-th value in fk,1 ≤j ≤p. (2) Input the aforementioned feature sequence into the decoder to generate an inpainted image, which is then fed back to the encoder to obtain the inpainted sequence F′ corresponding to the inpainted image. (3) Analyze the original image feature sequence F and the inpainted sequence F′ , and calculate the dif- ference metric between the feature vectors at each position to characterize the degree of feature fluc- tuation before and after image inpainting. (4) F = [f1, f2, . . . , fn] (5) fk = [fk1, fk2, . . . , fkp] (4) 4) Determine whether each feature point is suitable as a steganographic point based on the aforemen- tioned fluctuation amplitude results. Specifically, a fluctuation threshold is set, and only when the fluc- tuation degree of a feature position is lower than the threshold is it deemed a suitable steganographic point; otherwise, it is discarded. (5) 5) Generate the point sequence Sq based on the screening results: Among them, " + " denotes a suitable steganographic point, and " − " denotes an unsuitable one. This screening strategy ingeniously avoids the masking of feature-value offsets from the steganographic module by feature fluc- tuations during the inpainting process, while minimizing the adverse impact of steganographic operations on image quality. In addition to identifying spatial locations suitable for embedding, the point sequence Sq further establishes a corresponding reference record for each point. This record consists of two parts: first, the index position of the point, which enables unique localization within the high- dimensional feature sequence; second, the summary of the original feature value corresponding to the point before inpainting. To avoid information redundancy and poten- tial leakage risks, the original feature values are not stored as complete vectors but are summarized through low- dimensional statistical description (i.e., mean). The afore- mentioned records only serve as a comparison benchmark during the extraction stage, used to calculate the feature change rate of corresponding points in the stego-inpainted image, thereby determining the embedding status of bit information. Since their storage scope is strictly limited to a small number of selected steganographic points, and the data form is non-invertible statistics or low-dimensional projections, they can neither be used to reconstruct the original image nor pose a threat to steganographic security. Steganography module As the core component of the model, the main function of the steganographic module is to accurately control the offset amplitude of the characteristic value according to the suitable steganographic points specified by the point sequence Sq , embed the message into the characteristic sequence, and thus generate a secret sequence. Encode the message into a binary bit stream S: where L is the length of S,si ∈{0, 1},1 ≤i ≤L. (6) Sq = [[−, −, +, . . . , +, +, −] [−, +, +, . . . , −, −, −]... [+, −, +, . . . , +, −, −]] (7) S = {s1, s2, . . . , sL} Page 8 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 Point sequence preprocessing mechanism In order to enhance the concealment and anti-steganal- ysis capability of steganography, before the message is embedded, the point sequence preprocessing mechanism divides the suitable steganographic points in Sq into two parts: one part is used to embed the real message, and the other part is used for confusing embedding, and the fea- ture value offset is randomly set to disturb the analyst’s attention, thereby enhancing the anti-detection capability of steganography. The specific allocation process of suitable stegano- graphic points is shown in Fig. 3. According to the num- ber N of suitable steganographic points in Sq , when the binary sequence length L is less than N , L points are ran- domly selected from the N selected candidate points to embed the message, and the remaining points are used for confusing embedding, and the random offset disturbs the analyst; otherwise, S is cut to length N . At this time, all N points are used to embed message. Message embedding In the message embedding stage, the eigenvalues at the selected points are fine-tuned according to the preset eigenvalue offset strategy to ensure its visual consistency with the original image, while embedding the message covertly at the feature level. First, a mapping between the binary bit stream and the eigenvalue offset amplitude is formulated to extract the eigenvalue set F = {f1, f2, . . . , fL} from the selected steganographic points. Then, for each embedding position i , the following operation is performed according to the corresponding binary bit value si : If si = 1 , then the eigenvalue is offset (assuming the offset amplitude is α ), that is, If si = 0 , then keep the eigenvalue unchanged, that is, (8) f ′ i = fi × (1 + α) (9) f ′ i = fi Fig. 3 Flowchart of suitable steganographic points allocation Page 9 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 In addition, for the confusing embedding position, a shift of magnitude δ or no shift is randomly determined, δ ∈{−α, α}. Extraction module Since the secret message is embedded in the high-dimen- sional feature layer, the extraction module also needs to operate at the high-dimensional feature layer to effec- tively recover the embedded message. To ensure the accuracy of secret message extraction, the point sequence synchronously records the index of each steganographic point and the corresponding summary of its original feature value, serving as a reference for the subsequent extraction stage. This auxiliary information is transmit- ted together with the stego-inpainted image in the form of metadata, and its size is extremely small (usually only a few hundred bytes, far smaller than the size of the image itself), which does not incur significant communication overhead. More importantly, this summary is only used to calculate the feature change rate for determining the bit embedding status; it is not input to the decoder and does not have the ability to reconstruct the image, thus preventing information leakage. In specific operations, following the design princi- ples of the steganographic module, the secret message is embedded in the selected feature points with a fixed offset amplitude: when the bit value is “1”, the feature value of the corresponding point undergoes a fixed pro- portion offset; when the bit value is "0", the feature value remains unchanged. Therefore, by comparing the change rate between the features of the stego-inpainted image and the reference feature value summary, the extraction module can accurately determine the steganographic bits and recover the complete secret message. The message extraction process is specifically shown in Fig. 4. First, the stego-inpainted images is input into the encoder to extract the feature sequence. Then, the visible area of the bit sequence and the inpainted sequence is accu- rately aligned to clarify the corresponding position of each stego point in the sequence. Next, the feature value change rate γi is calculated for each stego point: Among them, f stego i is the eigenvalue of the stego- inpainted images, f clean i is the eigenvalue of the inpainted image only, and ε is the minimum constant to avoid the denominator being 0. And it is judged according to the preset change rate critical value γ0 . If it is γi > γ0 , the steganographic value of the point is judged to be “1”, oth- erwise, the steganographic value of the point is judged to (10) γi = f stego i −f clean i max(f clean i , ε) be “0”. With the powerful ability of deep learning in fea- ture extraction and the sophisticated analysis strategy based on the change rate, this module can efficiently and accurately extract message, effectively ensuring the integ- rity and security of the steganographic process. In summary, the model is based on the MAE architec- ture and adds a steganographic point prediction module, a steganography module. This integrated design enables simultaneous information hiding while performing image inpainting, effectively combining steganography with damaged image inpainting. Experiments Experimental setup The experiment is built on Python 3.7, using PyTorch1.7.1 deep learning framework, and GPU acceleration with CUDA11.8 to improve computing efficiency. In order to effectively reduce storage space requirements and improve the execution efficiency of research projects, the experiment uses the ILSVRC2012 ImageNet mini dataset after strict screening of ILSVRC2012 ImageNet (Li et al. 2009), which contains 40,000 training images and 10,000 verification images, with a file size of about 17 GB. Metrics To comprehensively evaluate the model’s performance, we employ three key metrics: absolute embedding capac- ity, feature-domain embedding rate, and verification success rate. These metrics collectively provide a multi- dimensional assessment framework for quantifying the performance of MAE-InpaintSteg, which together pro- vide a holistic evaluation perspective that captures both capacity and reliability aspects of the steganographic system. Absolute embedding capacity (Khalid and Kurnaz 2024) is used to quantify the maximum amount of mes- sage that can be hidden in a single image. The unit is bit. It directly reflects the ability of steganography to embed message in a single image and is a direct standard for measuring the performance of the steganography sys- tem. In the field of image steganography, the larger the absolute embedding capacity, the more message the steg- anography system can carry and the stronger its message embedding ability. The feature domain embedding rate PDER is a custom metric used to accurately measure the ratio between the absolute embedding capacity and the total number of feature values in the visible area of the image during image steganography. This indicator draws on the traditional concept of bits per pixel (bpp) (Yin et al. 2013), but expands its application scenario from the pixel level to the feature level, and carefully evaluates and optimizes the performance of the steganography algo- rithm at the feature level. The formula PDER is as follows: Page 10 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 Among them,Cabs is the absolute embedding capacity and Ft is the total amount of eigenvalues in the visible area of the image. Based on the standard bit accuracy used in the litera- ture (Ntivuguruzwa and Ahmad 2023), this paper uses the verification success rate Adec to measure the perfor- mance of extracting message from the stego-inpainted (11) PDER = Cabs Ft images, expressed as the proportion of correctly decoded message to the total message, and the formula is as follows: where m is the number of correctly decoded bits and p is the total number of bits of embedded message. The abso- lute embedding capacity directly reflects the maximum amount of message that the model can hide in a single (12) Adec = m p × 100% Fig. 4 Flowchart of extracting message Page 11 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 image, reflecting its level of message embedding. The fea- ture domain embedding rate further refines the efficiency of message embedding from the feature level, and pro- vides a new dimension for evaluation through innovative expansion of traditional concepts. The Adec focuses on the accuracy of message extraction in practical applica- tions to ensure that the steganographic information can be correctly decoded. By comprehensively using these three key indicators, the model performance can be com- prehensively and accurately evaluated, which not only ensures that the steganographic model can achieve high- capacity and high-efficiency message embedding in tech- nology, but also ensures that it has high reliability and practicality in actual use, providing a solid and reliable basis for the evaluation of the applicability of the steg- anographic system in specific tasks and environments. Model parameter experiment • Impact of offset amplitude and fluctuation amplitude threshold on Adec In order to explore the impact of the setting of the off- set amplitude and fluctuation amplitude threshold on the Adec , multiple groups of experiments were designed, with the offset amplitude set to 1%, 3%, 5%, 7%, and 10%, and the fluctuation amplitude thresholds to 0%, 3%, 5%, 7%, 10%, 15%, and 20%. During the experiment, the absolute embedding capacity was fixed to 300 to ensure the con- sistency of the experimental conditions. The experimen- tal results are shown in Table 1. As shown in Table 1, different combinations of offset amplitude and fluctuation amplitude threshold have a sig- nificant impact on the Adec . When the fluctuation ampli- tude threshold is 0%, no matter the offset amplitude is 1%, 3%, 5%, 7% or 10%, the Adec is always stable at 95.32%, which fully shows that when the feature does not fluctu- ate, the stability of the embedded message is excellent, and the offset amplitude has almost no effect on the Adec at this time. As the fluctuation amplitude threshold grad- ually increases, the Adec shows a downward trend, and when the threshold is in the range of 0%-10%, the Adec decreases relatively slowly; however, once the thresh- old exceeds 10%, the downward trend becomes steeper. Taking the fluctuation amplitude threshold of 10% as an example, the Adec drops from 95.32% when there is no fluctuation to 73.47–73.63%; further climbing to 15%, the Adec drops sharply to 64.47%-64.73%; and when it reaches 20%, the Adec is already at a low level of 57.65–57.96%. In the comparison of different offset amplitudes, although the overall difference is small, under specific fluctuation amplitudes, the Adec corresponding to some offset amplitudes shows slight changes. For example, when the fluctuation amplitude threshold is 10%, the Adec of the offset amplitude of 3% is 73.63%, which is slightly higher than 73.47% when the offset amplitude is 5%. Tak- ing the above data into consideration, in order to obtain a better Adec in subsequent experiments, combined with considerations of stability and experimental convenience, a parameter combination with an offset amplitude of 3% and a fluctuation amplitude threshold of 0% was selected to carry out experiments. This parameter combination is expected to provide more reliable data support for sub- sequent research while ensuring the high stability of the embedded message. • Impact of Different Steganographic Capacities on Adec and Image Quality To analyze how steganographic capacity affects the Adec , experiments used the 3% offset and 0% fluctuation threshold parameters. Absolute embedding capacities (30, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1000 bits) were tested to examine relationships with success rate and image quality (PSNR, SSIM), with results in Table 2. As can be seen from Table 2, with the increase of steg- anographic capacity, the Adec shows a slight downward Table 1 The impact of offset amplitude and fluctuation amplitude threshold on Adec Offset amplitude(%) 1 3 5 7 10 Fluctuation threshold(%) 0 95.32 95.32 95.32 95.32 95.32 3 88.35 88.32 88.33 88.26 88.30 5 83.63 83.58 83.54 83.58 83.47 7 79.60 79.59 79.54 79.54 79.48 10 73.47 73.63 73.60 73.54 73.58 15 64.73 64.66 64.53 64.52 64.47 20 57.65 57.96 57.88 57.92 57.85 Table 2 The impact of different message bits on the Adec and Image Quality Absolute embedding capacity/bit PDER/bpp Adec(%) PSNR (dB) SSIM 30 0.0008 96.67 27.362 0.863 100 0.0027 95.44 27.616 0.865 300 0.0080 95.32 27.359 0.862 500 0.0133 95.33 27.542 0.863 700 0.0186 95.27 27.360 0.863 1000 0.0267 95.27 27.308 0.863 Page 12 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 trend, but the overall fluctuation is small: when the embedding capacity is 100 bits, the Adec the highest value of 95.44%; when the capacity increases to 1000 bits, the Adec only slightly decreases to 95.27%. This indicates that although the increase in the amount of embedded infor- mation will make the feature distribution more complex and increase the difficulty of model decoding, the Adec changes minimally in the range of 300 bits to 1000 bits, demonstrating that the model can still effectively main- tain feature consistency and decoding stability under rel- atively large steganographic capacities. At the same time, PSNR remains in the range of 27.3 dB to 27.6 dB, and SSIM stabilizes in the range of 0.862 to 0.865. The maximum differences between them are 0.308 dB and 0.003 respectively, with extremely slight changes, far below the threshold perceptible to the naked eye. The above results fully illustrate that within the investigated embedding capacity range, the impact of steganographic operations on the objective quality of images is negligible; even under the condition of 1000- bit large capacity, the model can still effectively balance visual imperceptibility and decoding stability. Analysis of model robustness To further verify the robustness of MAE-InpaintSteg in practical application scenarios, this study designed and conducted extraction experiments under several com- mon image perturbation conditions, including: JPEG compression (with a quality factor of 50, simulating lossy compression in social media or storage optimiza- tion), Gaussian noise interference (adding noise follow- ing an N(0, 0.052) distribution to pixels), Gaussian blur (with a radius of 1.5, simulating smoothing processing), and combined interference by sequentially superimpos- ing the three aforementioned perturbations. The Adec was adopted as the evaluation metric, and the Adec of the extraction module under different conditions were statis- tically analyzed. The specific results are shown in Table 3. As shown in Table 3, under the no-perturbation con- dition, the extraction accuracy of the model reaches 96.67%, demonstrating extremely high extraction reliabil- ity for clean images. After JPEG compression, although the accuracy decreases, it remains at an acceptable level, proving that the model has a certain resistance to lossy compression. When facing Gaussian blur and noise interference, the accuracy maintains a high level. More notably, under the combined perturbation of JPEG com- pression, Gaussian blur, and noise interference, the extraction accuracy still reaches 94.68%, indicating that the feature-domain embedding mechanism exhibits excellent robustness. Overall, this experiment fully veri- fies the practicability and stability of MAE-InpaintSteg in various typical image distortion scenarios. Analysis of the impact of adding steganography on the quality of image inpainting To rigorously evaluate the impact of MAE-InpaintSteg on image inpainting performance, we selected two distinct mask ratios (50% and 75%) as experimental conditions. The 50% ratio represents moderate image damage, simulating common real-world scenarios, while the 75% ratio tests the model’s capability to han- dle severe image degradation. Under these conditions, we generated both conventional inpainted images and stego-inpainted images (with embedded message). Fig- ure 5 presents a comparative visualization of the origi- nal image, carrier image, standard inpainted image, and stego-inpainted image. As can be seen from the Fig. 5, MAE-InpaintSteg has basically no negative impact on the image inpainting effect. After the steganographic operation on features, some texture features such as lines and contours are optimized, making the quality of the secret restoration image better than the simple restoration image. This not only confirms the effectiveness of the steganographic scheme, but also highlights the application potential of steganography in the field of image inpainting. To intuitively present the impact of image steganog- raphy on the image inpainting effect, a representative image was selected for analysis under the condition of a 50% mask ratio. Figure 6a is the original image, and Fig. 6b, c respectively show the residual images between the original image and the inpainted image, and between the original image and the stego-inpainted images. Observing the residual images, it can be seen that they are overall dark, meaning that the pixel differences in most areas are small, that is, both the inpainted image and the stego-inpainted images are extremely close to the original image in most areas. It can be seen that under the selected mask ratio, the image Table 3 The Adec under different image perturbation conditions No perturbation JPEG compression Gaussian blur Noise interference Adec(%) √ 96.67 √ 88.30 √ 92.75 √ 93.47 √ √ 92.83 √ √ 94.33 √ √ 95.15 √ √ √ 94.68 Page 13 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 steganography technology has no significant impact on the image inpainting effect, and even if message is embedded, it will not significantly reduce the inpaint- ing quality. This finding is of great significance for the application of image steganography without sacrificing the inpainting quality. To further quantitatively analyze the visual quality dif- ferences between the original image (Fig. 6a) and the inpainted image, as well as the stego-inpainted images, detailed index evaluations were carried out by selecting the Mean Squared Error (MSE), Mean Absolute Error ( MAE ), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), Peak Signal- to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), and Structural Similarity Index (SSIM), and the results are shown in Fig. 7. It can be seen from Fig. 7 that after adding the steg- anography technology, the differences between the stego- inpainted images and the original image in most key indicators are even smaller than those between the ordi- nary inpainted image and the original image. Specifically, the stego-inpainted images is superior to the ordinary inpainted image in MSE, MAE , and RMSE indicators, indicating that it is closer to the original image in terms of pixel-level errors. The PSNR of the stego-inpainted images is higher than that of the ordinary inpainted image, indicating that its signal quality is better. Although the SSIM value between the stego-inpainted images and the original image is slightly lower than that of the ordi- nary inpainted image, the difference is not significant. Fig. 5 The impact of MAE-inpaintSteg schemes on image inpainting effects under different mask ratios Fig. 6 a Original image b Visualization of Residuals in Inpainted Image c Visualization of Residuals in Stego-inpainted Image Page 14 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 Fig. 7 Comparison between Original Image and Inpainted/Stego-inpainted Image Fig. 8 Comparison between 200 Original Images and Inpainted/Stego-inpainted Images damage the image inpainting quality, but also is superior to the pure image inpainting in overall performance, providing new ideas and methods for image inpainting and image steganography Page 15 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 Finally, the mean values of the differences between the inpainted and stego-inpainted images of 200 images and the original images were also compared, and the results are shown in Fig. 8. It can be seen from Fig. 8 that the mean values of MSE, MAE , and RMSE between the stego-inpainted images and the original images are all smaller than the corre- sponding mean values between the inpainted images and the original images, which indicates that in a large number of image samples, the stego-inpainted images are overall closer to the original images in terms of pixel errors. At the same time, the mean PSNR of the stego- inpainted images is higher than that of the inpainted images, meaning that the stego-inpainted images per- form better in signal quality. Moreover, the mean SSIM (0.852) between the stego-inpainted images and the orig- inal images is significantly higher than the mean SSIM (0.770) between the inpainted images and the original images, which also shows that the stego-inpainted images are superior in structural similarity. Comprehensive these indicators, it is further proved that the MAE-InpaintSteg not only does not. Comparative experiment To comprehensively and fairly evaluate the model per- formance, the number of message bits is fixed to 30, and five typical image steganographic schemes—SAE-JS (Chen et al. 2023), IGA (Zhang et al. 2022), PIMoG (Fang et al. 2022), TSDL-BW (Liu et al. 2019) and HiDDeN (Zhu et al. 2018)—were selected as comparison objects. Performance evaluations were conducted from three dimensions: the Adec , image quality (PSNR, SSIM), and steganalysis resistance. The results are shown in Table 4. As shown in the comparative data in the Table 4, MAE- InpaintSteg exhibits significant advantages in overall performance. With the number of secret message bits fixed at 30, the Adec of MAE-InpaintSteg reaches 96.67%, which is significantly higher than that of other schemes: an 8.83 percentage point increase compared to SAE-JS (87.84%) and a 13.77 percentage point increase compared to IGA (82.90%), reflecting its core advantage in informa- tion extraction accuracy. It also outperforms HiDDeN (91.00%) and the fluctuating range of TSDL-BW (80.00– 95.00%), particularly in stability. TSDL-BW shows result fluctuations due to unstable training for noise adaptabil- ity, while MAE-InpaintSteg achieves stably high success rates relying on the robustness of its algorithm design. In contrast, PIMoG only achieves a 70.00% Adec due to its embedding method causing significant interference to image features, further underscoring the rationality of MAE-InpaintSteg’s design in the “steganography-extrac- tion” pipeline. Notably, the image size of MAE-Inpaint- Steg is 224 × 224, larger than the 128 × 128 of other schemes. However, a larger size is not a necessary factor for improving the Adec ; the experimental results precisely demonstrate that MAE-InpaintSteg fully utilizes the potential of larger image spaces through optimized steg- anographic point selection and information embedding strategies, rather than simply relying on size advantages. In terms of image quality indicators, MAE-InpaintSteg achieves a PSNR of 27.488 dB and an SSIM of 0.852, which are lower than those of schemes such as HiDDeN (33.230 dB) and PIMoG (32.210 dB). However, this dif- ference stems from the essential distinction in process- ing objects and workflows: most other steganographic schemes embed information directly into original com- plete images, and their image quality indicators reflect "the degree of perturbation caused by steganographic operations to the original image," involving only subtle changes from steganography itself. In contrast, MAE- InpaintSteg’s steganographic process has distinct sce- nario-specific characteristics: its information embedding is implemented on "damaged images simulated by ran- dom masking," and the final generated stego-inpainted image not only carries secret information but also com- pletes the restoration of damaged regions. The PSNR and SSIM calculated between “stego-inpainted images and original complete images” here mainly derive from two Table 4 Comparison of different steganography schemes Bold font is used to highlight the key data of the proposed model in the experiment. These bolded data points directly reflect the core performance indicators of the model, which are critical for demonstrating the model’s advantages compared to other methods Steganography scheme Image size Message length (bit) Adec(%) PSNR (dB) SSIM Steganalysis resistance (%) SAE-JS 128 × 128 30 87.84 29.807 0.996 47.71 IGA 128 × 128 30 82.90 20.415 0.807 93.13 PIMoG 128 × 128 30 70.00 32.210 0.941 51.25 TSDL-BW 128 × 128 30 80.00–95.00 11.969 0.413 91.25 HiDDeN 128 × 128 30 91.00 33.230 0.945 51.67 MAE-InpaintSteg 224 × 224 30 96.67 27.488 0.852 49.79 Page 16 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 aspects: first, the unavoidable quality loss from informa- tion completion in damaged regions during restoration, an inherent feature of all image inpainting tasks; second, subtle perturbations caused by steganographic embed- ding. Thus, this indicator level does not reflect insuf- ficient performance of the steganographic algorithm but rather highlights the unique advantage of MAE- InpaintSteg: on the special carrier of damaged images, it simultaneously realizes restoration functionality and steganographic encryption. Even with necessary qual- ity loss introduced during restoration, it still ensures the practicality of stego-inpainted images while maintaining the security of steganographic information. In the steganalysis resistance experiment, the main- stream steganalysis network SRNet (Boroumand et al. 2019) was adopted for evaluation. In accordance with the standard protocol of Boroumand et al. (2019), the model was trained from randomly initialized weights. During the experiment, cover/stego datasets were constructed for five steganographic methods (MAE-InpaintSteg, SAE-JS, HiDDeN, IGA, and TSDL-BW) respectively. Each dataset consisted of paired stego-images (gener- ated by the corresponding method) and original images, which were split into training and test sets at an 8:2 ratio to ensure no overlap between training and test samples. In the training process, binary classification labels 0 and 1 were assigned to normal images and stego-images, respectively. The batch size was set to 32, the total num- ber of training epochs was 200, the Adam optimizer was used (with a learning rate of 1 × ­10−3), and the cross- entropy loss was adopted as the loss function. After train- ing on the training set, the model performed prediction on the independent test set, and the verification results showed stable convergence—this confirms the reliability of the training process. The evaluation results indicate that MAE-InpaintSteg achieved a detection accuracy of 49.79% under SRNet. Since SRNet, as a binary classifier, exploits deep residual structures to capture subtle statistical traces left by steg- anographic operations, a detection accuracy closer to 50% implies stronger imperceptibility and higher secu- rity. MAE-InpaintSteg’s performance is comparable to SAE-JS (47.71%) and HiDDeN (51.67%), both of which fall within the ideal covert range, and it significantly out- performs IGA (93.13%) and TSDL-BW (91.25%), whose artifacts are more easily detected by SRNet. These find- ings demonstrate that the embedding strategy of MAE- InpaintSteg provides superior imperceptibility and robustness against state-of-the-art steganalysis, thereby meeting the core security requirements of practical steg- anographic applications. Ablation experiment In order to evaluate the actual contribution of the steg- anographic point prediction module to the model perfor- mance, an ablation experiment was designed to compare the Adec when the module was included and when it was removed. The experiment used the control vari- able method to keep the encoder, decoder, stego module, and extraction module structure unchanged, and only adjusted whether the Steganographic point prediction module was enabled to test the Adec of the two groups of models on the standard stego dataset. Experimental results (Table 5) demonstrate that the model incorporating the steganographic point predic- tion module achieves a Adec of 96.67%, significantly out- performing the control group (52.8%) where points were randomly selected. This 44.87% improvement quanti- tatively validates the module’s critical role in enhancing steganographic robustness by identifying secure embed- ding regions with minimal feature fluctuations during inpainting. The data further confirms that our feature- fluctuation-based screening strategy effectively recon- ciles the dual objectives of concealment and extraction reliability, providing empirical justification for the mod- ule’s design. Conclusion This paper proposes MAE-InpaintSteg, a novel Inpainting- Steganography framework for damaged images, which employs variable-ratio random masking to simulate diverse image degradation scenarios. Operating in the feature domain, the model simultaneously achieves high-fidelity image inpainting and secure data embedding while pre- serving visual richness. Experimental results show that MAE-InpaintSteg achieves a high Adec , which does not sig- nificantly decrease with changes in offset amplitude, fluctu- ation amplitude, or steganographic capacity. This highlights the potential of feature-domain steganography in applica- tion scenarios requiring information security and high anti-interference performance. Extraction experiments designed under JPEG compression, Gaussian noise, Gauss- ian blur, and their combined interference conditions indi- cate that MAE-InpaintSteg maintains a high Adec in various common image perturbation scenarios, fully demonstrating Table 5 The impact of the presence or absence of a steganographic point prediction module on the Adec Adec(%) With steganographic point prediction module 96.67 Without steganographic point prediction module 52.8 Page 17 of 18 Zhang et al. Cybersecurity (2026) 9:28 excellent robustness. Meanwhile, the addition of stegano- graphic functionality does not significantly negatively affect image inpainting; some indicators even show improve- ment. Compared with classical steganographic schemes in recent years, the Adec of this scheme significantly surpasses that of the comparative schemes, strongly confirming its superior performance in information extraction accuracy. By conducting steganalysis experiment to verify its anti- steganographic detection capability, it is further proved that the information embedding method has strong secu- rity and can meet the core requirement of steganographic concealment in practical scenarios. Ablation experiments further verify the key role of the steganographic point pre- diction module in improving steganographic robustness by screening safe embedding regions, as well as the effective- ness of the point screening strategy. However, the model currently relies on manually set feature fluctuation thresh- olds for steganographic point screening, lacking a dynamic adjustment mechanism. Future research can introduce adaptive threshold selection mechanisms based on content distribution or context awareness to enhance the model’s versatility in different image scenarios and the flexibility of embedding strategies. Additionally, efforts will be made to expand its application in complex practical scenarios and explore the possibility of integration with other cutting- edge technologies. 42. **PhysioBridge: Physiology-Constrained Self-Supervised Foundation Model for Cross-Device ECG–PPG Learning with Conformal Risk Control**
   - DOI: https://doi.org/10.1145/3797491.3797535
   - Verdict: yes
   - Classifier reason: The abstract explicitly describes a self-supervised foundation model with masked modeling. MAE is a canonical masked autoencoder method, and the paper appears to adapt that idea in its coupled masked modeling approach for ECG–PPG pretraining.
   - Matched patterns: none
   - LLM evidence source: abstract_fallback
   - LLM confidence: medium
   - LLM evidence quote: "(ii) coupled masked modeling and cross-modal contrastive alignment to bind ECG and PPG dynamics"
   - Abstract evidence: Wearable and bedside sensors continuously generate electrocardiograms (ECG), photoplethysmograms (PPG), and related physiological waveforms that could enable earlier detection of deterioration and more personalized care. However, current deep learning pipelines in biomedical signal processing often remain task- and device-specific, degrade under domain shift (new hospitals, sensors, skin tones, motion), and provide limited uncertainty information for safety-critical decisions. We propose PhysioBridge, a foundation-model approach that learns a shared representation space for ECG and PPG via self-supervised pretraining and explicit physiology constraints, then supports downstream adaptation with distribution-free risk control. PhysioBridge introduces (i) multi-rate patch tokenization that preserves clinically meaningful morphology across heterogeneous sampling rates; (ii) coupled masked modeling and cross-modal contrastive alignment to bind ECG and PPG dynamics while remaining label-efficient; (iii) differentiable physiology regularizers that enforce heart-rate consistency and pulse-transit-time plausibility; and (iv) calibrated conformal prediction wrappers that provide coverage guarantees for classification and regression under minimal assumptions. We specify a reproducible evaluation protocol on public waveform resources and ECG corpora, emphasizing cross-device generalization, out-of-distribution detection, and fairness-aware reporting. PhysioBridge is positioned as a practical step toward trustworthy AI foundations for healthcare signal intelligence—supporting scalable reuse, transparent risk management, and safer deployment pathways.
   - Full-text evidence: not available in extracted text set
